data.title: Oxford start-up backed by CIA plots British Starlink rival
data.description:
An Oxford start-up backed by the CIA is preparing to build hundreds of satellites a year as it seeks to rival Elon Musk’s Starlink.

OpenCosmos, a British satellite-maker backed by investors including the CIA’s venture capital fund, announced plans on Monday for a new orbital network called ConnectedCosmos – which it said would counter Europe’s “over-reliance on transcontinental mega-constellations”.

Rafel Jorda Siquier, the founder and chief executive, said the start-up would soon have capacity at factories including its main facility in Harwell, near Oxford, to make nearly 200 satellites per year.

“We have the ability to deliver up to 150, soon close to 200, satellites per year,” he said. “In Harwell in particular, we now have capacity for manufacturing on average a satellite every three days.”

Mr Siquier said the start-up could provide a sovereign alternative for European governments and telecoms companies, which until now have been forced to turn to Mr Musk’s Starlink for critical communications.

Starlink, which is made up of thousands of small satellites orbiting the world, has been widely used to support the conflict in Ukraine by providing connectivity to front line forces and the government.

However, officials in Europe have become increasingly wary of relying on Starlink, a division of SpaceX, because of the mercurial nature of Mr Musk.

Mr Siquier said: “It’s very important that we don’t depend on systems where decisions are being taken by a single individual on the other side of the Atlantic.

“We want to be there as a very reliable solution. Of course for the UK and of course for across Europe – and also to countries that want to partner and collaborate without having to depend on the US or China.”

Europe has been eyeing building its own sovereign satellite network, called Iris 2, but it is expected to cost billions of euros and not be ready until 2030.

In January, OpenCosmos secured key spectrum rights for low-orbit satellites from regulators in Liechtenstein that could allow it to compete with Mr Musk’s SpaceX. The licence gives it the right to launch up to 288 satellites for its network by a deadline of 2028.

“It took many by surprise we had reserved this spectrum that is worth billions,” Mr Siquier said.

Mr Siquier announced OpenCosmos’s plans for its new satellite network at Mobile World Congress, a gathering of telecoms industry leaders in Barcelona.

Separately Vodafone struck a deal with Jeff Bezos’s satellite company that will help improve mobile coverage in remote, rural areas.

The telecoms giant will use Amazon Leo’s network of satellites in low Earth orbit to help connect calls, texts and data to mobile masts in areas where it is too difficult or expensive to install fibre cables, which usually underpin networks.

In addition to tackling mobile “not-spots” in rural areas, Vodafone will also use the technology to improve network resilience for emergency calls and critical online services if fibre cables are damaged or affected by flooding.

The partnership will be rolled out across Europe, including the UK, from this year. It will also be deployed in Africa.

OpenCosmos launched the first satellites that will form part of its new network in January in partnership with New Zealand launch business Rocket Lab.

Last week, SpaceIntelReport disclosed that OpenCosmos was seeking to raise up to $200m (£160m) to fund its expansion plans.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: ‘Everyone is getting beaten with a stick’: Tradesmen turn their backs on Britain
data.description:
Vince Towns never planned to down his tools and leave Britain at the age of 51.

Having worked as a tradesman for 24 years, he hoped he would continue going for at least another decade so that he could pass on everything he knew to his two teenage sons.

But tax rises, soaring costs and suffocating bureaucracy have pushed him towards the exit long before his time.

“It’s just not worth me doing it,” he says. “Why should I bother? More and more is being taken away from me. I’ve lost so much heart in it now that I don’t want to put my body through it any more.”

Towns is making arrangements to move from Nottingham to Spain as soon as his wife obtains her Irish passport. When he gets there, he is considering continuing his work in construction.

And he thinks other tradesmen will soon follow him. “Everyone is getting beaten with a stick. People are earning just enough to live and you’ve seen how many businesses are going under – 2026 will be a big year for builders dropping out,” he says.

As Labour pursues its ambitious target of building 1.5 million homes by 2029, it has a real problem on its hands. To achieve its housebuilding dream, it needs an additional 300,000 construction workers in a range of trades from carpenters and bricklayers to plumbers, plasterers and roofers.

But not only are older workers throwing in the towel, younger workers are also seeking opportunities abroad, put off by punitive government policies, particularly on small businesses.

Since the start of Covid, Britain’s construction workforce has shrunk by 280,000 to just above two million, its lowest level in almost 25 years.

Meanwhile, other countries’ workforces have grown. In the same period, Australia added 183,000 tradesmen and builders, equivalent to a 15pc jump.

As the UK struggles to hold on to its workers, Chancellor Rachel Reeves’s decision to raise taxes for businesses threatens to accelerate the exodus and leave Britain trapped in a construction crisis for generations to come.

‘You get a better life back in Romania’

The foundations of Britain’s current construction industry crisis were laid around more than a decade ago as the world was still recovering from the global financial crisis.

Housebuilding starts had collapsed from around 180,000 before the crash to less than 80,000 by 2009, wiping out companies and triggering hundreds of thousands of job losses. As activity began to pick up again, however, construction firms increasingly turned to skilled migrant workers rather than invest in training up apprentices.

Professor Iain Begg, an expert on Eastern European economics at LSE, says the trend was fuelled by Poland’s entry into the EU in 2004, followed by Romania and Bulgaria in 2007, which opened the door to an influx of cheap labour.

“Employers could suddenly say, ‘I can hire a Pole rather than train a Brit’,” he says.

By 2016, non-UK-born workers made up 14.2pc of the entire sector, having comprised just 7pc in 2007. In London, this figure was as high as 40pc, according to the Office for National Statistics.

However, following the Brexit vote in 2016 and the shock of the pandemic, the inflow of migrants that had defined the previous decade went into reverse. The number of Poles in the UK fell from a peak of just over a million in 2017 to 698,000 four years later.

For many Eastern Europeans working in Britain, the twin effects of Brexit and Covid became an “inflection point”, says Prof Begg. “It motivated quite a lot of Poles in the UK to say, ‘okay, I’ve been here, done that, now is the time for me to move on and do something new’.”

Because the construction industry heavily relied on skilled migrants, it was disproportionately hit by the exodus and exposed a chronic lack of training for UK workers.

Yet even as the dust has settled on Brexit and the pandemic, foreign-born workers are continuing to up sticks as taxes and rising costs take the shine off higher salaries.

Florin Napa, a 27-year-old carpenter who moved to the UK from Romania in 2018, says he is planning on moving back after seeing a growing proportion of his income go to the taxman.

“Before it was fine because I could save money,” says Napa, who shared an apartment with several other foreign workers during his first few years in the UK. “When I arrived I wanted to buy a nice car and do something with my life.

“But I feel like now [as a labourer] if you don’t push yourself, work weekends and longer hours, longer days, it’s very hard to save money. Because the more you earn the more you pay [in tax].”

At the same time, wages and living standards in eastern European and Balkan countries have improved since they joined the EU. In 1990, for example, the GDP per capita in the UK was three times more than Romania; today the gap has closed to just 1.2.

This has convinced many like Napa that they will be better off back home. “You can now earn good money back in my country doing what I’m doing,” he says. “Carpenters can earn €4,000 a month, they have their own house, they don’t pay a mortgage. You’ve got a better life.”

‘Politicians don’t seem to understand how our industry operates’

Much of the blame falls on governments that have for years squeezed small builders and businesses. But recent Labour policies affecting the cost of hiring staff have made conditions particularly tough.

Since coming into office, Reeves has raised both employer National Insurance and the minimum wage. This includes a jump in apprentice wages from £6.40 in 2024 to £8 from April this year.

At the same time rising material costs and shrinking pipelines of work as projects get held up in planning purgatory are squeezing profits to breaking point.

Rico Wojtulewicz, of the National Federation of Builders, says as a result it has become increasingly difficult for small construction businesses to recruit and train young workers.

“The Government sets the environment for business to succeed or fail,” he says. “If the industry is continually seen as a cash cow, and the Government thinks it can keep piling on costs or bureaucracy or tax, then those people are going to struggle to run their businesses.”

While big housing developers can typically ride out such pressures, small businesses, which provide jobs for 80pc of UK apprentices, are buckling under the strain.

There are fewer jobs for tradesmen: in the construction industry vacancies have fallen fallen 32pc in the last three months to 28,000, the largest percentage decline across all sectors.

When those entering a trade cannot see a secure career path for themselves, Wojtulewicz says it is inevitable they will either move abroad or leave the profession altogether. “Politicians don’t seem to understand how our industry operates,” he says. “You can train a million people to do the jobs but if there aren’t a million career opportunities, then you’re wasting people’s time.”

‘You’re going to see a lot of the tradesmen of a certain age disappear’

Faced with diminishing career prospects, many young British labourers have moved to Australia, which has sought to tackle its own skills shortages by offering a A$10,000 visa grant scheme for UK tradesmen.

Between 2022 and 2024, the number of Australian visas issued to British carpenters and joiners more than doubled from 66 to 135, while the number of civil engineering professionals jumped from 72 to 179.

Charles Esterling-Cooper, who runs a relocation agency in Perth, says generous salaries, nice weather and a perception around better opportunities are helping to attract British labourers. “Skilled tradespeople often find jobs quickly, sometimes even before they get here,” he says.

“A lot of the tradespeople I talk to are moving with young families – and they’re coming for the long term.” He adds that only one of his clients has moved back to the UK in the last few years.

At the opposite end of the career ladder, the Making Tax Digital scheme is convincing some older workers to put down their tools early.

Under the new rules, self-employed workers with a gross income of £50,000 will be required to file quarterly tax reports using HMRC-compatible software. Currently they only need to submit one self-assessment form every tax year.

More than a third of the construction workforce is over the age of 50, meaning the sector is already facing a retirement cliff edge. Towns, who is preparing for his move to Spain, says it will pile on administrative costs and push others like him to quit the industry.

“To them, it’s a major hindrance. So you’re going to see a lot of the tradesmen of a certain age disappear and move into different things.”

And for experienced builders who do stay, Towns says there is little incentive to grow their businesses because of “nonsensical” tax thresholds, such as when VAT is triggered at a revenue of £90,000.

“There’s no point in going over that,” he says. “If you’re inefficient then the costs are going to outstrip any profit that you’re going to make. They’ve taken away the incentive to work.”

‘Construction underpins so much success in the country’

The consequences of Britain’s depleted construction industry workforce extend far beyond falling short of bold housebuilding pledges.

Skilled tradesmen are crucial for everything from kitchen extensions to vast public infrastructure projects. Without them, it creates an insurmountable hurdle that can have a damaging knock-on effect for other parts of the economy.

“Construction underpins so much success in the country, particularly growth,” says Wojtulewicz. “It’s not just 1.5 million homes, it’s all the stuff alongside it: the roads, the rail, the commercial, the retail, the amenities – all this stuff actually requires skilled labour.”

If the demand for such work remains, yet the workforce diminishes, it can also present an opportunity for less competent labourers to pick up jobs, warns Jeremy Gray, of trade association the Federation of Master Builders.

“Because you don’t have to have any minimum competence to be a builder, there are a lot of rogues out stealing business,” he says. “We’re getting more reports about a lot of remediation.”

The FMB estimates that over the last five years alone, homeowners have lost more than £14bn to unreliable builders.

Like Wojtulewicz, Gray also believes the country is being held back not just by a lack of new apprentices coming through but a distinct lack of opportunities for those that do.

The long-awaited Planning and Infrastructure Act, which came into law in December, may offer some reprieve for the construction industry.

For builders like Towns, however, it is far too late. After a quarter of a century of hard graft, he’s packing up his toolbox for the last time – with a lingering feeling of regret about how his career has ended.

“If I’d have known how things were going to change, I wouldn’t have done all that work and put my body through the stress,” he says.

“Your natural instinct is to work hard, have a family, educate them, save a bit for them and help them along. That’s the natural progression and it feels like it’s going backwards.”

A spokesman for the Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government said: “This Government inherited a dire shortage of construction workers, and we are determined to deliver more opportunities for young people as we work to boost construction skills.

“We have committed £625m to recruit an additional 60,000 construction workers and £1.5bn to get hundreds of thousands of young people earning or learning, including in construction.

“We’re leaving no stone unturned to build 1.5 million homes and we’re already seeing green shoots of recovery with an 18pc increase in housing starts last year.”

data.title: Government no longer backs business, says Asda chief
data.description:
The Government has become “more and more difficult” to deal with and less supportive of business, the chairman of Asda has said.

Allan Leighton said that decisions made in Westminster were having a bigger impact on companies than when he first ran the supermarket in the 1990s.

“Politics and government have a much more bigger impact on what happens today than they did,” Mr Leighton said.

“You know, I think in that period of time, most of government was pretty business-friendly, and over a period of time that’s got, I think, more and more difficult.”

He added that there were “lots of constraints that businesses have today that are not of their own making”.

Mr Leighton, who is the executive chairman of Britain’s fourth largest supermarket, said when he last led Asda in the 1990s, the Labour Party had gone “out of their way to try to engage with businesses”, but that today the environment was “less helpful”.

It comes ahead of the introduction of a higher minimum wage in April, adding to a series of cost increases and new red tape since the Labour Party came into power.

Rachel Reeves has already increased National Insurance contributions for employers and introduced new recycling levies while Chancellor. Businesses are also bracing for new workers’ rights laws to come into force.

Speaking at the Retail Week x The Grocer conference on Tuesday, Mr Leighton said: “In the end, you have to deal with it, which is why I don’t complain about it.”

The comments came as new industry figures from Worldpanel showed sales are continuing to fall at Asda, down 2.6pc in the 12 weeks to Feb 22 compared to the same period a year earlier.

Asda was once again the only major supermarket to record a decline in sales. Its market share now stands at 11.5pc, down from 12.3pc a year earlier.

However, the figures suggested an improvement on the previous month, when sales tumbled 3.7pc year-on-year.

Mr Leighton said the Worldpanel data showed that Asda was starting to “edge forward”, adding: “I’m just looking for signs of progress, and there are signs of progress.

“But I’ve said from the beginning, this is a [turnaround that will take] three to five years. You do not turn these things around in a short period of time.”

Mr Leighton was brought back as the chairman of Asda in late 2024, tasked with reviving the struggling supermarket following its debt-fueled takeover. He has sought to win customers back by pushing through a series of price cuts.

He has yet to appoint a chief executive at the grocer, although on Tuesday he signalled work was underway to identify potential candidates to fill the position.

Asda has been without a permanent chief executive since the abrupt departure of Roger Burnley in August 2021.

Mr Leighton said he was planning to promote somebody internally to the chief executive post, adding: “I’ve always been of the belief that you try to promote from within, and a measure of your success is, can you promote from within?”

He said he had hired a “group of really talented executives”, several of whom could take up the chief executive post. “That would be the most sensible thing to do.”

Asda will report its full year results later this month.

data.title: Germany’s rent controls ‘making housing shortage worse’
data.description:
Rent controls in Germany have worsened the country’s housing shortage and made it harder and more expensive for tenants to move, a leading economist has warned.

Restricting the monthly bill landlords can charge has undermined housebuilding, according to Clemens Fuest, the president of the Ifo Institute, and limited young workers’ ability to take a job in a new area.

Similar ideas have been proposed in Britain, with Sir Sadiq Khan, the Mayor of London, calling for the power to control rents amid the introduction of the Renters’ Rights Act, which comes into force in May.

“Rent control not only leads to less being built, but is also responsible for the rental market becoming increasingly frozen. In other words, fewer and fewer people are moving out of their rental property when it no longer meets their housing needs,” said Mr Fuest.

“The existing regulations protect tenants who have an apartment and no reason to move. This is at the expense of landlords and all prospective tenants looking for a rental apartment.”

Since 2013 rents in existing tenancies have risen by around 18pc amid tight restrictions on how much and how often landlords can raise the bill, the Ifo found. By contrast rates for new tenancies, which face fewer restrictions, are up by around 50pc.

In Berlin, the rent on new tenancies is capped at 10pc more than a local reference rate, though looser rules apply to short-term furnished lets, a segment of the market that has grown as a result.

The result is that the number of people moving dropped sharply between 2010 and 2020, the year for which the latest data is available.

During that decade – which included significant new restrictions on rents in major cities – the number of moves in urban centres fell from more than 85 per 1,000 inhabitants to around 66.

Mr Fuest said this threatened young adults’ job prospects and undermined growth in the wider economy.

“Young people in particular, who have found a job in another city and want to move, are increasingly reporting that they have failed to find accommodation,” he said.

“This is not only frustrating for those affected but also damages overall economic development. Economic growth only works if people can take up new jobs and move to do so.”

The Government in Berlin plans to extend and tighten rules capping rents in an effort to make monthly bills more affordable for tenants.

Mr Fuest urged the Government to focus on building more homes instead.

“The housing shortage in Germany can only be overcome if more housing is built and the existing housing stock is better utilised,” he said.

It is a similar problem to that in Britain, where annual rises in rents peaked at more than 9pc in 2024. In the past 12 months, the average rent increased by 3.5pc, according to the Office for National Statistics.

Despite the Government’s goal to build 1.5 million new properties in this Parliament, construction rates have been in decline.

Housebuilding in England fell to a 12-year low excluding the pandemic in the three months to September, with 30,880 homes completed.

Only 175,290 homes have been completed since the general election, well below the average 300,000 per year needed to meet the Government’s target.

London’s construction sector is particularly struggling to build more homes amid new regulatory requirements in the wake of the Grenfell Tower disaster.

A spokesman for the Mayor said he “has consistently called for the devolution of rent controls, which would enable us to develop a system that works for London and the unique challenges facing the capital”.

data.title: Seven ways to help your children buy their first home
data.description:
Getting on the property ladder has become increasingly difficult over the years. House price growth has long outstripped wages, while rising rents have inhibited tenants’ ability to save, and stricter lending criteria have compounded the problem.

Almost a third of first-time buyers now rely on financial support to buy a home, according to figures from Barclays. And, for most, this means making an often sizeable withdrawal from the Bank of Mum and Dad.

But it can be tricky for parents to work out the best way to approach this and how much they can realistically afford to give.

Here, Telegraph Money explains the main ways to support first-time buyers, including the pros and cons, so you can decide what’s right for your family.

1. Pay into a Lifetime Isa

If your child is aged 18 or over and buying a home is a future aspiration in a few years, encouraging them to open a Lifetime Isa (Lisa) now and offering to contribute to it is a good step.

Anyone aged 18 to 39 can open these accounts and save up to £4,000 a year to put towards their first home or retirement. The big incentive is that the Government adds a 25pc bonus, capped at £1,000 a year.

Richard Dana, of mortgage and savings platform Tembo, said: “If a parent were to support their child from the age of 18 to 28 with the full annual contribution to a Lisa, that would bolster their child’s deposit by £10,000 in bonuses alone. 

“When including compounding interest gains, this accumulates further into a very meaningful sum.”

This could be a good option for those who don’t have a large lump sum sitting waiting for their child, but who can afford to contribute to their child’s savings each tax year.

Lisa funds can be held as cash or invested in stocks and shares, and all interest growth or investment gains are tax-free.

It’s worth noting the Government’s plan to replace the Lifetime Isa in 2028 with an alternative savings product geared towards first-time buyers. It’s understood that anyone who already has a Lisa will be able to continue saving with it after this time.

2. Give a lump sum as a gift

If your child dreams of buying a home soon and you have the means, giving a lump-sum gift is a straightforward way to boost their deposit. A larger deposit can make it easier to qualify for a mortgage and secure more competitive interest rates.

As it’s a gift, the money doesn’t need to be repaid. But this makes it crucial not to risk your own financial security or retirement plans by giving away more than you can afford.

Giving gifts can also have inheritance tax implications.

Tom Evans, of estate agency Purplebricks, explained: “This type of gift is known as a Potentially Exempt Transfer (PET), where if you die within seven years of granting the loan, some or all of it might fall back into your estate for inheritance tax.”

You may be able to reduce the tax risk by using available gift allowances, and it’s essential to keep accurate records of when the money was given.

Mortgage lenders will usually require written confirmation that the funds were a gift and that there is no need to repay them.

3. Provide an interest-free loan

Instead of gifting the money outright, you could provide an interest-free loan. This gives your child similar benefits to a cash gift, but the money must be repaid, limiting the financial impact on you.

From an inheritance tax perspective, this option means the loan remains part of the parents’ estate.

However, Mr Evans warned: “If you opt for the loan route, you need to be able to prove to the HMRC that it was always intended to be repaid. Otherwise, you risk it being reverted to being a gift, which would trigger PET rules.”

The best way to navigate this is to draw up a formal written agreement that outlines the repayment terms and confirms that the loan is interest-free. Both parties must sign it.

Mortgage lenders may be willing to accept this type of arrangement, provided the loan doesn’t need to be repaid immediately to avoid affordability issues.

Mr Evans said: “In practice, this often means your child will not be saddled with monthly repayments, plus the repayment can be deferred until the property is sold or remortgaged.”

4. Remortgage to release funds

Not everyone is fortunate enough to have readily available funds to give to their children. Remortgaging your own property to release cash can be an attractive choice if you have built up significant equity in your home, particularly if mortgage rates are low.

However, taking on additional borrowing later in life can be risky. Your monthly repayments could increase, putting extra strain on your finances, and lenders may restrict the amount you can borrow as you approach retirement.

Alexandra Loydon, of St James’s Place, said: “If you decide to go down this route, you need to be completely comfortable that you have the means by which to repay the mortgage and are happy to absorb the set-up costs.

“You then need to decide whether you’re lending or gifting the funds, or a combination of the two.”

5. Opt for equity release

Those over 55 could consider equity release instead. This enables you to unlock tax-free cash from your property’s value without moving – but it must be used with caution.

The most popular type of equity release is a lifetime mortgage. It’s designed to last your lifetime or until you move into permanent care.

Lifetime mortgages usually come with flexible payment options. This means you can choose to pay interest each month or not pay any interest and have it “rolled up” and added to the loan. When the property is sold, the loan is repaid in full.

The advantage of equity release is that you can help financially support your child while continuing to live in your home. But there are downsides to consider.

Mr Evans said: “Compounding interest can chip away at the value of an estate, making this an option best suited to families who want to provide immediate support over safeguarding future inheritance.”

Because interest builds over time, equity release can significantly reduce the value of what you leave behind. Releasing equity can also affect your entitlement to means-tested benefits. This makes it essential to seek financial advice first.

6. Downsize your home to free up cash

Selling a larger home and moving to a smaller property can seem like a neat way to free up the funds you require without taking on additional borrowing.

Helen Morrissey, of investment platform Hargreaves Lansdown, said: “You may feel like the family home is now too big for you and need something a bit smaller. You may believe you can release enough money to get that smaller home while releasing enough funds to give your loved one money towards a deposit.”

However, it’s important to do your sums. Moving can be expensive, especially when you factor in stamp duty, estate agent fees and solicitor fees.

Ms Morrissey added: “You also need to make sure that you can release enough money to meet your own housing needs, as well as those of your family members.”

7. Sign on to the mortgage

If it’s not possible to stump up a large cash gift or loan, release equity or move home, lenders such as NatWest and Skipton offer mortgage products aimed at parents wanting to help their children buy a property.

Often known as Joint Borrower, Sole Proprietor (JBSP) mortgages, they allow up to four people to be named on the mortgage.

Phil Spencer, TV property expert and founder of property advice website Move iQ, said: “The principle is that the parents and their adult children borrow the money together, thus allowing everyone’s income to be taken into account.

“The child will own the property – that’s the ‘sole proprietor’ bit – but because they’re borrowing alongside their parents, the lender will lend significantly more than they would if only the child’s income were included.”

Unlike a traditional joint mortgage, where all parties’ names appear on the deeds, JBSP mortgages keep ownership separate from support. However, all borrowers on the mortgage are liable if repayments are not made, so this type of mortgage still carries risk.

Mr Dana added: “Lenders will also assess the finances of everyone involved, meaning existing debts, mortgage commitments or approaching retirement can limit eligibility.”

If you are considering a JSBP mortgage, it’s best to consider it as a temporary arrangement. This means that once your child can afford the mortgage on their own, the parent should be removed from it.

data.title: Billionaire’s shipping empire sends tankers into deadly Strait of Hormuz
data.description:
A tycoon’s shipping empire is sending oil tankers into the Strait of Hormuz, despite Iran’s warning that any vessels using the vital trade lane will be sunk.

At least five ships owned by George Prokopiou’s company Dynacom Tankers have travelled through the 30-mile wide narrows at the mouth of the Gulf since the war began, according to maritime analytics specialist Kpler.

Crews have allegedly been ordered to turn off the transponders on the vessels before entering the strait in order to make them harder to hit.

Ship-tracking websites showed them disappearing from view as they approached the waterway before reappearing on the other side after their transponders were reactivated.

The International Transport Workers’ Federation (ITF) condemned the practice and said that ship owners should not be forcing crews to risk their lives.

The high-risk strategy has not been limited to loaded tankers carrying oil from the Gulf – even empty ships have passed through from the east.

One such tanker, the 72,000-metric ton Athina, appears to have crossed through the strait unladen on Saturday night, loaded up in Bahrain and departed two days later.

It was still in the Gulf on Friday, headed east toward the strait, according to the VesselFinder website.

The much larger Pola – which at 150,000 metric tons is one of the largest tankers able to use the Suez canal – was among the other Dynacom ships to make the transit, according to Kpler.

Mr Prokopiou, 79, a controversial figure in the shipping industry, said after the Russian invasion of Ukraine that “sanctions have never worked”.

Ukraine added Dynacom to its list of “international sponsors of war”, although it later withdrew the designation.

The company’s decision to continue to use the Strait of Hormuz risks its ships coming under attack from Iranian missiles after Tehran pledged that any ships in the area would be set “on fire”. Iran has declared the trade lane closed.

At least nine cargo vessels have been hit in the area since the start of the conflict, with three seafarers killed.

However, the rewards for making the journey are significant. Freight rates have more than doubled in a week, with a tanker sailing through the strait to China now earning about $500,000 (£374,000) a day, according to price-reporting agency Argus.

Ana Subasic, a trade risk analyst at Kpler, said the Dynacom tankers and a handful of shadow fleet vessels – which transport Iranian oil in defiance of international sanctions – were the only ones still using the strait.

‘Unique’ insurance arrangements

She said the Greek firm would have had to negotiate “unique” insurance arrangements to restart operations and that other shipping lines were unlikely to follow since most insurers would baulk at such terms.

Ms Subasic said she understood that the US was also continuing to look at ways to provide insurance cover that would get oil flowing out of the Gulf again, but that no solution was close to hand.

She said she expected the waterway to remain closed for at least four more weeks.

Stephen Cotton, the general secretary of the ITF, said seafarers have agreements offering them the right to refuse to sail into the area.

The ITF and major shipping lines agreed to designate the strait a “warlike operations area” on Thursday, enhancing protections for crews. The agreement also includes a 100pc bonus for those staff who do agree to sail through the strait and doubles compensation for death or disability arising from an attack.

Mr Cotton said: “The risk to life for seafarers in the Strait of Hormuz is real and present and seafarers have already been killed.

“It is alarming to hear that companies are choosing to sail and put seafarers’ lives at risk. No worker should have to risk being killed or maimed simply for doing their job.”

Mr Prokopiou’s three shipping companies have a collective fleet of more than 150 vessels, with many more currently under construction.

The tycoon also owns a 106.5-metre “mega-yacht,” the Dream, moored near Athens, and has developed extensive real estate holdings, including the only Four Seasons hotel in Greece and an adjoining marina development.

However, he drives an ageing Mercedes 4x4 and is known to eschew personal bodyguards, unlike the majority of Greece’s ship-owning elite.

Dynacom Tankers was contacted for comment.

data.title: £70k on red tape and remediation: the true cost of home renovation
data.description:
When Sarah Hope embarked on a home improvement project, all she really wanted was a place to hang up the laundry.

Her modest ambition to keep the kitchen clear of drying clothes morphed into a six-figure renovation project. It also gave Sarah, 38, a crash course in the sheer complexity and expense of building in Britain.

Together with her husband Matthew, 42, the couple spent more than £60,000 on just the admin costs. This ranged from a £2,300 report on the history and heritage of their Victorian terrace to £16,000 in fees paid to Greenwich council.

This included the cost of licences for mandatory safety hoarding around the house, skips and a conveyor belt used to remove rubble.

This was on top of the usual cost of applying for planning permission, at £208, plus another £34 for the discharge of planning permission.

“I was like: ‘Are you joking?’” says Sarah. “It felt excessive, and I certainly wasn’t aware before we started that we’d need to do all of these things.

“All we wanted to do was modernise and improve the house to a really high standard, but they made it really hard.”

A spokesman for the council defended the amount of due diligence home improvers must undertake before being allowed to work in the borough. “The importance of good planning cannot be underestimated,” he said.

“Basement extensions are complex builds. Documents such as a basement impact assessment are required to support these types of planning applications.

“But the cost of those documents – including a heritage statement – is for the applicant to negotiate and is completely unrelated to the council.”

The need for a heritage statement particularly irked Sarah. In order to install lightwells into the basement of their house, which is in a conservation area, they had to produce a 43-page document on the property and the local area’s history.

“The thing is, you can’t see the lightwells from the street at all,” she adds. “They are down the side access, and no one can see them.”

Sarah is American and was living in New York when she met Matthew, a British lawyer, in 2015. Two years later, she moved to London and into Matthew’s flat in Maze Hill, south-east London, and the couple married.

They both loved the neighbourhood, close to Greenwich Park and the River Thames, but by 2018, they wanted more space and decided to upsize locally to a house.

In November 2019, they paid £1.08m for their four-bedroom terrace. Although perfectly liveable, the couple was unimpressed by the kitchen.

One wall was blocked by a large chimney breast, which meant all the cabinets were crowded into the other side of the room. By removing the chimney, they could rearrange the kitchen layout and add an island. They also set out to turn the former coal cellar into a basement utility room.

In April 2021, they hired architects to help draw up plans for the house. During this period, they learnt that converting the coal cellar was going to be a complex task, involving digging out substantial amounts of earth and underpinning.

“We started to think that it would only really be worthwhile if we dug out the full width of the house and did something bigger,” says Sarah.

They had their plans redrawn, this time with a full basement level with a utility room, shower room, spare room/gym and a wine cellar.

The immense preparation involved in getting all the necessary reports and licences together meant that work did not actually start on the house until December 2023. By then, Sarah had quit her day job to get the build started and project manage the work.

Early on, the contractor hired to dig out the cellar failed to turn up to work. Further investigation revealed the company had gone bust, still holding a £30,000 deposit from the Hopes.

Matthew, now a partner at a US law firm, was able to put a strong case to the defunct firm’s legal representative, which resulted in 90pc of the money being repaid.

But Sarah is keenly aware that not everyone has a free lawyer on tap and must take a huge risk when paying large deposits out to firms with no guarantee of the quality of their work or even stay solvent long enough to carry it out.

“We were very lucky,” she says.

There were other issues, such as an air conditioning unit being installed incorrectly. They were able to get their money back from the original workman, but to solve the problem, they had to pay a specialist firm to come in and assess it (£1,200) and then get it removed, repaired and reinstalled (£5,600).

In all, they lost £10,000 to vanishing building firms and snagging costs.

Sarah, Matthew and Copper, their fox red labrador, moved back into the house at the end of 2024.

In the final analysis, the project – which increased floor space from 1,400 to just over 2,000sq ft – cost Sarah and Matthew £675,000, including more than £70,000 on red tape and remediation.

The bill was also high because building costs have spiralled since the pandemic. The cost of basics like bricks and plaster has increased by 20pc to 25pc, while the price of steel has doubled, according to Amit Patel of the Royal Institution of Chartered Surveyors. Labour costs have also doubled.

This means the Hopes’ total investment in their home, including the purchase, came to £1.76m. When remortgaging last year, the property was valued at £1.4m, although Sarah believes it would fetch more like £1.6m if it were to be sold.

If she is correct, then it is the money they spent on preparation and resolving builder problems that has pushed them into the red.

“If you add up what we paid for the house and what we spent, yes, we have lost money,” says Sarah. “We intend to stay for at least five years, so it doesn’t really matter, but for a lot of people, it would make it impossible to modernise their homes.

“The UK has some of the oldest housing stock in Europe; our house is almost 150 years old, and it needs work to make liveable family homes. It was just a bit frustrating.”

A potential paper loss aside, Sarah, who now runs her own consultancy firm, is thoroughly enjoying the house. But, given her experience of getting a renovation project over the line, is it something she would ever do again?

After a long pause, the answer is a not-terribly-confident yes.

“I need time, but maybe in five years, if we moved out of London, I would do another,” she says. “I probably would not do a lot of structural work again, but I do enjoy designing a space.”

data.title: Vistry boss exits as pivot to social housing sparks £500m crash
data.description:
One of Britain’s biggest housebuilders has seen around £500m wiped off its value in the wake of the surprise exit of its boss.

Shares in FTSE 250 housebuilder Vistry Group plunged by as much as 24pc in early trading as it announced Greg Fitzgerald’s departure after his radical pivot to affordable housing failed to bear fruit.

The company warned that its profit growth in 2026 is unlikely to meet expectations.

Mr Fitzgerald blamed the disappointing numbers on uncertainty and speculation around last November’s Budget for the decline.

Vistry staked its bets on a so-called partnerships model, which focuses on building homes on behalf of housing associations, local authorities and private rented sector landlords rather than selling them on the open market like many of its rivals.

However, the builder’s partner-funded forward order book dropped by 10pc to £3.7bn in the year ending December 2025, with Vistry citing a slowdown in contracts.

Partner funded-homes, which accounted for nearly three quarters of total completions, dropped by 8pc during the year, as housing associations paused funding and some private rental housing partners refinanced.

Revenue at the housebuilder fell by 4pc to £4.1bn, while total completions dropped by 9pc to 15,658 homes.

Known for his charisma, Mr Fitzgerald took the helm at the company after Bovis Homes took over Galliford Try’s housebuilding business, which he ran, in a £1.1bn deal. Bovis was later rebranded as Vistry. 

After more than 45 years in the property industry, Mr Fitzgerald said it was “the right time” to leave.

His strategy to pivot to building most of its homes for partners in 2023 was well-regarded by many in the industry.

However, it later emerged that build costs at Vistry’s nine projects had been understated by £165m, leading to a string of profit warnings. More than £1bn was wiped off the value of Vistry in 2024.

Anthony Codling, analyst at RBC Capital Markets, said that Mr Fitzgerald’s strategy to shift Vistry into a partnership model was a “bold and logical move given the dire shortage of social and affordable homes”.

“However, such a change is a paradigm shift, and the execution of the strategy has, so far, not lived up to the group’s bold aspirations,” he said.

“The need for social and affordable housing remains acute, and we may look back with hindsight and conclude that Mr Fitzgerald’s move ... was a little early.”

The builder warned that its profit margins will be lower since it will need to increase its use of incentives to tempt more buyers and boost sales. These include helping buyers with deposits and stamp duty contributions.

However, Vistry said it expects to see sales growth and improved cash flow in the second half of the year.

Mr Fitzgerald is stepping down as executive chairman of the housebuilder in May while retaining the chief executive role for up to a year, or until his successor is found.

Elsewhere in the sector, Barratt Redrow said David Thomas, its chief executive, would retire after 11 years at the business. He will be replaced by Dean Banks, currently group chief executive of infrastructure provider Ventia.

data.title: Reeves accused of putting vital South West rail route at risk
data.description:
Rachel Reeves has been accused of putting South West England’s main rail route at risk by limiting maintenance spending while promising billions for Northern projects.

Fears are mounting that a sea cliff could collapse on to the line in Devon, cutting off Plymouth and the whole of Cornwall, after recent storms saw a sinkhole near the railway in Dawlish.

The cliff in question had been earmarked for £400m of earthworks after a 2014 collapse closed the line for eight weeks, costing the local economy £1.2bn.

However, the plan was abandoned after Labour sought to cut costs across the UK’s rail network. Instead, ministers suggested the cliff would be monitored for signs of movement. 

Storm Ingrid, which battered Devon two weeks ago, also saw waves destroy sections of a sea wall protecting the tracks at Dawlish, closing the line for two days.

Rebecca Smith, Tory MP for South West Devon, said the damage had stoked concerns that spending cuts had left the lifeline route exposed to potential disaster.

She said: “With all the tightening of the budget by the Chancellor, she has taken away contingency funding. So if there were something that needed an emergency reaction, the implication is that the money isn’t actually there.

“If you get the right kind of freakish storm, who knows what could happen. The question for me is, should this not be something that is in capital funding on a year-on-year basis?”

After Ms Smith asked in the Commons whether funding could be guaranteed for unplanned resilience work, James Murray, the chief secretary to the Treasury, said that budgets allow ministers to respond to emergencies.

He said: “All individual departments must recognise that unexpected pressures will come along; they need to prepare for them and make sure they have robust plans to respond.”

Ms Smith, a member of the transport select committee, said the Government’s reluctance to fund work on the cliff stood in contrast to its backing for the £45bn Northern Powerhouse Rail project, for which Ms Reeves last month announced £1bn of preparatory funding.

She said: “Why do they want to take a gamble when they are investing billions in other parts of the country? Why do we need a marginally faster rail system in the North when in the Southwest, if this went again, we’d be cut off for, I don’t know how long?”

Ms Smith also raised doubts about the level of monitoring that Network Rail is carrying out on the cliff, which is protected by the railway from the sea but prone to becoming sodden with surface water.

She said: “Are they monitoring it or just draining things? We need answers if they are not prepared to put the investment in to fix it.”

Martin Wrigley, Liberal Democrat MP for Newton Abbot, which includes Dawlish, said that had Storm Ingrid hit when seas were higher, the damage could have been much more severe.

He said the Department for Transport (DfT) should grant Network Rail money to undertake a study into a long-term solution for the cliff.

He said: “Monitoring is no good because the last collapse was a single, catastrophic failure, almost a tectonic shift of part of the cliff which came down on to the railway.”

Mr Wrigley said the damage to the Dawlish sea wall was also concerning, having affected areas on either side of a new section installed after the 2014 collapse.

The DfT said work carried out after the 2014 events held up well in the recent storms, with damage limited to a low wall between the coastal path and the railway. 

It said funding had been provided for a drainage trial and a range of advanced monitoring equipment, including tilt sensors that would provide a quick response if triggered.

Work will continue with Network Rail to assess the investment needed to ensure the line’s resilience and to develop any longer-term solutions.

data.title: US is best place to invest, says boss of British drugs giant
data.description:
The boss of Britain’s largest drug developer has praised the US as the best place to invest amid fears over the waning appeal of the UK.

Dame Emma Walmsley, the outgoing chief executive of GlaxoSmithKline (GSK), said it was delusional to suggest Britain could become a competitor to the US. She lauded America as key to the group’s future growth.

“It’s more than half of GSK’s global turnover today,” she said of the US. “It’s still the leading market in the world in terms of the launches of new drugs and vaccines. It’s still, probably alongside China … the best market in the world to do business development.

“No one should be deluded that the UK is going to be a massive scale market, a domestic market, but it can be an exporter of innovation in life sciences.”

Dame Emma said GSK, which spends £1bn developing new drugs in the UK every year, is preparing to invest $30bn (£23bn) in the US by the end of this decade.

She is set to leave GSK at the end of the year after eight years in charge.

‘Worst country in Europe’ for drug prices

Her comments are likely to fuel fears about the attractiveness of Britain’s pharma sector after several major drugmakers cancelled UK projects worth billions – amid concerns over NHS drug price rules and pressure from Donald Trump, the US president.

AstraZeneca recently announced it was pausing a planned £200m investment in a Cambridge research facility, while Merck scrapped plans for a £1bn research centre in London and laid off 125 scientists.

In September, Eli Lilly, the American manufacturer of weight loss drug Mounjaro, also said that it was reconsidering plans for Gateway Labs, its London-based hub that provides support for biotech startups.

The UK Government has struggled to revive overseas interest in Britain in recent years, with the number of foreign direct investment projects falling 12pc last year to a record low of 1,375, according to official figures.

High energy costs, recent pressures like the increase in employers’ National Insurance contributions and wider geopolitical uncertainty have all been cited as reasons for declining investment in the UK.

One of the key concerns of Britain’s pharmaceuticals industry has been over NHS drug pricing in the UK. 

After postponing its investment, Dave Ricks, the chief executive of Eli Lilly, said the UK was “probably the worst country in Europe” for drug prices.

The Government agreed to a deal with the US at the start of the month to scrap tariffs on UK drug shipments – in a bid to end the row with Britain’s pharmaceutical industry. 

Dame Emma welcomed the deal as “a step in the right direction” for Britain.

The agreement means the UK will pay more for medicines through the NHS, in return for a guarantee that US import taxes on pharmaceuticals made in Britain will remain at zero for three years.

data.title: Killer motorists should be treated like the murderers they are
data.description:
April 25 2007 is etched forever in my mind. It was a beautiful day, and summer was just around the corner. My twin sister had given birth to a baby boy, and my 65-year-old mother, Elizabeth, had come to stay with me in Mortlake, south London. We planned to go to the Chelsea and Westminster Hospital to visit the new baby.

My husband walked my two eldest children, Barnaby and Sapphire – who were six and four at the time – to school and then hopped on a train to work. My mother, my two-year-old daughter, Pollyanna, and I set off to catch the bus to the hospital.

My mother, who lived in rural Shropshire, loved London buses and much preferred them to the train.

We walked, with Pollyanna in her pram, the short distance from our house to the local bus depot. As we approached, Pollyanna climbed out of her buggy and held my mother’s hand.

This was when my life changed irreversibly. I turned around, and all I could see was a monstrous, red Metroline bus approaching at a ferocious speed. Instead of turning into the depot, it drove on to the pavement, aiming straight for us. It happened so fast – almost like a bomb going off.

My legs were trapped under the bus, and I was like the jam in the middle of a horrid sandwich – bus, me, wall. Blood was everywhere. I slipped in and out of consciousness. Out of one eye, I saw my darling, precious, beautiful mother slipping away. She did not survive.

Out of my other eye, I saw my little daughter about 20 ft away. She had been flung through the air, and her right leg was hanging off. I was trapped and could not help her. I shouted for my mother so loudly. I wanted her to answer me, but she did not. She could not.

The crash quickly flooded the news as my husband – The Telegraph’s Christopher Hope – received a call at work to tell him what had happened.

As he made the terrible journey from his office in Victoria to Mortlake, he did not even know if we were alive or not. Four surgeons spent seven hours trying to save Pollyanna’s leg, but they could not, and she had it amputated.

I suffered severe degloving in my leg (where skin and soft tissue is torn from the muscle or bone underneath), among other injuries. However, knowing that I would never see my mother again was the most unbearable wound.

The bus driver who hit us was found guilty of one count of death by dangerous driving and two counts of grievous bodily harm. He was sentenced to four years in prison, which is simply not long enough. Often, only aggravating factors like being under the influence of drink or drugs result in a longer sentence, but it is rare that criminal drivers get more than 12 years behind bars.

On average, four people die every day on UK roads, and many dozens more suffer life-changing injuries. It is no wonder that victims of road crime – like me – feel utterly let down by the UK’s criminal justice system.

Metroline would not speak to me when I rang up their head office. I felt upset, hopeless and angry. So, in February 2014, I set out on a mission – for road crime to be treated as real crime, and for victims and their families to be treated with kindness and compassion in navigating the criminal justice system.

I met Boris Johnson, who was the mayor of London at the time. He said he was very sorry for what had happened to my family. He asked me whether Transport for London (TfL) had apologised, and I said no.

He told me that must change. A few weeks later, my husband and I met with TfL and Metroline. They said they were very sorry. I said that “sorry” must hold meaning, and things began to change from then on.

The Sarah Hope Line was launched in February 2016. It is an incident line that offers confidential support to people involved in, or affected by, life-changing incidents on the TfL network. We have provided to more than 500 families what TfL did not give to us: trauma counselling, assistance with funeral costs and arrangements, and short-term accommodation for family members. Next Monday will mark our 10-year anniversary.

While the line supports families of the bereaved, we still have not yet changed the attitude within government that road crashes are “just one of those things”. This Government should spend much more on policing our roads, so that criminal drivers can be caught. We need to issue immediate fines and driving bans so motorists are deterred from driving dangerously.

Road crime and the damage it causes cost the taxpayer billions of pounds every year – it strains the NHS, criminal justice system and our emergency services. Fatalities, alone, cost the NHS billions every year. That money should be spent on policing our roads and preventing such devastation.

Perpetrators think they can get away with causing death by dangerous driving because sentences do not reflect the severity of the crime. Change must start now. We must no longer be the forgotten victims.

The Sarah Hope Line is on 0343 222 5678, Monday to Friday, 8am-6pm. For more details, go to https://tfl.gov.uk/travel-information/safety/incident-support-service

data.title: Heathrow boss: Airport isn’t crowded... foreigners just walk the wrong way
data.description:
The chief executive of Heathrow has said the airport is not crowded because foreigners just walk the wrong way.

Thomas Woldbye said people complain the airport is too busy when in fact people are just “crashing into each other”.

This is because European travellers are used to walking on the right, while the British walk on the left.

Mr Woldbye told an Aviation Club UK event on Thursday: “One of the reasons people think [Terminal 5] is crowded – it’s not, actually. But yes, people are in the wrong place.

“Problem is that all the British people keep to the left and normally Europeans keep to the right. And they do that in both directions. So we can be crashing into each other and I see that from personal experience.

“We just [need to] make sure that everybody going this way keeps going left, and [those] going this way keep to the right,” he added, gesturing with his hands to illustrate the different directions.

The humorous aside came after the Danish-born airport boss warned earlier this week that Heathrow could lose its coveted spot as Europe’s busiest airport to rivals such as Amsterdam or Paris unless a third runway is approved for construction.

Mr Woldbye said on Tuesday: “We remain Europe’s largest airport but latest figures show we may lose that position in 2026 and we cannot keep driving growth for the UK economy without more capacity.”

When asked what he thought of the Government’s position on the third runway, he said it was broadly supportive. But he added: “We don’t have long-term regulation, which is clear in terms of us building a business case.”

Last year, Heidi Alexander, the Transport Secretary, gave the go-ahead to Heathrow’s preferred plan to build a third runway over the top of the M25 motorway, a scheme likely to generate controversy over its cost and complexity.

The third runway scheme, which will see a 3,500-metre strip built to the north-west of Heathrow’s existing site, is currently estimated to cost around £49bn.

Included in the controversial plans are a scheme to build a 66,000-space car park at a cost of £2.6bn, as revealed by the City AM financial newspaper last summer.

The airport boss was also criticised on Thursday for raising drop-off fees paid by drivers to £7 – and threatening an £80 fine for motorists spending longer than 10 minutes saying goodbye to their loved ones.

He said in response to the criticism: “So when we make profits on all parts of the business, rather than just running the airport, that actually goes to reducing the charge [applied to passengers’ tickets].

“Whenever we make less money ... passengers are actually paying [for] that,” he added.

“We have an incentive to make as much money as we can, on the side, from anything that actually reduces the charge for passengers.”

Heathrow’s charges are regulated by the Government. The airport pays for its infrastructure by imposing a charge on airline passengers’ tickets.

Airlines, including Virgin Atlantic and British Airways, have set up a pressure group called Heathrow Reimagined, which calls for “urgent and fundamental reform” of this funding system.

data.title: Train driver who fell asleep at controls drops attempt to overturn sacking
data.description:
A train driver who fell asleep at the controls has abandoned a trade union-backed attempt to overturn his sacking.

The unnamed Hull Trains driver was dismissed a year ago after experiencing “fatigue” at the controls of the company’s 125mph services on several occasions, The Telegraph previously disclosed.

These came to light when he mentioned them in passing during a work meeting, having previously been helped through similar health-related problems by Hull Trains’ managers.

Trade union Aslef called almost a full year of strikes to try to reverse the sacking but admitted defeat on Friday, with the former driver accepting he would not be reinstated.

The series of ineffectual Aslef strikes led to union membership among Hull Trains’ drivers dropping by more than a third during the past year, The Telegraph understands.

Fewer and fewer drivers were keeping up their Aslef membership, which averaged £373 per year in 2024, even as the union claimed that its members were repeatedly voting in favour of walkouts.

In a letter from last March explaining the driver’s dismissal, Martijn Gilbert, Hull Trains’ managing director, said: “The disclosures made by this driver ... presented us with a safety risk that we could not ignore.”

It continued: “We cannot be confident that they can be trusted to properly report safety matters ... so that we can support them and manage the risk.”

Under Mick Whelan, its previous general secretary, Aslef claimed Hull Trains was spreading “false and inaccurate” information.

Aslef and other unions claimed victory over the Conservative government in 2024 in a long-running row about anti-union laws designed to limit strikes’ ability to halt rail services.

Labour has repealed that law – the Minimum Service Levels Act 2023 – as part of pro-union reforms designed to boost membership numbers within such organisations, which in turn channel some of their members’ subscriptions into the political party’s funds.

Aslef failed to respond to a request for comment.

A spokesman for Hull Trains said: “We are pleased that our customers can continue to travel with confidence when booking with Hull Trains, following the positive outcome of our talks with Aslef.

“The safety of our customers and colleagues has and always will continue to be our number one priority.

“We look forward to welcoming record numbers of customers onboard in 2026 after achieving best long-distance train operator in the rail customer experience survey published in December.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: ‘What does the RSC really need? A better train line to Stratford’
data.description:
I meet Daniel Evans and Tamara Harvey on the morning they announce their biggest coup to date – bringing Game of Thrones to the Stratford main stage this summer. The Mad King, a prequel play to the epic fantasy saga, has been written with the blessing and input of author George RR Martin. The news coincides, oddly, with the dramatic arrest of Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor (“Very Westeros,” Evans quips).

Have the two artistic directors of the Royal Shakespeare Company hit on the holy grail – the next Stranger Things: First Shadow, perhaps or Harry Potter and the Cursed Child? “That thinking is dangerous,” Evans, 52, counters. “The minute you think like that, you can jinx it.

“The reason we’re passionate about this is because it reads like a Shakespeare history play. It concerns leadership, succession, power, internecine fighting. George RR Martin is obsessed with Shakespeare.”

The globally successful HBO series (2011-19) was notorious for its scenes of sex and violence – it carried an 18 certificate in Britain. The prequel centres on the fabled Great Tourney [jousting tournament] at Harrenhal castle, and the tyrant king Aerys II Targaryen. “It’s going to be total theatre,” promises Evans of the production, which will be directed by Dominic Cooke (Hello, Dolly!). “There will be a content advisory, but we’re expecting it to be something the whole family can enjoy.”

The pair also tell me about the next wave of RSC programming for the autumn and winter which is announced today. This includes an all-male production of As You Like It (directed by Evans), Harriet Walter reprising her star turn as Brutus in a 90-minute version of Phyllida Lloyd’s acclaimed all-female Julius Caesar, plus a two-part adaptation of Middlemarch from Nina Raine. The year looks set to end on a swashbuckling high with The Three Musketeers, in which Dumas’s musketeers will be – gasp! – female.

Evans and Harvey, are a seamless and courteous double act. Having both enjoyed success running regional theatres (him: Sheffield and Chichester; her: Theatr Clwyd in Wales), they bonded during the stresses of the pandemic, and applied for one of the biggest jobs in British theatre on a joint ticket in 2022, succeeding Erica Whyman, who was briefly promoted from deputy after 10 years of Gregory Doran at the helm. Their tenure began properly in 2024. It’s full-on stuff, with Harvey, 48, talking about days that run from dawn to midnight, with her family commitments as a mother of two interspersed: “We often wish we could clone ourselves.”

In a departure from longstanding RSC man Doran’s methodical march through the canon, they have modified the approach so that it’s now more about bringing in diverse artistic talents with fresh, exciting ideas about what to stage, and how to stage it. As Harvey explains: “For us, there’s no point in putting on King John just because it hasn’t been done for a few years.”

The new rule is that there is no rule – anything could, conceivably, be an RSC show. While that approach might alarm traditionalists, it has reaped dividends in terms of ticket sales. More than 352,000 people attended productions in Stratford in their first year and The BFG last Christmas was the highest-grossing play in the history of the RSC. Rupert Goold’s Hamlet also played to 90 per cent capacity, while Kenneth Branagh’s imminent return to the RSC for the first time in more than 30 years in The Tempest has already sold out, just as Macbeth, starring Outlander’s Sam Heughan, did. Cyrano de Bergerac, starring Adrian Lester, is transferring to the West End this summer on the back of rave reviews.

A recent hit, Measure for Measure at Stratford, played fast and loose with the text – what do they think is acceptable in terms of tweaking texts many regard as sacrosanct? Evans tells me: “It’s OK to rewrite Shakespeare, but it’s not a free-for-all. Give us your rationale. The rule is that we’re not opposed to it. We’re not purists.” Harvey chips in with thoughts fresh from her rehearsals on Henry V, which she is about to bring to the Stratford main stage, with Alfred Enoch in the lead. “You can pick up any Arden edition and the footnotes read like a food fight between different editors,” she says. In her staging, too, she’s feminising some parts: “I didn’t want it to be a world where we assumed that almost everyone is male other than Katharine and Queen Isabel.”

One could easily spend an hour talking about Henry V (Harvey is, shockingly, the first woman to direct the play in the RSC’s history), but there are other serious matters to discuss.

Earlier this month, Trevor Nunn, the former RSC chief, stated that the RSC needed a London home; its purpose-built abode, the Barbican, is now one of a number of venues taking RSC work. “We are working out what London means for us,” says Harvey.

The more pressing task is transport to Stratford, which can seem Elizabethan in its remoteness. “To be frank,” Evans says, “a direct and better train service from London would be transformational. There used to be a direct train to Stratford [until 2023]. There no longer is. And we suffer because of it. When you look at theatre programmes from the 1960s, they used to have the last train time.” Could transport failure scupper their mission? “It might, because if we can’t get people to Stratford, how on earth are we meant to put on plays?”

That sounds quite existential, and behind the upbeat news there are clearly pressing challenges. Dividends reaped from ticket sales have not been substantial enough to mitigate the financial strain on the organisation, which is looking to reduce its workforce to achieve annual savings of £2.8m. In the autumn, the RSC announced that 420 of its 835 employees were eligible for voluntary redundancy – that consultation period is almost complete. The accounts for 2024-25 highlight the deficit problem: total income was £82m, substantially down on the previous year, with total expenditure running over £84m. Since then, annual repayments of the £20m Culture Recovery Fund loan taken out during Covid have kicked in, too. The drop in income partly reflects previous bumper takings at the box office – the 2022 Matilda film helped boost audiences for the stage show. But a cluster of factors are making life tougher.

“If you talk to most theatre leaders up and down the country, this is the case for them too,” Evans says. “Audiences have less money to spend because of the cost of living crisis. Costs have gone up exponentially. And there has long been a stand-still in Arts Council funding [currently approx £15m], which means a real-terms cut.”

There are no magic wands. Shows such as Les Misérables – which bankrolled the RSC for decades – are, he says, “like gold-dust – you’d be a fool to rely on them and part of the work we’re doing [in cutting back expenditure] is insulating the RSC from that reliance”. The desirability of any given corporate sponsor aside (and the RSC withdrew from a BP sponsorship deal in 2019 following protests), those options have dwindled, too: “There’s less money. Wherever the money comes from, there’s a deeper issue about relying on corporate sponsorship now.”

One option would be to scale back on productions, but that isn’t in their game plan, Harvey argues. “We have to find different ways of working but a sure-fire way to oblivion would be to reduce our artistic ambition and do less because then there’d be no point in our existing. You can cut back or you can be ever more ambitious and find different ways of making work and we’re going that route.”

There have been rumblings of discontent, particularly over plans to close the music department, with its in-house musicians. An online petition – attracting almost 7,000 signatures – has called for a rethink. If enough people complain, will they change their position? Harvey is swift to justify the proposed changes: “It’s about ensuring that music is more fully integrated into how we make work and giving composers the opportunity to work with freelance musicians. Some music will be created electronically, some will be recorded, some of it will be live.”

Upheavals notwithstanding, they’re looking to the future, with Evans (also an actor who left drama school early in order to join the RSC in the 1990s), hinting at repertory ensemble models. “We want to keep surprising audiences,” says Harvey.

As the actors bound back into the spacious Ashcroft room to resume rehearsals for Henry V, she leaves me with a final thought. Whenever the weight of responsibility presses hard, she confides, “I love to remember that this place exists because in the 1870s a local brewer and his son thought there should be a theatre in Stratford. The town and the theatre industry told them that they were mad but they understood that these plays survive because they’re performed. I love to remember that right at the root of this is a local brewer who had a radical idea, and the passion to see it through.”

Priority booking for all newly announced productions opens March 2 at 10am, with public booking opening March 18 at 10am. Priority booking for Game of Thrones: The Mad King opens April 14 with public booking from April 24. Info: rsc.org.uk

data.title: Revolut seeks US banking licence as its wait for UK approval drags on
data.description:
Revolut has launched an application for a US banking licence as it faces ongoing delays to a full authorisation in the UK.

The digital banking and payments giant said it had applied for a US national bank charter, after talks with the Bank of England to end restrictions on its current UK permit entered their 18th month.

A US licence will allow Revolut to offer personal loans and credit cards, as well as giving it direct access to federal deposit insurance and faster nationwide payment systems.

The application in America follows a lengthy wait for full approval in Britain.

Revolut, which last year was valued at $75bn (£56bn), secured a UK provisional banking licence in July 2024 in a process that took three years.

However, it has since been stuck in what is known as the “mobilisation” phase – meaning it operates under a number of banking restrictions, such as limits to its total level of deposit protection.

Revolut has now waited more than 18 months for a full licence to be awarded, far longer than the 12-month window normally set out by the Bank of England’s Prudential Regulation Authority (PRA).

It means the financial technology giant has waited longer than rival Zopa, which was also stuck in an 18-month mobilisation period. Only AlbaCo, a small, Glasgow-based business lender, has faced a longer wait, currently standing at two years.

Revolut is the single largest business to attempt to secure a UK banking licence under the authority of the PRA, with 13 million UK customers and 70 million worldwide.

Its business has also expanded well beyond traditional banking services into mobile phone contracts, cryptocurrency trading and travel insurance.

Revolut has had a European banking licence through its Lithuanian branch since 2018.

Nikolay Storonsky, Revolut’s founder, who is worth an estimated $18bn, said the US banking licence application marked a “major milestone” in its North American expansion plans.

He added: “The United States is a key pillar of our global growth strategy. This charter will give us the direct control needed to innovate faster and deliver the Revolut experience to millions more Americans as we move toward our goal of 100 million customers.”

The US push comes just over a month after it emerged that Mr Storonsky had shifted his residency back to the UK after briefly appearing to have relocated to Dubai.

The Telegraph reported in January that Mr Storonsky’s family office had lodged new documents with Companies House stating that he is a resident of the UK.

An earlier filing published in October last year said that the founder was based in the UAE, blind-siding banking regulators.

Mr Storonsky, who has previously criticised British red tape over the lengthy delays to Revolut’s banking application, has consistently remained a resident of the UK in company’s own corporate filings.

Revolut had previously explored buying up a US bank as a shortcut to a full banking charter, but later opted to pursue its own application instead. It also submitted a draft US banking application at state level in 2021, but it did not progress the application.

The digital bank added that it had appointed Cetin Duransoy, a former Capital One and Visa executive, as its US chief executive.

Sid Jajodia, its current US boss, will remain global chief banking officer.

data.title: Vegan sausage rolls saved from EU ban
data.description:
Vegan sausage rolls in Northern Ireland have been saved from a ban by the European Union.

Brussels was set to forbid the use of meaty terms such as “veggie burger”, nuggets and sausages being used to sell plant-based products on the grounds they were confusing for consumers.

The ruling would have applied in the UK because of its Brexit deal with the bloc.

But MEPs and EU governments thrashed out a deal which means some labels can continue to be used.

However, they did agree upon a blacklist of 31 terms that will be banned from being used to sell vegan and vegetarian food.

They include steak, chicken, rib-eye, liver, pork and foie, forcing some producers to change the names of their products.

The deal paves the way for the bill to apply to the EU and Northern Ireland, which follows hundreds of single market rules to avoid a hard border with Ireland.

The proposed ban on veggie burgers was divisive, with Sir Paul McCartney among those calling on the EU to ditch it.

Critics accused MEPs of being influenced by the meat and agriculture lobby to back an unnecessary ban. Right-wing MEPs said they were supporting farmers.

Céline Imart, the centre-Right lead MEP on the bill, which also prevents laboratory-grown meat being called meat, was an “undeniable success for our livestock farmers.”

It “recognises the value of livestock farmers’ work and protects their products, fruits of unique know-how, against a form of unfair competition”, she said.

There will be a three-year transition period before the labelling ban enters into force.

‘Conservative word police has failed’

British producers selling products in Northern Ireland can avoid it by using the “green lane” in the Irish Sea border. The border between Britain and Northern Ireland was created by the Windsor Framework.

Greggs makes vegan sausage rolls in Britain before they are shipped to 24 bakeries in Northern Ireland, using the lighter touch green lane and the UK trusted trader scheme.

That meant they would be able to continue selling the pastries under their proper name even if producers in Northern Ireland could not.

“Fortunately, the conservative word police has failed to ban the ‘veggie burger.’ Unfortunately, a number of other words still end up on the blacklist,” said Green MEP Anna Strolenberg.

“That’s a shame. Europe should be backing innovative entrepreneurs, not putting new obstacles in their way.”

While the EU ban on some meaty terms will not apply in the UK, British producers will have to observe it to sell in the bloc.

British MPs, including Jeremy Corbyn, wrote to Brussels to oppose the ban, fearing it would impact British supermarkets because the UK and EU markets are closely linked.

After The Telegraph revealed the threat to Northern Irish vegan sausage rolls in October, unionists reacted with fury. Many of them bridle against the Brexit deal because of the fear it makes a united Ireland more likely and puts their place in the UK at risk.

“While the European Parliament debates what a sausage roll can be called, businesses here must brace for yet another layer of regulation that applies in Belfast, but not in Birmingham,” Timothy Gaston, the member of the Northern Ireland Assembly for the Traditional Unionist Voice, said at the time.

It is not the first time sausages have become contentious after Brexit. In 2021, the UK and EU came to the brink of a trade war over a ban on fresh British bangers in Northern Ireland. The row was dubbed “sausage wars”.
