data.title: Daily Mirror-owner suffers biggest loss in a decade as Google traffic slumps
data.description:
The Daily Mirror’s owner has slumped to its biggest loss in more than a decade after a collapse in Google traffic led to a steep drop in digital readership.

Reach, formerly known as Trinity Mirror, fell £165m into the red after warning of a “sharp decline in referral traffic” from the search giant.

The loss, which reversed a £63m profit last year, was driven by a £223m impairment charge following a decline in visitors from Google’s search engine, which cut growth forecasts.

“The unhelpful referrer and macro environments have tempered our view on digital growth over the near term,” said Piers North, Reach’s chief executive.

The loss for the publisher of the Daily Mirror, Express and dozens of regional newspapers is the deepest of the Reach era.

The business was rebranded from Trinity Mirror in 2018 after the purchase of the Express titles from media mogul Richard Desmond. Trinity Mirror last reported a similar-sized loss in 2014, when it fell £160m into the red.

The company has been grappling with Google’s shifting algorithm, which has threatened traffic to free-to-read websites.

Google’s introduction of “AI Overviews”, which give short descriptions of news topics, has left media sites scrambling to attract visitors as more readers turn to the search engine for information or advice.

Data from Ipsos for January showed that Reach had suffered a collapse in page views for some of its biggest local titles, which have suffered from Google’s AI shift.

Page views of Surrey Live tumbled almost 85pc, Leeds Live’s page views fell by 77pc, and Bristol Live dropped just under 70pc.

Despite the falls, Reach hailed its own use of AI, claiming that a tool called “Guten”, which rehashes stories from across its titles, is used for 26pc of its articles.

The publisher has been forced to push into subscriptions for its red top and regional papers, adding premium subscriptions to titles including the Manchester Evening News, Liverpool Echo and WalesOnline.

Reach said it had so far seen about 15,000 subscribers to its premium subscription products and was targeting about 75,000 digital subscribers this year.

Last September, Reach announced 321 job cuts, including 186 editorial roles. At the time, it said it intended to create 135 new jobs, mainly in video production.

Unions have claimed that Reach’s decision to shut most of its printing presses has put more than 250 jobs at risk.

Sharon Graham, the Unite general secretary, said: “It is completely wrong that Reach is intent on making skilled print workers redundant, especially as its financial success was built on their backs.”

Aside from the impairment, Reach confirmed that the closure of two of its printing sites, in Watford, Hertfordshire, and Saltire, Scotland, would save £25m.

In its annual results, Reach reported an 8pc fall in page views and a 0.9pc drop in digital revenues. In the final three months of 2025, its digital revenues dropped by 7.8pc.

data.title: Top BBC executive quits as broadcaster races to cut costs
data.description:
The BBC’s chief operating officer has resigned, becoming the third senior executive departure at the broadcaster in four months, as the corporation pushes ahead with a cost-cutting drive.

Leigh Tavaziva, who spent five years at the BBC, told staff on Tuesday that she would be stepping down in September to “pursue new opportunities”.

In a resignation email, Ms Tavaziva said she would be leaving amid a restructuring of the BBC’s product and technology divisions, with the company set to combine teams from its public service and its BBC Studios commercial arm.

The reorganisation comes after Tim Davie, the BBC’s outgoing director general, announced plans for further cost savings of 10pc in the next three years.

A BBC spokesman said: “We are not announcing any cost saving measures as part of the announcement now.”

However, Ms Tavaziva said: “I do recognise the uncertainty many of you are facing with the changes we are announcing today.”

She added that combining the teams would help the BBC “focus our investment, make faster decisions, adopt shared tools and standards and innovate quickly”. It would also remove “duplication”, she said.

Ms Tavaziva joined the BBC from Centrica in 2021, taking on responsibility for operations and the technology behind products such as iPlayer. She received a salary of up to £470,000 in 2025.

The exit of its operations chief is the third senior departure from the BBC’s top team in a little more than four months.

Mr Davie and Deborah Turness, the broadcaster’s news chief, announced in November they would be stepping down after The Telegraph revealed instances of bias within the BBC.

An investigation by the newspaper found that a Panorama documentary had misled viewers with edits to a speech by Donald Trump, the US president.

Mr Davie is expected to depart in April, with the BBC hunting for a new director general who will be responsible for handling its negotiations on a new funding settlement with the Government.

The broadcaster’s current charter expires in 2027. Labour has said it is considering other ways to raise revenue, including part-funding by advertising or subscriptions.

Mr Davie previously pushed back on calls for subscriptions or adverts, arguing they would undermine the BBC’s public service mission.

The BBC has also been grappling with growing pressure from technology giants and an exodus of young viewers who have abandoned traditional television in favour of YouTube videos or TikTok.

In an exclusive interview with The Telegraph this week, Greg Peters, the co-chief executive of Netflix, said he would be “eager” to host more BBC programmes to help them reach audiences that had drifted away.

Netflix has been seeking wider deals with traditional TV channels, including signing up France’s TF1 and integrating it into its app menu.

The BBC signed a deal with YouTube in January to produce original shows for the app for the first time.

However, speaking this week, Mr Peters warned: “When you think about what a BBC production or a Netflix production costs, it’s not really supported by the YouTube model.”

data.title: Britain in danger of outsourcing its intellect, warns AI pioneer
data.description:
The artificial intelligence revolution threatens to erode Britain’s sovereignty, one of the country’s most experienced tech entrepreneurs has warned.

Ken Mulvany, co-founder and executive chairman of Benevolent AI, which has pioneered artificial intelligence in drug discovery, told The Telegraph that the United States and China are increasingly dominant in the crucial field.

He says: “If you look at this from the UK or Europe’s perspective, the risk is that these – China and America – are now the two cognitive powers. They’re the ones that are investing the most into this.

“I do worry about having the nation’s intellect effectively outsourced. It’s not just that, either. It’s the productivity gains.

“If for every hour of cognitive work that you do there’s a machine doing what would take you 50 hours, how do you as a nation compete with that?

“Electricity multiplied the power of muscles and AI can do the same with brains in certain scenarios. But it’s almost like you, as Britain and Europe are outsourcing national IQ to US and Chinese tech companies.

“So you’re left trying to regulate something you don’t own. And I do worry about that. I think of it as cognitive border control.”

Mulvany is better placed than most to sound the alarm. An eerily youthful 57 who betrays his Hawaiian roots with a surf-ready hairdo, he is an entrepreneur who has seen tech boom and bust more than once.

In a world increasingly haunted by visions of an AI apocalypse that would make the dotcom crash look like a minor market setback, Mulvany and Benevolent have already been tested to near-destruction.

The technology excited investors – including the star fund manager Neil Woodford long before his self-inflicted implosion – enough to snag a £1.5bn valuation way back in 2018.

This was long before the likes of Sam Altman of OpenAI and Dario Amodei of Anthropic emerged to seek thrones as the next generation of tech plutocrats.

Benevolent sought to help researchers to quickly design new drugs with computer assistance, using machine learning techniques to scour a treasure trove of science information for possibilities at an unprecedented clip. Expansion into defence and energy markets promised new horizons and riches.

It seemed to work. Benevolent burst onto the public markets long before the current AI frontrunners had attracted the attention of everyday investors, too. With the world still in the grip of the pandemic and having discovered an anti-inflammatory that was proving useful on pandemic wards, the start-up made a splashy debut.

“I wasn’t really part of it at that time,” Mulvany recalls ruefully. Having seemingly set up Benevolent for enormous success, he was already easing into semi-retirement.

As it secured a lucrative listing in Amsterdam, Benevolent won endorsement and investment from some of the pharmaceutical industry’s biggest names. AstraZeneca backed Benevolent as part of a transaction that raised hundreds of millions.

Alas, Mulvany’s big payday and exit did not set the scene for a sustainable company. A starry board and big executive hires steered Benevolent off a cliff.

The company lost almost all its value within two years, prompting Mulvany to abandon his early retirement and mount a boardroom coup to take back control last year. He had become frustrated that Benevolent had shifted from his concept of a technology company to a drug developer in its own right, with all the risk and costs associated with it.

“They brought in some luminaries,” Mulvany recalls. “Some really heavy hitters from pharma. And Benevolent morphed from a tech company to a pharma company. For me as an investor, and for some of my fellow investors, we didn’t have the appetite for that. We were technology investors.

“You had a technology shareholder base and a management and board doing biotech. All of its revenue was from its technology but it was spending a ton of money developing drugs.

“The technology cost was very low relative to drug development. So I became more of an activist shareholder at that point because it wasn’t going the way I wanted it to go and the markets reflected that too. It lost 95pc of its value.”

It’s a tale of hype meeting with financial and scientific reality that now seems to be playing out on an extreme scale as OpenAI and its rivals test the limits of pre-profit funding and investor patience. Altman alone has committed to spending more than $1tn (£750bn).

Having abandoned his retirement to mount his successful coup, Mulvany is now rebuilding Benevolent as an enterprise software company. Its new software and services are focused sharply on helping drug developers, rather than developing drugs. It’s a more commercial proposition, with a closer eye on returns for Benevolent and its big pharma customers.

“We help on the science side of things, reviewing the literature, but also how you make business decisions as a drug developer, how you expand into a particular market, how you design trials and all of that.

“People in the industry are making decisions with a fraction of the information they need.”

These are still the early days of Benevolent’s attempted comeback and Mulvany’s unexpected return from early retirement, which he had intended to spend skiing. His hopes of using the company’s targeted AI technology beyond drug discovery in fields such as defence and energy have been revived.

Benevolent, without revenue, now has the cash and investor support to pursue its new business into 2027, according to Mulvany.

Despite the mounting fears of an AI bust, he remains a believer in the technology. After a deep cull of staff, Benevolent’s coders are 50 times faster with the help of computer intelligence, for instance. But Mulvany, now an AI survivor, admits a shake-out lies ahead for those with grandiose visions.

“I saw this in the dotcom. I was there right at the bubble. But whether it’s railroads, or electricity or the internet there has always been over-investment and hype.

“So there’s going to be losers in this but the infrastructure is being put in place. So yeah, you’re seeing an AI bubble, but I’ve seen it before.”

data.title: Another shadow bank hit by sell-off as AI fears spread
data.description:
The world’s biggest “shadow bank” fund has been hit by a record sell-off amid fears about the impact of AI on companies backed by private loans.

Blackstone’s private credit fund, known as Bcred, saw investors pull $3.8bn (£2.9bn) in the last quarter – equivalent to 7.9pc of its total shares.

The fund typically limits withdrawals to 5pc per quarter, meaning that Blackstone – a “shadow bank” that acts like a lender but is unregulated – has been forced to change its rules to meet the requests. Bcred is the world’s largest private credit fund with $82bn of assets under management.

The move highlights investors’ heightened fears about the impact of AI on the tech companies the fund has lent to.

Software companies have been heavily backed by private credit funds in recent years, but the rise of Anthropic’s Claude and ChatGPT has raised questions about their future.

That poses questions for private credit lenders, who must ensure the companies they lend to are viable enough to repay the loans.

Shareholders in a fund focused on technology managed by Blue Owl, another major player in private credit, redeemed shares worth about 15pc of its net assets in its most recent quarter.

Another fund managed by Blue Owl opted to halt quarterly redemptions and started selling assets to return capital to investors.

Blackstone said it would meet requests by increasing its redemption limit to 7pc of the fund’s total shares. To make up the rest, the private equity firm, alongside employees, will offset the remaining 0.9pc.

Lloyd Blankfein, who led Goldman Sachs through the 2008 financial crash, said this week that he saw parallels between the boom in private credit and the global financial crisis.

“I wonder where there’s hidden secret leverage,” he said.

“Now everyone says, ‘Oh, the world’s not leveraged.’ That’s exactly what everybody said in the mortgage crisis until you suddenly discover that there was a lot of mortgage risk in Iceland.”

“It sort of smells like that kind of moment again,” Mr Blankfein added. “I don’t feel the storm, but the horses are starting to whinny in the corral.”

His comments echoed similar warnings from Jamie Dimon, the chief executive of JPMorgan, who was among the first to note the growing number of so-called “cockroaches” in private credit.

“Unfortunately, we did see this in ’05, ’06 and ’07 – almost the same thing – the rising tide was lifting all boats. Everyone was making a lot of money,” Mr Dimon said last week. “I see a couple of people doing some dumb things.”

Britain’s private market has grown by 56pc since 2015 to $185bn and is the second largest after the US, according to a recent report by the House of Lords.

The sector’s rapid growth has raised concerns about its stability and the potential impact of a crash on the wider financial system.

In the UK, the Bank of England plans to conduct the world’s first “stress test” of the shadow banking sector to see how it would cope with a global shock next year.

data.title: Where to put £100k savings right now for the highest return
data.description:
Have you got a savings strategy to maximise your returns? We want to hear from you. Email money@telegraph.co.uk

Savings rates are very much on the decline. To protect your money from the damaging effects of inflation, it’s worth putting some effort into finding the accounts paying the best returns possible.

But with so many accounts to choose from, all with differing rates and conditions, finding the best home for your cash can be difficult. Everyone’s circumstances are different: some can afford to lock cash away for years, while others only feel comfortable if they can make instant withdrawals in case of emergencies.

Telegraph Money asked Katie Horne of Flagstone, a cash deposit platform, to give her views on three scenarios that represent common questions we get asked by readers.

For simplicity’s sake, we have not considered tax. In reality, tax on savings interest would be a problem for any money held outside an Isa.

Once you earn interest over £1,000 a year (for most basic-rate taxpayers) or £500 (for higher-rate payers), returns are subject to income tax, due to the personal savings allowance.

Scenario 1

A retired couple in their 70s with £100,000 in cash. They are happy to lock up £80,000 for more than a year, but need access to the rest – though not necessarily daily

Katie says: “The retired couple could take advantage of some longer-term fixed accounts, specifically the Chetwood Bank 12-month Fixed Term Deposit, which pays 4.22pc.

“They can place £80,000 into this product, earning themselves interest of £3,424 a year. If they are comfortable securing a longer fixed term, and concerned that rates might be significantly lower in 12 months’ time, another option is Chetwood Bank’s 5 Year Fixed Term Deposit, paying 411pc AER, which would generate £3,342 a year in interest.

“For the remaining £20,000, they could use their Isa allowance – LHV Bank Instant Access Cash Isa pays 3.6pc AER, generating interest of £732 a year.

“However, if they have used their Isa allowance, they could choose the Paragon Bank instant access account, paying 3.8pc AER, earning £794 in interest.

“All of these accounts would be covered by the Financial Services Compensation Scheme. This balanced portfolio would meet their near-term liquidity requirements and generate a blended interest rate of 4.07pc to 4.22pc, equivalent to between £4,074 and £4,219 a year.”

Scenario 2

A working woman in her 50s with £100,000 in cash. She needs ready access to £80,000 but can lock up £20,000 for up to three years

Katie says: “The lady could opt to place £80,000 into an instant-access account from Paragon Bank, which pays 3.86pc AER.

“This account allows unlimited withdrawals without any restrictions and would earn her £3,176 in interest a year.

“If she is willing to restrict access somewhat, she could achieve a slightly higher rate with the Gatehouse Bank three-month fixed term product, currently paying 4.06pc AER, earning £3,248 in interest a year.

“For the remaining £20,000, she could secure the Chetwood Bank 5 Year Fixed Term Deposit, currently paying 4.11pc, earning £836 a year in interest. Alternatively, she could lock in for a shorter period and secure a slightly higher rate of 4.22pc with the Chetwood Bank UK 12 Month Fixed Term Deposit, earning £856 a year.

“Her selection here would probably be driven by her view on the likelihood of significantly lower savings rates in 12-18 months.

“This balanced portfolio would meet her near-term liquidity requirements and generate a blended interest rate of 4.01pc to 4.1pc, or £4,012 to £4,104, again covered by the FSCS.”

Scenario 3

A working man in his 40s. Has no savings, but can put aside £1,500 a month for the next five years. Doesn’t need access for five years

Katie says: “A number of big banks offer good savings rates with specific requirements. For example, Santander Edge Saver offers 6pc AER with a minimum deposit of £1 and a maximum deposit of £4,000, or £233 a year in interest. Once his savings have grown, he can reassess and diversify his investments.

“We would also suggest that he look into cash Isas to take advantage of the tax-free status. You can currently invest up to £20,000 in Isas each year without paying tax on the interest you earn.

“The best rates over the past 12 months have been around the 4.5pc mark. Currently, Trading 212 offers a cash Isa at 4.54pc with no minimum required. If he puts in £300 a month, he’ll get a return of £89 a year.”

Scenario 4

A saver in their 60s with £100,000 in cash. They don’t need access to all of his money immediately, but want to generate a steady return while keeping some flexibility

Katie says: “For savers who want to strike a balance between flexibility and income, laddering fixed-term deposits can be a really effective strategy. It allows you to earn higher returns on money you don’t need straight away, while keeping some accessible in case of emergencies.

“In this example, £25,000 could be held in an easy access account with Paragon Bank, paying 3.86pc AER, earning £993 in annual interest.

“A further £25,000 could be placed in a six-month fixed term account from Al Rayan Bank, paying 4.15pc AER, earning £1,044 in interest over the year.

“For the next portion, £25,000 could be locked into a 12-month fixed term deposit from Chetwood Bank, paying 4.22pc, which would generate £1,070 in annual interest.

“The final £25,000 could go into a 24-month fixed term deposit from Chetwood Bank, paying 3.92pc AER, earning £980 a year.

“This combination would provide access to a portion of funds at all times, while maintaining steady income from staggered maturities. It also ensures full FSCS protection across four banks, a key advantage of spreading deposits.

“Altogether, this portfolio would achieve an average blended rate of 4.09pc, generating around £4,087 in annual interest, with funds maturing every six to 12 months to allow reinvestment at new market rates.”

*Please note that by submitting your content to us, you are consenting to The Telegraph processing your personal data where required by law. For further details, please see our Privacy Notice.

data.title: All mouth and Dior trousers: Industry-esque bankers become laughing stock of Wall Street
data.description:
To most viewers, the TV series Industry is a cautionary tale about the excesses of high finance, portraying the downward spiral of a colourful cast of devious and backstabbing young bankers.

But for some of Wall Street’s real-life dealmakers, the financial thriller appears to be an aspirational blueprint for their own careers.

That’s the only conclusion to be drawn after a group of Gen Z bankers faced ridicule online after posing for a glossy magazine photoshoot flashing $10,000 (£7,500) Rolex watches and designer suits and ties.

The young bankers from Goldman Sachs, Barclays and PwC posed for photographs wearing expensive watches, Céline suits and Hermès ties while discussing their high-paying jobs for the March issue of Interview magazine.

The article, titled “Meet the Finest Boys in Finance”, has triggered concern in the upper echelons of the firms involved. Goldman Sachs has long prided itself on its restrained and discreet image.

A spokesman said bluntly: “Goldman Sachs media relations did not approve these interviews.”

One of Goldman’s investment bank employees, 24-year-old Columbia graduate Mason Clarke, was photographed stretched out on a plush sofa wearing an Omega watch.

Asked when he wraps up work for the evening, he claimed: “When my Excel freezes at 3am.” He told the magazine that his idea of hell was “mediocrity”.

But asked how many gilets he owns – a fashion staple for any self-respecting Wall Street banker – Mr Clarke was more sheepish. “Unfortunately, only one,” he said.

Clay Nelson, a 25-year-old Goldman banker who specialises in “equity swaps and custom baskets”, said he once spent $3,000 on a Moncler jacket “I definitely don’t need” and described himself as “innately curious”.

The shoot took place at Delmonico’s, a Lower Manhattan steakhouse that offers a caviar service for $225.

Social media storm

The story has since been picked up by the US press and has caused a storm on social media. Posts about the shoot have been viewed more than one million times on X and have drawn ridicule from Wall Street.

On Interview’s Instagram post, social media users left comments including “I’m laughing so hard at this”, “is this a joke” and “I can’t tell if this is satire”.

Many others drew comparisons to the fictional characters in Industry, a joint production between HBO and the BBC that has become a hit and has been commissioned for a fifth and final series.

The Telegraph’s review of season four described the show as “very much planted in 1 or 0.1 per cent land, a fluid global currency exchange of start-ups and selfishness and bros in gilets spouting specious fintech nonsense”.

Stephanie Guild, the chief investment officer at Robinhood Markets, wrote on social media: “This is special.”

However, others appeared to blame Interview magazine, with one writing: “Why did you set them up like this?”

Brian Sozzi, the executive editor of Yahoo Finance, said it was “amazing” that Goldman Sachs’ communications team had allowed the photo-shoot to go ahead.

“Very counter to the well-known non-showiness (at least externally) of the culture at Goldman,” he said.

The interview did not name Goldman as Mr Clarke’s and Mr Nelson’s employer. However, the article did state that Tommy Doherty, 25, was employed at Barclays – a reference that has since been removed.

Mr Doherty, who works as an associate vice-president on the bank’s foreign exchange desk, posed wearing a Rolex that costs around $10,000 and Loro Piana shoes.

He told Interview that he is on the high-end Raya dating app and advised readers to “know your risk tolerance”. He claims to own “five to 10” gilets.

Demarre Johnson, a 23-year-old analyst at PwC, wore Dior trousers and told Interview magazine that he had bought a painting for $1,400 “that is simply a bunch of lines”.

Mr Nelson said he tried to avoid the “corporate and stale” bars near the bank’s offices.

He told Interview magazine that he was on the dating app Hinge and complained about the cost of living pressures in New York. “It feels like you have to tap your card just to leave your apartment,” he said.

Barclays declined to comment. PwC was contacted for comment.

data.title: Khan seeks to lure AI giant blacklisted by Trump to London
data.description:
Sir Sadiq Khan is seeking to exploit Donald Trump’s row with AI giant Anthropic by luring the company to London.

The Mayor has written to Dario Amodei, the company’s boss, offering to discuss how the capital “could provide an even more significant location and platform for the future of Anthropic”.

The letter threatens to reignite tensions with Mr Trump, amid an already strained UK-US relationship due to the Iran war.

On Thursday, Pete Hegseth, US secretary of defence, designated Anthropic a “supply chain risk”, which restricts how other military suppliers can work with the company.

It comes after a Silicon Valley giant refused to allow the Pentagon to use its technology for domestic surveillance and autonomous weapons.

Sir Sadiq’s move is designed to take advantage of the breakdown in relations between the White House and Anthropic and encourage the company to move staff to the UK.

In his letter to Mr Amodei, Sir Sadiq praises Anthropic’s position and criticises the US department of defence.

“It is extremely concerning to see this kind of behaviour, which is a clear attempt to intimidate and punish Anthropic for refusing to remove ethical safeguards. I applaud your steadfastness in the face of such pressure,” he wrote.

“Anthropic’s approach to safety and governance reflects the principles we want to see embedded in frontier AI development – that innovation must be accompanied by robust safeguards and genuine public trust.”

He added that London would provide a “pro-innovation environment” so that Anthropic’s AI “can flourish”.

“That is why I would welcome the opportunity to talk to you, not only about what AI safeguards you think are needed but also about how London could provide an even more significant location and platform for the future of Anthropic,” the Mayor said.

He offered to meet Mr Amodei the next time the tech boss is in Britain.

Sir Sadiq’s offer is the latest sign of tension between the Mayor and Mr Trump. The president has branded Sir Sadiq “horrible” and claimed without evidence that he had allowed London to become a “different place” due to mass migration.

Anthropic opened a London office in 2023. OpenAI, a rival to Anthropic, last week said it was making London its biggest base outside of its San Francisco headquarters.

Mr Trump declared Anthropic a “radical left, woke company” and threatened criminal action against it.

On Thursday, Mr Amodei apologised for saying that Mr Trump did not like Anthropic because it had refused to lavish “dictator-style praise” on the president.

In an email to staff last Friday, shortly after talks between Anthropic and the Pentagon broke down, Mr Amodei suggested his treatment by the administration stemmed from not donating money to the president.

“It was a difficult day for the company, and I apologise for the tone of the post,” Mr Amodei said. “It does not reflect my careful or considered views.”

The White House was contacted for comment.

data.title: All mouth and Dior trousers: Industry-esque bankers become laughing stock of Wall Street
data.description:
To most viewers, the TV series Industry is a cautionary tale about the excesses of high finance, portraying the downward spiral of a colourful cast of devious and backstabbing young bankers.

But for some of Wall Street’s real-life dealmakers, the financial thriller appears to be an aspirational blueprint for their own careers.

That’s the only conclusion to be drawn after a group of Gen Z bankers faced ridicule online after posing for a glossy magazine photoshoot flashing $10,000 (£7,500) Rolex watches and designer suits and ties.

The young bankers from Goldman Sachs, Barclays and PwC posed for photographs wearing expensive watches, Céline suits and Hermès ties while discussing their high-paying jobs for the March issue of Interview magazine.

The article, titled “Meet the Finest Boys in Finance”, has triggered concern in the upper echelons of the firms involved. Goldman Sachs has long prided itself on its restrained and discreet image.

A spokesman said bluntly: “Goldman Sachs media relations did not approve these interviews.”

One of Goldman’s investment bank employees, 24-year-old Columbia graduate Mason Clarke, was photographed stretched out on a plush sofa wearing an Omega watch.

Asked when he wraps up work for the evening, he claimed: “When my Excel freezes at 3am.” He told the magazine that his idea of hell was “mediocrity”.

But asked how many gilets he owns – a fashion staple for any self-respecting Wall Street banker – Mr Clarke was more sheepish. “Unfortunately, only one,” he said.

Clay Nelson, a 25-year-old Goldman banker who specialises in “equity swaps and custom baskets”, said he once spent $3,000 on a Moncler jacket “I definitely don’t need” and described himself as “innately curious”.

The shoot took place at Delmonico’s, a Lower Manhattan steakhouse that offers a caviar service for $225.

Social media storm

The story has since been picked up by the US press and has caused a storm on social media. Posts about the shoot have been viewed more than one million times on X and have drawn ridicule from Wall Street.

On Interview’s Instagram post, social media users left comments including “I’m laughing so hard at this”, “is this a joke” and “I can’t tell if this is satire”.

Many others drew comparisons to the fictional characters in Industry, a joint production between HBO and the BBC that has become a hit and has been commissioned for a fifth and final series.

The Telegraph’s review of season four described the show as “very much planted in 1 or 0.1 per cent land, a fluid global currency exchange of start-ups and selfishness and bros in gilets spouting specious fintech nonsense”.

Stephanie Guild, the chief investment officer at Robinhood Markets, wrote on social media: “This is special.”

However, others appeared to blame Interview magazine, with one writing: “Why did you set them up like this?”

Brian Sozzi, the executive editor of Yahoo Finance, said it was “amazing” that Goldman Sachs’ communications team had allowed the photo-shoot to go ahead.

“Very counter to the well-known non-showiness (at least externally) of the culture at Goldman,” he said.

The interview did not name Goldman as Mr Clarke’s and Mr Nelson’s employer. However, the article did state that Tommy Doherty, 25, was employed at Barclays – a reference that has since been removed.

Mr Doherty, who works as an associate vice-president on the bank’s foreign exchange desk, posed wearing a Rolex that costs around $10,000 and Loro Piana shoes.

He told Interview that he is on the high-end Raya dating app and advised readers to “know your risk tolerance”. He claims to own “five to 10” gilets.

Demarre Johnson, a 23-year-old analyst at PwC, wore Dior trousers and told Interview magazine that he had bought a painting for $1,400 “that is simply a bunch of lines”.

Mr Nelson said he tried to avoid the “corporate and stale” bars near the bank’s offices.

He told Interview magazine that he was on the dating app Hinge and complained about the cost of living pressures in New York. “It feels like you have to tap your card just to leave your apartment,” he said.

Barclays declined to comment. PwC was contacted for comment.

data.title: The Traitors producer agrees £6bn merger with French rival
data.description:
The British production group behind The Traitors has struck an $8bn (£6bn) deal to merge with French rival Banijay Entertainment.

All3Media confirmed the tie-up with the studio behind Peaky Blinders on Tuesday, in a deal to create the world’s largest independent TV maker.

The combined group, called Banijay, will be responsible for producing more than 170 shows and events across Europe.

This will include shows such as MasterChef, Fleabag, Gogglebox and Call the Midwife.

RedBird IMI, which owns All3Media, and Banijay will be equal partners in the new joint venture.

However, the French business will receive €796mn in compensation owing to the fact that it is the larger company.

The deal will create a European media giant with distribution spanning nearly 250 countries.

This will put it in direct competition with Paramount, the American TV giant controlled by the billionaire Ellison family, as it prepares to take over Warner Bros in an $111bn deal.

Both companies had unsuccessfully explored a merger in 2023 when All3Media was put up for sale by its former joint owners, Warner Bros, Discovery and Liberty Global.

However, the deal was revived in the wake of All3Media being sold in 2024 to RedBird IMI, the Abu Dhabi-backed fund run by Zucker, which in November pulled out of its bid to buy The Telegraph.

All3Media is by far RedBird IMI’s largest asset.

Mr Zucker, who will become the group’s executive chairman, said the deal “brings together Europe, the Americas and Abu Dhabi as investors”.

“It is something that’s exciting to us because it gives us a really global outlook on the media ecosystem going forward,” he told the Financial Times.

The merged business will be led by Marco Bassetti, the Banijay chief executive, while All3Media boss Jane Turton will be named deputy.

The merger is expected to result in job cuts.

Speaking to the BBC on Wednesday, Mr Zucker said: “When there’s any combination like this there are duplicative roles and so we have identified the fact that there will be some cost synergies.

“Those are going to take place in things like real estate and procurement and duplicative roles in corporate function, but what we’re committed to is making sure that there’s no impact on the creative community or any of the production companies that come together as part of this.”

The deal is also likely to raise questions over the future of ITV’s production business.

Both Banijay and All3Media last year explored a £3bn takeover of ITV Studios but a deal did not materialise.

ITV is currently in talks to sell its broadcasting arm to Sky owner Comcast for £1.6bn.

If that deal goes ahead, it will likely need to seek another buyer for its production division.

data.title: How Axel Springer grew from anti-Nazi roots into transatlantic empire
data.description:
As German publishing tycoon Axel Springer laid the foundation stone for his eponymous company’s headquarters in occupied Berlin in 1959, his ideology was also committed to the earth.

A deed accompanying the stone, signed by Berlin mayor Willy Brandt, read: “For the free word in a free and reunified Germany with its capital city Berlin.”

When the Berlin Wall went up two years later, just 12 metres away, the headquarters served as an imposing visual reminder of the principles of freedom underpinning one of Germany’s largest media groups, which was founded in the rubble of Hamburg following the end of the Second World War.

Now, 80 years after it set out, Springer has arrived in Britain after pulling off an audacious £575m swoop to buy The Telegraph.

It is a bold move that has derailed a planned takeover by Lord Rothermere, the owner of the Daily Mail, and brought a major new player to Fleet Street. As Mathias Döpfner, the Axel Springer chief executive who started his pursuit of The Telegraph more than two decades ago, puts it: “Now our dream comes true.”

Established in 1946, Axel Springer’s founding principle was to restore freedom of speech to shell-shocked Germany after years of Nazi propaganda.

Its founder, then 33 years old, set up the publisher in Hamburg alongside his father after receiving a licence from the occupying British forces and a pass to move freely within the British zone.

The publisher’s first title was the Nordwestdeutsche Hefte magazine, followed by the radio programme guide Hör zu! – Listen, in English. It moved into daily newspapers in 1948 with the Hamburger Abendblatt.

In the 1950s, Springer established itself as a major tabloid force akin to Rupert Murdoch’s News UK. It bought Bild, Germany’s best-selling daily, as well as Die Welt.

Springer believed that newspaper coverage should be partisan, blaming the rise of the Nazis in part on the German media’s failure to editorialise. “It is my credo that a newspaper publisher has no right to remain politically indifferent,” he said.

He told Bild’s editors the newspaper must show clear support for the West and insisted that his titles always spell the DDR in inverted commas as the East German state was “neither German, nor democratic, nor a republic”.

The publisher’s ties to the West went even deeper: the CIA invested in Bild in the 1950s, identifying Springer in now declassified documents as a “liberal” who would both “counteract Nazis” and be sympathetic to “American geopolitical interests”.

In 1969, Springer publicly enshrined his ideology into a corporate constitution. This emphasised the publisher’s commitment to freedom and democracy while rejecting extremism and discrimination and upholding the principles of a free market economy.

In a sign of the new post-war order, the constitution also advocated the alliance between Europe and the US. Until very recently it backed a “united Europe”, too.

It also included a clause supporting Israel’s right to exist and opposing all forms of anti-Semitism. Springer requires all its German employees to sign a pledge in support of these principles.

The founder died at the age of 73 in 1985, just weeks before his company floated on the stock exchange.

It was around this time that Döpfner began his ascent.

Beginning his journalism career in 1982 as a music critic, Döpfner worked his way up to become editor of Die Welt in 1998. Four years later, he took over as chief executive of Springer.

In 2020, following the company’s delisting from the stock exchange, Döpfner tightened his control. The chief executive purchased around 4pc of the group’s shares and was given a further 15pc by Freide Springer, the widow of Axel, making him a billionaire overnight.

Under Döpfner, an imposing figure at 6ft 7in, Springer has expanded aggressively beyond Germany’s borders, buying Business Insider, Politico, newsletter publisher Morning Brew and market research firm eMarketer. It also weighed up a takeover of online news site Axios, but a deal never materialised.

As well as online publishers, Döpfner has long sought a piece of the British press. Attempts have included a takeover bid for The Telegraph in 2004, as well as for the Financial Times in 2015.

Springer has taken a more flexible approach to its constitution in its international expansion.

After its takeover of the Politico in 2021, the company said staff at the American news site would not be required to sign the pledge, but added that anyone who disagreed with the principles “should not work for Axel Springer, very clearly”.

Press freedom and high-quality journalism

Alongside its founding principles, Springer has proved itself to be a staunch defender of press freedom amid a troubled Telegraph takeover saga that has dragged on for more than two and a half years.

In 2023, after Abu Dhabi-backed fund RedBird IMI lined up a takeover bid for The Telegraph, Döpfner warned there was a “concrete danger” of autocratic powers gaining control of media assets. Nevertheless, in October he travelled to the United Arab Emirates with Chancellor Friedrich Merz and met Sultan Al-Jaber, the IMI boss behind the failed bid.

Döpfner has said that his company does not do business in countries where the rule of law and freedom of expression don’t apply, directing particular distrust towards China.

RedBird IMI was blocked from taking control of The Telegraph by laws blocking foreign state ownership of British newspapers despite repeated failed efforts to fashion a bid.

A subsequent £500m deal agreed with Lord Rothermere’s DMGT, which was facing scrutiny on media plurality grounds, was never consummated, opening the door to Springer. RedBird IMI has now sold its security over The Telegraph, which can be converted into full ownership.

Claire Enders, the media analyst, describes the outcome as “wonderful”, saying the German publisher was “devoted to high-quality journalism”.

Döpfner said Springer “built his company inspired by the tradition of Fleet Street”, adding that the Telegraph was his “North Star”.

Last year, Springer carved itself up in a €13.5bn (£11.7bn) deal that saw private equity firm KKR take majority control of its classifieds advertising business.

The media outlets were hived off into a separate family-owned company, with Döpfner and Freide Springer together holding 95pc of the shares.

In an interview with The Wall Street Journal in December, Döpfner said he had been scouting out further acquisitions in the US but failed to find a suitable target.

‘Embrace AI or die’

Döpfner, who sits on the boards of Netflix and Warner Music, has been forthcoming about his desire to pursue a digital-only strategy and has advocated heavily for the use of AI in journalism, telling employees that his new mantra is “embrace AI or die”.

An entertainment industry source said he “takes no prisoners”.

On Friday, Döpfner said he saw “massive growth potential” for The Telegraph, promising an investment programme that would include US expansion and a technological transformation underpinned by AI.

As Springer has expanded its influence, the conduct of some of its senior journalists has come under scrutiny.

In 2021, Julian Reichelt, the Bild editor, was ousted following allegations of bullying and tawdry claims about his affairs with younger colleagues. In January, Jan Philipp Burgard, the Welt editor-in-chief, left the company amid an investigation into his behaviour at a work Christmas party.

Döpfner has himself courted controversy after leaked messages revealed disparaging comments about climate change, Muslims and East Germans, while he was embroiled in a row after the Welt am Sonntag published an opinion piece by Elon Musk endorsing the far-Right Alternative for Germany (AfD) party. Springer has denied that Döpfner was responsible for the article.

Springer’s swoop on The Telegraph promises to bring to an end a protracted period of uncertainty for the newspaper group, which has been trapped in limbo for several years.

While the deal may still face scrutiny from regulators on public interest grounds, the German publisher has said it believes its bid is fully compliant with the laws barring foreign state influence.

Döpfner said his ambition is to make The Telegraph the “most read and intellectually inspiring centre-Right media outlet in the English-speaking world”.

He added: “To be the owner of this institution of quality British journalism is a privilege and a duty.”

data.title: AstraZeneca to invest £11bn in China after snubbing Britain
data.description:
AstraZeneca has unveiled plans to invest $15bn (£11bn) in China just months after dropping hundreds of millions of pounds of investment in Britain.

The Cambridge-based company announced the investment, which will be used to expand its medical manufacturing and research capabilities, during Sir Keir Starmer’s visit to Beijing. The Prime Minister hailed the financing as a deal that would help to support British jobs back home.

But the announcement follows a period of frosty relations between the Government and AstraZeneca which has seen the drugmaker threaten to halt investment in Britain in a row over NHS pricing.

The pharmaceutical giant, which is Britain’s biggest listed company, has abandoned plans for a £200m expansion of its Cambridge research site and £450m overhaul of its Liverpool vaccine factory.

The deal may also raise eyebrows given that five of AstraZeneca’s staff were detained by authorities in China in 2024 amid claims of potential illegal activity. The president of its China division was subsequently placed under investigation by local police, with no further details provided.

Astra is the largest drugs company in China, which is the company’s second-biggest market after the US.

Separately, AstraZeneca also announced on Friday that it had struck a deal worth as much as $18.2bn with China’s CSPC Pharmaceuticals.

Under the tie-up, AstraZeneca will have the global rights to sell CSPC’s obesity drugs outside China.

CSPC is currently developing once-monthly weight-loss jabs. The biggest weight-loss treatments currently require people to inject themselves once a week.

In total, if all of CSPC’s eight obesity drugs make it to market, AstraZeneca said the deal could be worth as much as $18.2bn – including future sales of the weight-loss treatments.

Sir Pascal Soriot, the company’s chief executive, is one of a clutch of Western business leaders who are occasionally summoned for meetings with Xi Jinping, the Chinese premier.

Sir Pascal said on Thursday: “Today’s landmark investment of $15bn begins an exciting next chapter for AstraZeneca in China, which has become a critical contributor to scientific innovation, advanced manufacturing and global public health.”

The “landmark” move was recognition of China’s growing scientific capabilities in areas that could help unlock new treatments for cancer, haematological conditions and autoimmune diseases, among others. It will be focused on sites in Bejing, Shanghai, Wuxi, Taizhou and Qingdao.

Sir Keir said: “Unlocking opportunities for British businesses across the globe and delivering for working people back home is always the driving force behind my international engagements.

“AstraZeneca’s expansion and leadership in China will help the British manufacturer continue to grow – supporting thousands of UK jobs.”

Astra has been at loggerheads with the Government over how much the NHS pays for medicines.

The company and its rivals have argued that the way the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (Nice) evaluates “value for money” on medicines is flawed and deprives patients of potential breakthrough treatments.

For instance, Nice previously rejected Enhertu, Astra’s breast cancer drug.

Wes Streeting, the Health Secretary, walked away from talks with drugmakers last summer after failing to strike an agreement.

But in December, ministers announced a new deal with the US that saw Nice agree to pay up to 25pc more for treatments, a move aimed at protecting the UK life sciences industry.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Hope for run-down Boots as billionaire owner’s wife exits
data.description:
The British high street is experiencing its biggest cull in years.

Already reeling from a series of costly tax raids at the hands of Rachel Reeves, tough Christmas trading has proven to be the nail in the coffin for a string of struggling household names, including Claire’s, Russell & Bromley and LK Bennett. More high-profile casualties are expected in the coming weeks.

Amid the continued hollowing out of town centres, one high street stalwart remains: Boots.

Outposts of the lipstick-to-prescriptions business can still be found across the country, but many are in a sad state. After its exit from the stock market in 2007, Boots has spent the last two decades being starved of investment.

With serial dealmaker Stefano Pessina leading the charge, Boots has been allowed to dwindle – first under the ownership of private equity’s original Barbarians at the Gate, KKR, then as a forgotten overseas outpost of American pharmacy giant Walgreens.

Now Boots is in the hands of another private equity house, Sycamore Partners, after the New York-based outfit took Walgreens off the public markets last summer. Employees must fear yet more decline.

However, a shake-up at the very top of the company may signal a revival for one of Britain’s best-loved and most important retailers.

After leading the pharmacy giant for almost a decade, Boots chief executive Ornella Barra is stepping down to become chair. She replaces Pessina, who is also her business partner and husband, and is believed to still own half of the business at the ripe age of 84.

At the same time, managing director Anthony Hemmerdinger is being handed a much bigger role running Boots, including oversight of the No7 beauty brand – long regarded as one of several jewels in the stable.

The management reshuffle is viewed internally, and in the City, as another major step towards setting Boots free from its ownership purgatory and returning it to the UK stock market.

City sources said a float is still the main long-term ambition of the owners, and more likely to occur next year than in 2026.

A former executive said a spin-off of Boots “was always the plan” and the boardroom changes were “a step towards that”. The break-up of Walgreens into five standalone entities – Boots among them – shortly after its £8bn takeover last year was seen as the first real step.

The prospect of Boots being groomed for a spectacular public markets comeback under new leadership will raise hopes of a long overdue makeover of its unloved shops. Shoppers will be hoping that it also means a halt to any further closures.

The sale to Sycamore came after Boots axed 300 stores across the UK, drastically reducing its footprint from 2,200 to around 1,900 branches. Boots also shut 48 opticians in 2020, and had kickstarted a store closure plan the previous year that ended with more than 200 outlets being given the chop.

The status of Boots as more than just your typical bog-standard retailer means its future garners more attention than others.

Armed with Britain’s best-selling skincare brand No 7, the chain believes it can continue to compete in the booming beauty market despite the excitement surrounding the arrival of LVMH-backed French phenomenon Sephora in Britain, as well as other upstarts.

Amid the glamour of expensive lipstick and eyelash-thickening mascara, it is easy to forget that Boots is an integral part of the country’s healthcare system. Without its sprawling network of pharmacists, thousands of people would struggle to get their vital medicines and daily pills.

Its last round of store closures was accompanied by an apologetic statement explaining that the decision was “not taken lightly” and that in all cases there was an alternative store less than three miles away.

Yet that didn’t prevent an outcry in some affected towns, with locals complaining about being forced to endure long queues at other pharmacies, which in some cases had led to delays in getting their prescriptions.

Boots bosses have long rejected the portrayal of a business stuck in retreat as lazy and outdated.

“The Boots Group is in great shape, both financially and operationally, with Boots UK having grown market share for 19 consecutive quarters,” Barra said as she stepped aside.

But that fact masks the two-speed nature of the business. A former executive said there are around 500 Boots shops that are “the powerhouse of the business” – the vast majority of which have already been revamped.

However, the rest are much smaller shops, which are vital to the surrounding communities but cost “between £50,000 and £100,000” to refurbish with no resulting “uplift” in turnover, making it hard to justify the outlay.

“Pharmacies are really difficult to make money in now, because of what the Government pays per prescription,” another ex-director said. In November, the Department of Health and Social Care announced that NHS prescription charges would be frozen for the second year running.

 Clive Black, analyst at Shore Capital, adds: “There has been immense profiling of a small number of new and modernised stores, gaining lots of capital expenditure and senior management attention, to show the newfound vitality of Boots.

“But the reality is that many Boots are drab, under-invested, and needing of an injection of elevated execution that British shoppers will notice across the board.”

Those who know the business well say Boots branches need to become even bigger healthcare hubs, so that they can alleviate pressure on local GPs by providing more of the services that you would usually go to your doctor for.

The chain already does private online doctor consultations, vaccinations and blood pressure checks, as well as specialised treatments for skin and hair loss. However, it is eyeing a larger role in diagnostics with “at-home” testing kits for conditions such as high cholesterol, vitamin deficiencies and food intolerances. These will be offered under the mantra of standing “shoulder to shoulder with the NHS”.

If the move is successful, it would justify spending more capital on its smaller stores, many of which struggle to turn a profit.

The former executive says: “The mantra…was always ‘We’re going to have a great Boots in every major town, and none that we’re ashamed of.’ I think that will continue.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Morrisons to axe 100 more staff in fresh cost-cutting drive
data.description:
Morrisons has unveiled plans to cut up to 100 office staff as the struggling supermarket launches a fresh cost-cutting drive.

The grocer has announced the proposed redundancies as part of a plan to merge two divisions in its Bradford head office that source products for its convenience stores and supermarkets. 

By combining the teams into a single division, Morrisons said it would “remove duplication, simplify our store operations and capture efficiencies”.

As many as 100 jobs have been put at risk from the changes announced on Wednesday.

The threat of further job losses is the latest in a series of moves to slash headcount.

The supermarket scrapped a number of its cafes, in-store butchers and fish counters as well as smaller convenience stores across its estate in March last year, putting 365 workers at risk of redundancy.

It then sold its newspaper delivery division in November, in a move which resulted in 1,700 paperboys being made redundant.

The latest redundancy threat comes after Rami Baitiéh, Morrisons’s chief executive, said earlier this week that the supermarket was under pressure to cut costs after blaming the impact of Labour’s tax raid.

Speaking at the Retail Week X Grocer event, Mr Baitiéh said Morrisons needed to “unlock genuine opportunities that are worth £214m just to pay [for the extra taxes introduced by Rachel Reeves]”.

Mr Baitiéh claimed the tax changes from last year impacted Morrisons, which employs around 96,000 staff, “more than anyone else”.

This included Ms Reeves’s decision to increase National Insurance contributions for employers, alongside new recycling levies.

He added: “We have a lot of room to improve the cost without impacting the customer, without impacting the colleague and the shareholder.”

The Telegraph revealed last month that Morrisons’s latest cost-cutting efforts included putting dozens of pharmacies up for sale.

The supermarket launched a sale process for the sites after concluding that many of its in-store pharmacies were not financially viable.

It followed another year of losses at Morrisons as it battled steep borrowing costs. Morrisons recorded £381m of losses last year, with its £281m debt interest bill having derailed attempts to turn a profit.

The supermarket has been struggling with elevated debt costs since its £10bn takeover by private equity firm Clayton, Dubilier & Rice (CD&R) in 2021.

It has been working to cut its debt bill by selling off some of its supermarkets before leasing them back. Property agents have now been tasked with selling some of the pharmacies on a store-by-store basis, rather than the entire portfolio.

Following the proposed job losses, a spokesman for Morrisons said: “We understand this will be difficult news for them and will be offering them our full support, including helping them to find alternative roles elsewhere in the business wherever we can.

“There is no change to our convenience growth strategy and we continue to see the opportunity for significant expansion in the years ahead.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Morrisons to sell dozens of pharmacies in scramble to cut costs
data.description:
Morrisons has put dozens of pharmacies up for sale in a fresh effort to cut costs.

The private equity-owned supermarket has launched a sale process after concluding that many of its in-store pharmacies are not financially viable.

As a result of the review, property agents have been tasked with selling some of the pharmacies on a store-by-store basis, rather than the entire portfolio.

Those affected are likely to remain open in stores, but under the brand of any eventual buyer.

The move to sell dozens of pharmacies comes after Morrisons’ losses plunged to £381m last year, fuelled by steep borrowing costs.

Rising debt pressures have led bosses to reduce borrowings, which fell to £3.1bn from £3.5bn last summer.

The sale of pharmacies is the latest attempt to strip back the business and focus increasingly on traditional retail.

A separate restructuring last year triggered the closure of four pharmacies, alongside 52 cafés, 17 convenience stores, 35 meat counters and 13 florists.

This led to more than 300 jobs being put at risk of redundancy, as bosses identified areas “where the costs of operations are significantly out of line with usage, volumes, or the value that customers place on them”.

Rami Baitieh, chief executive of Morrisons, said the cuts were a “necessary part of our plans to renew and reinvigorate Morrisons”.

Morrisons is pursuing a turnaround strategy after Mr Baitieh said the business had been going through a “challenging period”.

The retail boss blamed weaker consumer spending among poorer shoppers, many of whom have been affected by price rises.

This has led to Morrisons struggling to boost its share of the grocery market, which is at 8.4pc.

That is up narrowly from 8.5pc this time last year, although it is well below the 9.9pc it achieved in October 2021, when it was bought by private equity firm Clayton Dubilier & Rice in a £10bn deal.

This has prompted speculation that Morrisons could lose its position as Britain’s fifth-largest supermarket to Lidl, which now boasts 7.7pc of the grocery market.

Morrisons is not alone in seeking to sell off pharmacies, as the sector has been battered by rising costs in recent years.

Around 1,400 pharmacies have shut in England since 2017, according to the National Pharmacy Association, leaving many households unable to access medicine in their areas.

This has been blamed on rising wage costs and a shortfall in how much the NHS pays pharmacies.

Boots has also closed hundreds of pharmacies in recent years, while LloydsPharmacy, which had 138 sites, collapsed in 2023.

Rachel Reeves has piled more pressure on pharmacies over the past year by increasing business rates and the minimum wage.

Supermarkets have also come under pressure from rising taxes under Labour, most notably by the Chancellor’s decision to increase employer National Insurance rates.

Speaking last month, Mr Baitieh pleaded with the Government to avoid hitting retailers with any extra costs.

He said: “I would say, please, no more. This is very important for the market. We expect inflation to go up.”

Morrisons declined to comment.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Denmark’s fat jab champion is slimming down the whole economy
data.description:
Novo Nordisk, Denmark’s fat jab pharmaceutical giant, reached an unwelcome milestone last week.

After a dizzying 76pc plunge in its share price over the past 18 months, a torrid week of trading meant it finally gave up the last of its market gains recorded since June 2021 when it released pioneering weight-loss drug Wegovy.

It follows years in which Novo Nordisk has been arguably Denmark’s greatest global success story. Its first-mover success with Wegovy and Ozempic turned it into Europe’s largest company, with the $100bn (£74bn) weight-loss drugs market at its feet.

The tide has since turned. This week, Novo admitted that its newest drugs were showing less promise in trials than those of its American rival Eli Lilly. It has also yielded to Donald Trump’s demand for hefty price cuts in the hitherto lucrative US market.

Novo is now in a metaphorical weight-loss programme of its own. It has shed its long-serving chief executive, cut 11pc of its workforce and lost hordes of foreign shareholders.

This is not a typical tale of corporate tumult. Novo is such a colossus in Denmark that its ructions can shake the country’s whole economy.

Yet Mette Frederiksen, the country’s Left-wing prime minister, chose this moment to take the country to an early election, set for March 24.

Since taking office in 2019, Frederiksen has steered Denmark through the turbulence of the pandemic, the Ukraine war and Trump’s threats to seize Greenland.

She will be hoping that Novo’s travails do not derail her bid for a third straight election victory.

It is something of a gamble. Novo’s fate is inextricably linked to that of the economy and thus to her own political prospects.

The company sold 309bn krone (£36bn) of drugs last year, which is more than 10pc of Denmark’s annual $430bn GDP. It employs one in every 100 Danes.

As Novo’s crisis deepened, Denmark’s economy – which was one of the fastest-growing in Europe – slowed dramatically at the end of last year. The country recorded 2.2pc growth in the third quarter, falling to just 0.2pc in the fourth.

Per Hansen, an economist at bank Nordnet, says: “One of the engines that has been driving the Danish economy over the past two to three years has been Novo Nordisk and the pharma industry.”

Las Olsen, the chief economist at Danske Bank, says that if you remove the pharmaceuticals sector, Denmark’s growth rate would be more like 1pc or 1.5pc.

“Which is fine, of course – but not the stellar growth rate that we have seen in the headline numbers,” he says. “I think we will grow more in line with European economies, perhaps a little less.”

These aren’t views that will cheer up the gloomy Danes.

Measures of Danish consumer confidence have languished in deeply negative territory ever since Russia invaded Ukraine.

“It’s a conundrum,” says Helge Pedersen, the chief economist at Nordea Bank. “House prices are at a record high, there’s almost no unemployment – everything is good but consumers are depressed.”

He says ordinary Danes are still preoccupied with inflation, even though the official rate is just 0.8pc.

“People are still worried about inflation, in particular food prices. We all discuss meat prices, they’re so high, coffee prices, chocolate prices. But that’s perceived inflation, which is higher than reality.”

Frederiksen and her centre-Left coalition partners have already started delving into the public coffers to soothe voters’ disgruntlement.

The Danish government recently cut the tax on electricity bills to almost zero. It also increased spending on childcare and welfare support and removed taxes on coffee, chocolate and sugar.

It has plenty of fiscal firepower. It was running a budget surplus estimated at more than 2pc of GDP in 2025 and its debt-to-GDP ratio was 30pc last year, one third that of Britain.

That allowed Denmark to ramp up defence spending last year by more than $4bn, which – along with higher healthcare expenditure – may prop the economy through any pharma slowdown.

The defence spending is a response to the Russia threat and also to pressure from Trump. He has called for Europe to fund its own defence and he is looking to wrestle at least de facto control over the Danish territory of Greenland.

Hansen says the Greenland issue may also have cast a pall over consumer confidence.

“Danish people are afraid that the new world order that is initiated by Mr Trump is something we have not dealt with since World War Two. That is really what is scaring the Danes,” he says.

Paradoxically, though, it may be helping the tough-talking and experienced Frederiksen. Since Trump’s Greenland manoeuvring became more explicit and tangible, her Social Democratic Party’s previously fading popularity has rebounded.

Geopolitical uncertainty and cost of living pressures are common across Europe. But in Denmark, there could be another more idiosyncratic source of economic anxiety.

Around 10pc of Danes hold shares in Novo. Many more will be exposed to its precipitous share-price decline via their private pensions.

Hansen, though, says Danish investors have been strangely patriotic: as foreigners have fled, Danes have been buying up the stock.

“The number of Danish private clients with us that hold one or more Novo Nordisk shares keeps climbing,” he says. “People still think there is a difference between what Novo Nordisk will achieve longer term and its current valuation in the stock market.”

Denmark’s economists, like its investors, also seem relatively sanguine about their country’s economic champion. Olsen says he was more worried during Novo’s breakneck growth phase than he is now.

“The fear was that it might erode the competitiveness of the rest of the economy. The large amount of hiring at Novo Nordisk could have led to very high base growth, that would then leave the rest of the economy uncompetitive if something was to go wrong,” he says.

“But what we have seen so far is that manufacturing wages growth in Denmark has, if anything, been lower over the last four or five years than we see in competitor countries. There is a competitive economy outside of Novo Nordisk.”

Pedersen agrees that the Danish economy is diversified enough to weather the Novo storm: “But of course you should keep an eye on it because the numbers are still so big on the macro level.

“Politicians do need to be aware that we have two Denmarks – one with and one without the pharmaceutical industry.”

Right now, the politician with the most riding on the Danish pharmaceuticals industry is Frederiksen.

As she called the election, she talked mostly about Greenland and wealth taxes.

Novo Nordisk’s decline might have garnered global attention with the company no longer deemed Denmark’s greatest success story.

Yet for Frederiksen, the hope is that this will simply be background noise as she bids for a third straight election victory – instead of a shot in the arm for her political rivals.

data.title: ITV insists Sky sale is still on as ad revenues fall
data.description:
ITV is racing to sell its television business to Sky for £1.6bn following a fresh drop in advertising revenues.

Dame Carolyn McCall, the channel’s chief executive, said the companies remained “actively engaged” in negotiations and insisted that the decline in ad revenues would not affect the price of any deal.

It came as the broadcaster behind hits like Love Island reported a 5pc drop in revenues for its television arm in 2025, though this was offset by growth in its production division. Overall revenues remained flat at £4.1bn.

ITV confirmed last year it was in talks with Sky’s US owner, Comcast, over a £1.6bn takeover of its television arm. But details of any agreement have yet to materialise and bosses warned there was no certainty a deal would be reached.

The broadcaster, which blamed Budget uncertainty for a sharp drop in ad revenues at the end of last year, said its performance was strong against a “challenging” market.

Dame Carolyn said it was too early to assess the impact of war in the Middle East on ad budgets and argued that there was strong interest from brands before the Fifa World Cup this summer.

However, an insider at rival Channel 4, which is also expected to suffer a decline in ad revenues, warned that broadcasters were facing growing uncertainty.

They said: “The ad market was volatile in the fourth quarter and has continued to be challenging in the first quarter, with macro issues like the situation in the Middle East prompting brands to focus on advertising on a month-to-month basis versus long-term investment.

“We’re seeing progress in our transformation with streaming minutes outgrowing the market – but there’s no denying it’s tough out there right now.”

Streaming rivals

The potential takeover by Sky is seen as key to building scale to compete with streaming rivals such as Netflix and Disney, which are increasingly drawing both viewers and revenues away from traditional broadcasters.

ITV said it had been boosted by strong growth for its streaming service, ITVX, with viewing up 16pc and digital advertising revenues 12pc higher.

But any transaction could also be complicated by ITV’s public service broadcasting obligations, including a requirement to provide news and regional programming to the ITV network.

ITV is also facing fresh speculation over the future of its more prosperous production division, which makes programmes such as I’m a Celebrity... Get Me Out of Here! and Disney’s Rivals. ITV Studios reported a 5pc rise in revenues to £2.1bn last year.

Two of ITV’s largest production rivals – French group Banijay and The Traitors producer All3Media – this week agreed to merge in an $8bn (£6.3bn) deal to create the world’s largest independent TV maker. Both companies had previously explored a potential takeover of ITV Studios.

Dame Carolyn played down concerns that the tie-up left ITV’s production arm without the scale to compete. She said: “We are very confident that we have scale globally. We’re not going to do something that you might say is transformative if it isn’t at the right value for ITV Studios.”

ITV, which has been reducing programme spend in an effort to shore up its finances, said it would make a further £20m of non-content cost savings this year. It has now made more than £250m of cost savings since 2019.

The broadcaster said it expected advertising revenue to decline by a further 2pc in the first quarter as brands hold back budgets before the World Cup.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Borrowing costs rise at fastest pace since Truss mini-Budget
data.description:
Britain’s borrowing costs are rising at their fastest pace since the Liz Truss mini-Budget crisis, as the war in Iran sends shockwaves through markets.

The yield on 10-year UK gilts, as the government bonds are known, has risen by more than 0.4 percentage points this week from 4.23pc to 4.69pc.

It marks the sharpest increase in the yield on the bonds – considered a benchmark for the cost of servicing Britain’s £2.9tn national debt – in four years.

The last time borrowing costs rose this quickly was the week after Truss’s chancellor Kwasi Kwarteng unveiled £45bn of unfunded tax cuts in his mini-Budget in September 2022. Then, the yield on 10-year gilts jumped by 54 basis points in a week.

If sustained, the latest increase in Government borrowing costs will wipe £1.5bn off the Chancellor’s fiscal headroom, according to Andrew Goodwin, an economist at Oxford Economics.

Bond yields – the return the Treasury pays to buyers of its debt – have surged at a breakneck pace around the world over fears that soaring oil and gas prices will trigger a resurgence in inflation.

James Athey, a fund manager at Marlborough Group, said rising oil and gas prices had put “energy-sensitive economies like Europe and the UK in the firing line”.

He said: “Investors don’t know how long this disruption will last and ultimately how central banks will respond – in the absence of certainty, it’s a sensible option to reduce the likelihood of near-term rate cuts.”

Mr Athey said bond yields have also surged as hedge funds struggle to unwind large bets that interest rates would fall over the course of this year. This had caused “chunky moves in shorter dated yields”, he said. The yield on two-year UK gilts has surged by more than 40 basis points this week, in the biggest jump since August 2024.

Mr Athey said: “It’s not a rational repricing, and it’s taken UK yields to a level which is far too high in our opinion, but in the short term it is difficult to stand in the way amidst so much uncertainty and ahead of a weekend.”

The war in Iran has seen dramatic movements in markets as investors try to gauge the impact of a potentially long period of disruption to oil and gas supplies.

Traders have effectively ruled out another rate cut by the Bank of England later this year, having priced in at least two reductions just a week ago.

Oxford Economics predicted that household energy bills would rise by 13.5pc from July as the effect of higher oil and gas prices filters through to the UK economy.

Edward Allenby, a senior economist, said he expects shipments through the Strait of Hormuz to be disrupted for at least two months, pushing UK inflation this year from an average of 2.3pc to 2.7pc as oil prices average just under $80 (£59.70) a barrel in the second quarter.

But Neil Birrell, at Premier Miton Investors, warned that “even now the much-heightened risks from the war in the Middle East are only getting reflected in commodity and rates markets”.

He said: “The speed and scale of those moves must impact equities and other assets soon.”

Brent, the international benchmark, surpassed $90 on Friday for the first time in two years, putting it on track for its biggest weekly gain since the first Covid lockdown in 2020.

European gas prices have surged by 67pc over the past week, their steepest rise since Russia’s invasion of Ukraine in 2022.

Robin Brooks, a former chief economist at the Institute of International Finance, said the risks from this conflict are “an order of magnitude bigger” for oil markets than Russia’s invasion of Ukraine.

He said he was more pessimistic because the Iranian regime “is battling for its survival”.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: GB News backers pump in more cash as news channel loses £22m
data.description:
The financial backers of GB News have pumped more cash into the TV station after it fell to a £22m loss.

All Perspectives, the broadcaster’s parent company whose backers include Sir Paul Marshall, the billionaire hedge fund manager, put £17.7m of new funds into GB News for the year ending May 2025.

It brings total backing for the channel, set up to challenge the BBC and Sky News and whose presenters include Nigel Farage and Sir Jacob Rees-Mogg, to £141m.

GB News said it remained “on track” to be Britain’s biggest news channel by 2028.

The broadcaster said annual revenues had grown by 58pc, from £16.6m to £26.2m, largely because of an increase in advertising revenue as its share of TV audiences grew.

Monthly viewers climbed from 3.1 million to 3.7 million while weekly radio listeners climbed from 517,800 to 547,300.

GB News narrowed its losses, which fell from £32.7m the previous year to £22m.

Accounts for All Perspectives said it had raised £29m in new debt from investors in the year to May, leading its debt to rise from £10m to £40m.

The debt has since been converted into equity notes which will become shares in the company in future. The parent company reported a £30m loss, which included financing costs.

A spokesman for GB News said: “The directors are confident about the future of the group and we remain firmly on track to achieve our ambition of becoming the UK’s biggest news channel by 2028.”

The company said revenue had been “driven by significant increases in audience share across TV and radio, alongside continued expansion of our digital platforms”.

“This audience momentum has translated directly into strong growth in advertising, digital, sponsorship and radio revenues.

“GB News and its parent company remain focused on sustained audience growth and a continued commitment to high-quality, independent journalism that is bold, fearless and serves communities right across the United Kingdom.”

A source close to the company said investors remained committed to backing GB News and it was moving closer to breaking even.

GB News surpassed the BBC News channel and Sky News for the first time in July and has recently pushed into the US.

It was fined £100,000 by Ofcom in 2024 for breaking impartiality rules with a programme featuring Rishi Sunak, who was then the prime minister.

However, last year it won a High Court victory against Ofcom, overturning a ruling that a programme featuring Sir Jacob had broken the broadcasting code.

Ofcom has also dropped a plan to stop sitting MPs from working as TV presenters, which GB News had called “irrational”.
