data.title: Tax rises making pubs unaffordable, warns boss
data.description:
A quiet pint was once one of life’s simple pleasures. But the boss of a major hospitality group has warned a trip to the pub is becoming unaffordable for millions of Britons because of Labour’s tax rises.

Mark Loughborough, the chief executive of Various Eateries, said pubs were being put out of reach for drinkers and families because of higher costs imposed by Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor.

The industry veteran said trading for landlords was “very, very tough” and they were facing the “worst crisis in many years”.

Mr Loughborough, who spent 14 years at pub chain Young & Co where he ran 280 pubs, said: “We can’t just keep passing on so much in price. There becomes a danger that pubs are no longer accessible to everybody within the community.

“Whenever anybody visits from abroad, the first thing they want to do is see and admire the British pub. It tends to be when a pub is thriving, it has a good knock-on effect for the community around it.”

He added: “They’re producing jobs, bringing people together, helping with wellbeing. They’re such an important, integral part of what we’ve always done in this country.

“We really need to make sure ... the pub sector continues to thrive and has an ability to expand.”

Hundreds of pubs have announced plans to close this year because of higher taxes. The Telegraph has launched a campaign to save Britain’s pubs, which are under strain from high business rates and inflated wage bills.

After pub landlords revolted over planned business rate rises, the Chancellor announced a rescue package providing temporary relief consisting of a 15pc discount on rates bills for a year followed by a two-year freeze.

However, pub operators say the measures do not go far enough.

Mr Loughborough says calls from the industry for more support from the Government, such as a reduction in VAT, are falling on “deaf ears”.

“Sometimes it feels as though we’re not heard. It is very tough to get the message out there at the moment,” he said.

Mr Loughborough added: “There are pub companies and operators out there who are still managing to navigate the way through what is a very tough period but there needs to be some meaningful relief to help what is an important part of our country and our community that’s been around for hundreds of years, [that] we would like to have around for hundreds more.”

Founded a decade ago by Hugh Osmond, the Pizza Express entrepreneur, Various Eateries owns a string of restaurants under the Coppa Club and Noci brands.

It is also expanding into pubs. On Friday, it unveiled a deal to buy an £11m portfolio of pubs with rooms in the Cotswolds, Berkshire, Surrey and Hampshire.

The deal will see it buy the sites from Grosvenor Pubs and rebrand them as the Linwood Collection.

The pubs are Wild Thyme & Honey in the Cotswolds, the Hare & Hounds in Berkshire, the Stag on the River in Surrey and the Wellington Arms in Hampshire.

A Treasury spokesman said: “We are backing Britain’s pubs – cutting their new business rates bills by 15pc, extending World Cup opening hours and increasing the Hospitality Support Fund to £10m to help venues grow. Later this year, we’ll build on our Pride in Place programme with the Government’s new High Streets Strategy to revitalise our town centres.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: The British-designed microchip that is powering Putin’s deadly missiles
data.description:
Yana Khudyo, 15, woke to find herself trapped and buried beneath the rubble of her home.

Two of her ribs and one leg had been shattered by an explosion.

It took rescuers 40 minutes to pull her from the wreckage after Igor, her 24-year-old brother, heard her calling beneath the debris.

Viktoria and Oleg, their parents, and Danylo, their 17-year-old brother, were already dead. It took rescuers another 18 hours to find their bodies, and for Igor to identify them.

What ripped the Khudyo family apart on April 24, 2025, was a KN-23 ballistic missile. And, at the heart of that tragedy, helping power the missile, there may have been a British microchip.

Fired by Russia, it struck a residential block in Kyiv’s Sviatoshynskyi district, murdering twelve Ukrainians as they slept.

After the attack, Volodymyr Zelensky, the Ukrainian president, said the weapon responsible for orphaning Yana contained 116 Western-made components.

Little additional detail was given.

The type of North Korean missile used in the strike has been found to contain British-made converters, according to Ukrainian investigators. 

It is one of thousands of Russian weapons that use Western – including British – dual-use military-grade components. Dual-use devices have both military and civilian use, meaning they can be used as easily in a computer as in a missile.

When Igor arrived at the site of the devastation, about 20 minutes after the strike, he saw his childhood home in smithereens.  

“I started desperately calling out to my mother, my father, to Daniel and to Yana,” he said from his apartment just outside Kyiv. 

“The mobile phones of the dead were ringing, but no one was picking up,” he continued as we sat in darkness caused by Russian air strikes.

“I tried to call Yana and when I heard her pick up the phone I knew she was alive. But there was no sound.

“She sent me a text which said ‘I am under rubble, I am dying’ and her rough location.

“I started frantically searching, despite Shahed drones circling overhead, until finally I heard her calling out to me from beneath the destruction.”

Yana had been thrown from her bed and trapped under a cupboard. 

She was operated on twice. “I found out my parents had died on Instagram while I was in the hospital,” she said.

“The psychologist was meant to tell me, but it was all over social media by the time I woke up from my surgeries.”

The impact of this experience on her is clear.

“I want to join the military [when I am 18],” she said proudly.

“This is the only future I see for myself because I want to be part of the people fighting for their land and their parents. I want to become a hero for Ukraine.”

With 16,000 international trading restrictions in place in Russia, how could British-made parts have found their way into Russia’s deadly arsenal, and why are sanctions not working?

The Telegraph and the Committee for Freedom in Hong Kong Foundation (CFHK), a human rights watchdog, are exposing how middlemen in Hong Kong have shipped thousands of dual-use components worth millions of dollars from Western Europe to Russia.

Campaigners want the Government to open an urgent “investigation” into how British parts reportedly ended up in weapons used against Ukraine, and to “close any loopholes” around pre-existing laws.

On Sept 7, 2024, a North Korean KN-23/24 ballistic missile with the serial number 312518759 was shot down in the villages of Myrne and Bilyky, Poltava Oblast, Ukraine.

It was one of 60 KN-23 or Hwasong 11A missiles supplied to Putin by Kim Jong-un and launched that year.

Buried among the rubble and shrapnel, investigators said they found two products labeled as coming from the British company XP Power: a DC/DC converter and a power converter.

Converters transfer direct currents from one voltage level to another and are commonly used to stabilise, boost, or lower a voltage within a machine.

XP Power, which has headquarters in Reading, Berks, said converters allegedly found in Russian weapons were either counterfeit or “sold before the invasion”. The firm has not provided any additional detail or evidence to support these assertations.

Both chips recovered in the rubble were allegedly manufactured in 2023, a year after the Kremlin’s invasion of Ukraine, and after XP Power said it had ceased trading with Russia.  

Russian customs data from 2022-2024, obtained by CFHK and reviewed by The Telegraph, found that 799 XP Power-labelled cargoes valued at around $2.5m (£1.8m) had been imported by Russia. It is not clear how XP Power-labelled devices ended up in North Korea. 

It is unknown where exactly the missiles containing the XP Power-branded converters were made in North Korea. The country expanded its missile production capactity in 2024. The Ryongsong Machine Complex in the Hamhung region is a key factory where these weapons are built.

The CFHK figures, which come from tracking the sale and shipments of high-risk goods to sanctioned countries, suggest almost half were sent via Hong Kong to electronic supply companies Woeroon Electronic Sourcing Limited, SSP LTS and Imaxchip. None of these companies responded to requests for comment.

It is not clear if these companies were seeking to breach sanctions, or if they were aware these shipments might be used by the Russian military.

XP Power disputed CFHK's findings, stating that “all orders from customers and shipments to customers are thoroughly checked for sanctioned countries and companies”.

The company said it had “never traded” directly with Woeroon, SSP LTS, Imaxchip or any of the companies named in the shipment data.

There is no suggestion that XP had any knowledge of where its components would end up, and no evidence the company was complicit in sending parts to Ukraine.

But it is common for middleman businesses committing sanctions evasion to pass dual-use stock from company to company.

“Naturally, we have less visibility of sales that might pass through a number of hands in the supply chain,” a spokesman for XP Power accepted.

She added: “We have stringent export controls in place and take our responsibilities under the various sanction regimes very seriously – after the invasion in February 2022, we immediately stopped all shipments to Russia.  

“We take our obligations in this area very seriously and we will continue to work with others in the supply chain to ensure their controls are as tight as ours.

“All of our sales contracts now include clauses to specifically prohibit onward sale or re-exporting to Russia.”

It is not just the KN-23 missile that has been found to contain British-made hardware.

British components have also been found in deadly Shahed drones, First-Person View drones, guided missiles and scores of other weapons launched by the Russian military into Ukraine.

In total, Ukrainian investigators have found 41 British-made components on the battlefield since the start of the war in 2022.  

On Dec 1 last year, the Ukrainian military downed a newly modified Geran-2 or Shahed attack drone carrying an R-60 infrared guided AAM (Air-to-Air Missile) on a top-mounted rail launcher.

On Jan 12 2026, Ukraine brought down a Geran-2 or Shahed UAV with Verba MPADS (man-portable air defence system).

In both cases, investigators said they found a British-made Raspberry Pi microcomputer in the drone.

When contacted, Raspberry Pi said it was aware that its products had been “diverted through third-party supply chains” and used in Russian drones.

The tech firm said that “diversion can occur via complex and opaque chains of onward sale in jurisdictions where visibility is limited”.

A spokesman for the company added: “Raspberry Pi complies fully with all applicable UK, EU and international sanctions regimes and operates controls designed to track and prevent diversion to Russia or the Russian defence sector.

“The company does not disclose the detailed mechanics of its anti-diversion processes.”

Campaigners said this was not good enough.

Samuel Bickett, a former political prisoner in Hong Kong and co-author of CFHK’s report, said: “British manufacturers of components used in Russian weapons are not doing enough to control the flow of these goods across the world.

TT Electronics in Woking, Surrey, looks like any other grey, industrial manufacturing business.

According to its website, it makes advanced electronics for “aerospace, defence, healthcare, automation and electrification”.

It has 19 manufacturing sites across Britain, China, North America and Thailand.

Ukrainian investigators say they have found 11 transistors manufactured by TT Electronics in automated radio jammers and transmitter interference devices used by the Russians on the battlefield.

Between 2022 and 2024, documents reviewed by CFHK suggest that Russia received 59 imports of TT Electronics-labelled components with a declared value of $1.2m. Around 40 per cent of these shipments, worth $700,000, went via Hong Kong.

Hong Kong has become an increasingly important hub for sanctions evasion used by foreign governments seeking to dodge Western embargoes. 

The TT Electronics shipments were imported by four Hong Kong-based traders and logistics firms, including companies associated with the electronics supplier Woeroon. 

Woeroon, which made 25,707 shipments to Russia between 2022 and 2024, is a collective of several unrelated companies. 

Sir Iain Duncan Smith, co-chairman of the Interparliamentary Alliance on China, said: “This Government is turning out to be a lapdog to China.

“It is China and the CCP-controlled Hong Kong that is allowing dual-use components into Russia.

“Sanction evasion has been going on for a while and it is absolutely wrong for the government to turn a blind eye to it.

“The British Government doesn’t seem to care when the sanctions we are meant to be imposing are not being enforced at all. It is an absolute disgrace.

“And the result is that Russia can continue fighting its brutal war of invasion.”

Golledge Electronics, headquartered in Basingstoke, also has products which appear to have been used by Russia.

The company’s “frequency control products” are said by investigators to have been repeatedly discovered inside sophisticated Russian weaponry, including the Tornado C guided missile, the 9M727 cruise missile and the Kh-47M2 Kinzhal missile.

Since Putin’s invasion in 2022, Ukrainian government investigators say they have discovered eight Golledge-labelled components in Russian weapons. Most recently, a Quartz oscillator was found in a cruise missile on Oct 25 2025.

Between 2022 and 2024, Russia received 114 Golledge Electronics-labelled shipments, 30 of which came via six Hong Kong-based companies, including Woeroon and Asialink Shanghai International Logistics Co Ltd, according to analysis by CFHK.

Asialink, a cargo transportation service which has an official partnership with Russia’s national airline Aeroflot, supplied 3,700 high-risk goods worth $6.3m to Russia from March 2022 to January 2024, according to CFHK. It even used Russian as the default language for its website in 2023. 

While the US has already sanctioned Asialink for continued shipments to businesses in Russia, the EU and UK have not.

TT Electronics and Golledge Electronics were contacted for comment.

There is no suggestion that any of the British companies named are deliberately selling their products to Russia.

Bill Browder – the American-born businessman, author and head of the Global Magnitsky Justice campaign – said: “It’s outrageous that British components would have found their way into Russian weapons. 

“This is exactly what the UK sanctions were supposed to prevent. 

“I hope that we figure out how these components found their way into these weapons so sanctions can be tightened.

“Innocent people in Ukraine have died because of these weapons being used on them and we need to get to the bottom of this.”

Chloe Cheung, a Hong Kong dissident who has a China-issued 1,000,000 HK dollar (£100,000) bounty on her head, co-wrote the CFHK report. She said: “It’s shocking that the city I grew up in is now linked to illegal weapons trade rather than to the values of integrity and accountability that once defined it.

“Hong Kong is funnelling components into Russia’s war machine. Our city is indirectly contributing to the killing of Ukrainians. This is the result of Chinese Communist Party control.

“A city once known for rule of law and clean governance, which claims to be an international financial hub, has instead become a haven for dirty money and illegal traders, barely recognisable as the place I once called home.”

Lord Austin, vice-chairman of the Magnitsky Sanctions and Reparation all-party parliamentary group, said: “It would be completely unacceptable if British components are fuelling Russia’s war machine.

“Ministers must take immediate steps to close any sanctions loopholes.”

Mr Bickett, the CFHK report's co-author, added: “It is critical that Britain work with allies to create a genuine multilateral authority with the power to institute sanctions quickly and broadly against all Russian and non-Russian parties involved in evading export controls.”

Responding to our findings, a government spokesman said: “We take reports of goods from UK companies being found in Russian weaponry incredibly seriously.

“We have banned the export of thousands of goods to Russia, including every battlefield item Ukraine has brought to our attention, and together with our international partners have implemented the most severe package of sanctions ever imposed on a major economy.

“Non-compliance with sanctions is a serious offence punishable through large financial penalties or criminal prosecution.”

Konstantin Bychek, 36, was standing by his kitchen window when a North Korean KN-23 struck the neighbouring apartment block on April 24, 2025.

“The shock wave threw me against the wall,” he said while standing in the remains of his old home, almost a year after the deadly strike.

“My face and neck were shredded by shards of glass, and I was drenched in blood.

“I shut my eyes, but behind my eyelids I could see a blinding orange flash [from] when the explosion happened.”

Natalia, his wife, who also survived the explosion, said Europe should crack down on sanctions evasion. “We would like all possible supplies to Russia to be cut off,” she said.

“I don't understand how this can happen, given that Russia is under sanctions.”

Igor, who is now his sister Yana’s legal guardian, warned: “Western Europe should not forget what is going on in Ukraine.

“If Europe doesn't help us now, you will see the same in your countries.”

As we approach the fourth anniversary of Russia’s invasion of Ukraine, we must ask ourselves: why is Europe still shooting itself?

data.title: ‘Heartbroken’ BrewDog founder apologises to staff and investors
data.description:
James Watt, co-founder of BrewDog, has apologised to tens of thousands of investors left out of pocket by the company’s cut-price sale.

The entrepreneur said he was “heartbroken” by BrewDog’s downfall, admitting that the craft beer business had “expanded too fast and diversified too broadly”.

Growing struggles led to BrewDog, once valued at £1bn, being sold for just £33m to a US private equity firm earlier this week, leaving the 220,000 so-called “equity-punk” investors who crowdfunded the business empty-handed.

It also resulted in nearly 500 job losses as the company shuttered dozens of pubs as part of a rescue by Tilray Brands, a US drinks and medical cannabis company.

Posting on social media on Wednesday, Mr Watt said: “I am heartbroken for all of the hardworking and passionate team members who have lost their jobs.

“I am heartbroken for all of our brilliant equity punks who did not get the return on their investment they wanted. And heartbroken to have dedicated the best 20 years of my life to something that ultimately did not have the ending we all wished for.”

He added: “During my 17 years in charge, there were highs, lows, successes, failures, huge gambles and many mistakes along the way. Ultimately, the mistakes hurt far more than the successes console.

“To our team members leaving this week: thank you. You helped build something that mattered. I am sorry we could not protect you. To our equity punks: thank you for having the conviction to believe in the business when this was just two humans, one dog and a crazy idea.”

He also addressed his failed attempt to regain control of BrewDog earlier this month, saying: “I would have loved to save every single job and every single equity punk investment. Ultimately, I couldn’t. That will stay with me.”

Mr Watt, who co-founded BrewDog in 2007 and was chief executive until 2024, took some responsibility for the company’s downfall by admitting he “did not control spend well enough across the business”.

He also said that he “did not respond to certain crises that we faced – and we faced many – in a way that was authentic and true to who I am”.

“Those decisions sit with me,” he said.

Mr Watt has been subject to criticism from BrewDog fans ever since he sold a 22pc stake to TSG Consumer Partners, a US equity firm, in 2017.

He reportedly received £50m from the deal, which included a clause guaranteeing TSG an 18pc rate of return on its investment.

Mr Watt’s reputation was also sullied by allegations that he had presided over a “culture of fear” in which staff were bullied and “treated like objects” at BrewDog.

He said that his responses to criticism “always came from PR advisers”, claiming that he regretted not taking ownership of them.

Mr Watt’s post was met with anger by dozens of early investors who accused him of abandoning investors.

One wrote: “Why didn’t you make sure that the equity punks got a payout at the same time that you did, if you cared so passionately about them getting a return on their ‘investment’?

“There was plenty of opportunity to treat them as real investors.”

Another added: “Are you ‘heartbroken’ you gave the institutional investor preference over the equity punks, James? An arrangement that ensured we received no return on our investments.”

As part of the rescue deal, BrewDog’s brewery in Aberdeen, as well as 11 of its most profitable UK pubs, were acquired by Tilray. However, the remaining 38 were closed with immediate effect.

Bryan Simpson, of Unite the Union, said the handling of BrewDog’s downfall, during which staff were laid off during a 25-minute Microsoft Teams call, had been a “national disgrace”.

He said: “BrewDog once styled itself as ‘punk’, progressive and proudly different. It courted customers, workers and investors on the promise that it was challenging corporate excess.

“Yet it abandoned the real living wage, closed 10 bars last October, and has now overseen the collapse of a business once valued at £1bn into a £33m fire sale.”

Tilray Brands vowed on Tuesday to retain the brand’s recipes and “Scottish roots”.

A spokesman said: “Our focus is simple: reinvest in the brand, expand its reach, and strengthen the platform for long-term growth. Craft comes first. Always.”

A source familiar with the matter said it was hoped equity punks would retain the discounts and other benefits, including a free beer on their birthdays.

data.title: Major banks raise mortgage rates as war in Iran hits borrowing costs
data.description:
The first major lenders have raised mortgage rates as the conflict in the Middle East and a potential inflation shock push up the price of borrowing.

HSBC, Natwest, Nationwide and Coventry Building Society announced their fixed rates would increase from this week. Challenger lender, Gen H, said it had reversed a cut because of “global uncertainty”.

Before the outbreak of the war last weekend, markets had been expecting the Bank of England to cut the Bank Rate – currently 3.75pc – twice more this year, with an estimated 85pc chance of a reduction in March.

But markets now only expect one cut for the rest of 2026, suggesting that mortgage rates will remain higher for longer.

Natwest and Nationwide said rates on fixed mortgages would rise by up to 0.25pc on Friday. HSBC said it would also increase its rates on the same day. Coventry Building Society told its customers it was increasing all fixed rates for residential and buy-to-let mortgages from March 8.

On Wednesday, a smaller lender, Gen H, reversed a cut it had been poised to make and instead made rate increases of up to 0.25 percentage points. The lender said: “The war in Iran is creating uncertainty in global markets.”

The average five-year fixed-rate deal was 4.95pc on Thursday morning, according to financial analysts Moneyfacts.

Those looking for a two-year deal could expect an average rate of 4.83pc – with both averages ticking up slightly compared to Wednesday.

Inflation fell to a ten-month low of 3.2pc in January this year, official figures showed, with predictions that it would fall close to the Bank of England’s 2pc target by April. But these estimates are now out-of-date, as the sharp jump in the cost of oil is likely to drive up inflation once more.

Brent crude prices have jumped by 12pc since Israel and the US began bombing Iran on Saturday.

The National Institute of Economic and Social Research, an economic think tank, said if higher energy prices persist, it could force the Bank of England to push interest rates back up above 4pc.

The Bank of England will announce its latest interest rate decision on March 19.

Earlier this year, banks had been competing to lower rates, with Barclays lowering rates on two-year mortgages to as little as 3.70pc. Nationwide also dropped rates to as low as 3.58pc.

Another indication that mortgage rates will jump was that swap rates, the main pricing mechanism for fixed rates, hit 30-day highs on Wednesday.

The five-year swap rose by 6pc from 3.50pc on Friday, before the conflict started, to 3.71pc on Wednesday. It usually takes approximately two to three weeks for changes in swap rates to filter through to lenders.

David Hollingworth, of broker L&C Mortgages, said: “Once we enter this cycle of lenders adjusting their rates, we know that it almost invariably results in others following suit. The current uncertainty means that this upward pressure doesn’t look likely to ease quickly, although there are signs that the market reaction is at least levelling off for now.”

Aaron Strutt, of broker Trinity Financial, said: “It seems almost certain we are going to see a lot more rate changes over the coming days, so if you are on the hunt for a mortgage, it is worth locking into a new deal now.”

Adrian Anderson, a mortgage broker at Anderson Harris, said: “It was all looking very encouraging a week or two weeks ago, very encouraging for the mortgage market. Now, it is definitely much more uncertain.”

He added: “It shows how delicate the market is.”

data.title: The Nimby’s guide to taking on developers – and winning
data.description:
As Britain grapples with an acute housing shortage, one sub-section of society has become a lightning rod for righteous fury: Nimbys.

The term, which stands for “not in my backyard”, is used as shorthand for perceived militant localism, applied to community campaigners who throw sand in the gears of all manner of new developments, from house extensions to wind farms.

Sir Keir Starmer has said Labour is “taking on the Nimbys”, who see themselves as the last line of defence against greedy developers looking for a quick buck.

The Prime Minister has pledged to build 1.5 million new homes by tearing up planning restrictions and making it more difficult for the public to block new major infrastructure projects.

In December the Planning and Infrastructure Act 2025 became law, designed to speed up the planning process and accelerate housebuilding – for instance, by reducing environmental red tape.

However, Nimbys’ objections can still succeed; a well-organised campaign can stop a project in its tracks.

Here, Telegraph Money explains how to be an effective Nimby.

How can I find out about planning applications?

Property owners or developers need planning permission from the local authority to apply to build anything from extensions to a small solar farm. These applications must be made public, so it’s relatively easy to keep abreast of the proposals in your area.

“Extensions make up over 90pc of all planning applications,” said Martin Gaine, a chartered town planner and planning permission expert.

“The next most common is small developments, like somebody building a house in their garden, or a school being extended.”

A developer wanting to build multiple houses or a new block of flats on a plot of land will also need to go through this process. For applications for a major development, local authorities must publish a notice in a local newspaper and either post a notice at the project site that passers-by can read, or notify the occupiers and owners of adjoining properties.

Planning applications are assigned to a case officer, and members of the public then have an opportunity to comment on the plans. The deadline for comments is 21 days from the date a site notice is put up or notice is served on neighbours, or 14 days from when an advert appears in a local newspaper.

Parish and town councils have 21 days from the date they were notified to make an official comment.

Alongside inviting the public to comment, local planning authorities also have to consult any organisations whose interests may be affected by a proposed development.

If the development could mean an increase in traffic, for instance, the local highways authority will be notified. Natural England assesses applications that could affect wildlife, while issues concerning waste or pollution are referred to the Environment Agency.

Once the planning authority has what it needs it makes an assessment, it will write up a report recommending the development for approval, sometimes with conditions or obligations attached, or it will issue a refusal.

In either case, the authority must give reasons for its decision. Local planning authorities should usually make a decision within eight weeks.

The rules for a successful planning objection

If you find out a new development, neighbour’s extension or any other manner of projects are in the works that are going to affect you or your property negatively, there are certain rules you can follow to get your concerns addressed and, hopefully, resolved.

1. Don’t jump straight to an objection

Negotiation and compromise rather than outright objection can often be the best option, according to Mr Gaine. “Too many objectors adopt a ‘scorched-earth policy’, when they would be better off accepting that a development is likely to go ahead, and trying instead to influence its final design.

“Ask yourself: could the project be altered in a way that meets your concerns? Perhaps you could encourage the council to impose conditions limiting hours of construction, for example, or to require the developer to improve the landscaping.”

Mr Gaine also said discussing alterations with the developer directly, rather than through the planning case officer, is often the best bet.

“The case officer will only be interested if they think the concern represents a key planning consideration,” he explained. “So if you say to the case officer, ‘Could you ask them to move that window’, and the case officer thinks it’s not causing you any harm, they won’t do it. They’ll only do it if they think it’s an important planning issue.

“Instead, you could meet up with the developer or the homeowner and say, ‘Look, is there any way you could leave a one-metre gap to my boundary, or move this window?’. They might agree, or plant trees to screen it.

“A developer who thinks this is a good opportunity to stop [someone] objecting might well work with you.”

2. Be precise

If you go ahead with a formal objection, you must explain why you believe the proposed building plans will negatively affect you – rather than just objecting to the idea of the plans. Mr Gaine said: “Make sure you properly understand what is being proposed – take time to read the application plans and documents.

“Rather than taking a scattergun approach and objecting on all possible grounds, think carefully about what exactly bothers you about a development. Be brief and precise.

“Try not to be emotional – angry, ranting letters are not credible – you want to appear thoughtful and rational, not unhinged.”

As your objections go to a planning case worker, they ought to be directed at their level, according to Mr Gaine. “Limit your objections to planning considerations – design, impact on neighbours, parking, ecology – not the value of your home, the quality of your views or the impact of the building works themselves.”

3. Avoid burning bridges

“It’s important to take a step back and reflect on whether your concerns are reasonable,” said Mr Gaine. Is the sound of building work a valid reason for your neighbour to never have the loft extension they’ve always wanted?

If you think your objections are fair enough, be aware that it might burn bridges – so be careful with your wording.

“Remember objections are public,” said Mr Gaine. “You’ll have to live beside your neighbour long after you have objected to their planning application – don’t object in a way that poisons the relationship forever or starts a dispute.

“I have had cases of neighbours keying each other’s cars, or avoiding each other in the street.”

4. Don’t ‘bulk object’

According to Mr Gaine, residents banding together to try to halt a small planning application is rarely effective. “Starting petitions and copying and pasting other objections can come across as ‘contrived and overly-coordinated’,” he said.

“Objections are best if they are spontaneous, and if each objector expresses their own concerns in their own words.”

5. Submit objections on time, to the right place

Planning objections can be submitted in several ways, and can be as simple as clicking a button on the application.

“The easiest way to submit your objections is to go to the planning pages of the council’s website,” Mr Gaine said. “There’ll be a button to bring up the application so you can see the plans, and another button to click if you want to object. You can just type in your concerns directly there.

“Or, you can write a letter or email the planning email address. Make sure to include the [application] reference number so it reaches the right person.

“Although the council will set a 21-day deadline for a response, it must consider any representations made before it reaches its decision, so don’t worry if you have missed the cut-off.”

Planning applications may change after you’ve submitted your objection, but Mr Gaine said the case officer should be able to tell you about that. “Keep in touch with the case officer so you are aware of any revisions made to the application and to be sure they understand your concerns.

“If you are very concerned, you can contact your local ward councillors for support and advice. You can also hire a planning consultant to represent you.”

Are there other ways to tilt the odds in my favour?

The majority of planning applications are signed off by senior planning officers in the local authority, but in some cases the decision will go to a committee of councillors instead.

If this happens, the approval decision will be made at a public meeting, where anyone in attendance has the right to make a short speech.

This method of decision-making works in a Nimby’s favour, according to Mr Gaine.

“Most planning applications are decided by the planners, under ‘delegated powers’. But where local feeling is strong, they can be sent to the planning committee of elected councillors for a decision.

“Councillors are more likely to refuse permission than the technocratic planners, so it can be a good idea to find out what the council’s criteria are for sending applications to committee.

“You’d have to contact the council to find out. In extremis, you would need to do a Freedom of Information request, but it should be publicly accessible.

“It’s something they try to keep a bit quiet, as if everyone knows that you need, say, five objections, then people will just make sure there are five objections, one way or another. The authority doesn’t want the system played with.”

What happens if my objection is upheld?

If planning is refused, the developer or homeowner will decide whether they want to give up altogether, resubmit the plan to the council with revisions, or appeal.

If they appeal, the decision is taken away from the council and given to the Planning Inspectorate.

This independent body reviews all the evidence to reach a decision. If the appeal is successful, the project has planning permission and can go ahead. If the plan is refused then it is the end of the road for that proposal, because both the council and the appeal inspector have rejected it. But that’s not necessarily the end of the matter.

“Victories can be pyrrhic, and you may find yourself objecting again and again as the applicant keeps trying to get their proposal through,” Mr Gaine said. “If the application is resubmitted, you must object again.”

If the application goes to appeal and is approved, there’s no straightforward mechanism in the planning system for a further objection or appeal.

“As a last resort, you can go for a judicial review – an appeal to the High Court – as you can with any decision by a public body. But this route is costly and impractical,” Mr Gaine added.

What happens if my objection fails?

If a project gets given the green light despite your objection, all is not quite lost.

“Even if a project has been approved, it won’t necessarily go ahead,” Mr Gaine said. “Some people get permission and never build it, some get permission and then try to sell the land or property – planning approval is valid for three years.”

If work starts on a project, then it’s too late to influence the plan. But you can still monitor the development to make sure it’s carried out in accordance with the application.

“A lot of developers make an extension or property slightly bigger than they should. If so, you can contact enforcement and get that sorted.”

This tactic came into play when one of Mr Gaine’s clients had a neighbour who was building a large basement extension in Kensington & Chelsea.

“I said, ‘Look, you’re not going to win [the planning objection], so your best bet is to focus on trying to make sure the council is strict in applying its conditions – the hours of operation for the excavation works, and the need for a basement impact assessment to check that the works will not cause any harm. 

“You can also require them to do a construction management plan so you can see where they’ll put the skips and where they’ll remove debris.”

‘Getting involved early in the local authority’s plans is crucial’

When it comes to opposing plans for thousands of new homes, few have more experience than Rosie Pearson.

In 2021, Ms Pearson founded the Community Planning Alliance, a group of more than 700 grassroots campaigns trying to preserve urban and rural green spaces.

“Our supporters are fighting inappropriate housing from 40 houses at the edge of the village to a garden town of 20,000 homes – generally because what is proposed is not affordable to locals, destroys farmland or countryside and does not come with infrastructure,” she said.

“It’s not just housing, people are fighting against incinerators, roads, airports, pylons – anything in the planning system that people think is bad for the environment or foisted on communities without properly being thought through.”

Ms Pearson became a campaigner after a group of wealthy landowners brought a proposal for a new town in Essex, backed by the Government. Before long, two more towns had been added to the proposal.

In 2020, after years of lobbying, the plan to build 46,000 homes was slashed to just 9,000.

“We successfully argued the plan was so flawed, so broken, and that they couldn’t fund all the infrastructure that was needed – and they kept glossing over that.”

The same advice for opposing single building plans also applies to big town projects, but Ms Pearson says that getting involved in the local authority’s plans early is crucial if you’re trying to block a large development.

“Make sure you get involved right at the beginning of the process, even before the planning application is submitted,” she said. “If a plan arises for a new town, a local authority will start with a ‘call for sites’. They ask all the landowners ‘Who wants to get rich?’ and put land forward.

“Then it goes through three or four years of refinement, trying to create strategy and policy around it.

“Communities need to be watching their local council news to see what’s happening. By the time you hear about it, a site for a development might already have been allocated in a council’s local plan.

“Once this happens it’s very hard to say no to it, because it’s gone through a whole checking process to see if it’s suitable and sustainable.”

Ms Pearson and the Community Planning Alliance are currently opposing a Nationally Significant Infrastructure Project (NSIP) – the term for a much larger development project, which can include motorways, electricity pylons, nuclear power stations, runways or wind farms. In this case, it’s a National Grid project to run 112 miles of electricity pylons from Norwich to Tilbury, crossing Norfolk, Suffolk and Essex, to carry green energy generated from wind farms in the North Sea.

“We’re super supportive of that,” Ms Pearson said. “Wind farms are great, green energy’s great – what we’re saying is every time a wind farm’s built it asks for a connection onshore, and National Grid gives one without any sort of coordination.

“You get a horrific spider’s web of cables under the sea and then it makes landfall and is damaging – trees and hedgerows are torn up. And then they need to get this power to London and the South East.

“We’re saying it’s absolute madness, keep it off land for as long as possible, and make one big grid rather than this piecemeal approach that’s so damaging.”

The Community Planning Alliance is one group of many taking part in a nationwide day of demonstration on April 18, to show the Prime Minister “how much nature, parks and green spaces matter to voters”.

If a planned development is designated a Nationally Significant Infrastructure Project (NSIP), the decision goes straight to the Planning Inspectorate, instead of working its way through the local authority. The inspector will make a recommendation, and the Secretary of State will get the final say.

How can I object to an NSIP?

Objecting to larger projects works differently from more standard planning applications.

Understand the proposal

“With NSIPs, the crucial thing is to check if the project accords with the national policy statement on that infrastructure,” Ms Pearson said.

The National Policy Statements are a set of government guidelines for land use and development in England, promoting sustainable development, housing, economic growth, and environmental protection.

Before you object to the Planning Inspectorate, make sure you understand the NSIP proposal and its potential impact on the local community, environment, or interests.

Review all available documents, including the Development Consent Order application – the legal permission granted by the Government for major infrastructure projects – and Environmental Impact Assessment reports.

Mobilise support

Wider community support can be important for halting a project that the Government might already have set its sights on.

Ms Pearson said: “Start to make noise, get a campaign group and pull people together, fundraise, get a Facebook group, have a public meeting.

“Lobbying is important. It’s about persuading the powers that be to change tack, and make things uncomfortable for people – the council, local politicians and government.”

Ground your objection in planning policy

When objecting, clearly refer to the specific policies within the National Policy Statement that you believe the NSIP proposal does not comply with. Explain how the project may conflict with these policies.

Write a well-structured objection letter clearly stating your concerns. Be concise, factual and specific. Include your name and contact details.

If you have technical or scientific evidence that supports your objection, include it. This could be studies, reports, or expert opinions.

“It’s a twin-track process – shouting and evidence,” Ms Pearson said. “You can make all the noise in the world you want. But if you don’t get the evidence right it won’t change what’s being proposed.

“Make sure that what you’re not doing is jumping around saying ‘I don’t like it, it ruins the view, it affects my house price’ – that will get you nowhere.”

It can also be helpful to present an alternative to the project that’s being proposed; those in charge might be more willing to back down if they can see another option.

“With the pylons, we’re saying the problem with what’s being proposed is that there was no consultation about potential alternatives,” said Ms Pearson. “There are laws that you must present options and alternatives, rather than presenting something that’s already been decided

“The Community Planning Alliance always tries to focus on what the positive alternatives might be. For the pylons, we got some experts together to find a better solution. Instead of saying, ‘No, that’s rubbish’, we’re saying, ‘It’s bad because of this and that, and there’s also another way of doing it’.”

Submit the objection

To have your objection officially considered, you must submit it to the Planning Inspectorate.

Information about the consultation period – which varies depending on the project – and how to submit objections will be provided by the project developer or the Planning Inspectorate.

You can also request to speak at the examination of the NSIP application, giving you an opportunity to present your objections directly.

Seek legal advice

If you believe your objection raises legal issues, you may want to seek legal advice or representation.

“If you can afford to do so, definitely fundraise for a planning consultant or a planning barrister,” Ms Pearson said.

“With the pylons, for example, we have a really well-known planning barrister on board, and his help is invaluable, particularly now we’re at the examination stage.”

Stay informed

Stay updated on the progress of the NSIP application and any decisions or changes. Attend public meetings and consultations to continue expressing your concerns and monitoring the process.

data.title: Telegraph to be sold to Axel Springer for £575m
data.description:
The Telegraph is to be acquired by the German publisher Axel Springer for £575m in a surprise raid that brings a potent new force to Fleet Street.

Mathias Döpfner, Axel Springer’s chief executive and controlling shareholder, has gatecrashed a planned takeover by the owner of The Daily Mail, Lord Rothermere, to end two and a half years of debilitating uncertainty.

He has bought security over The Telegraph, which can be readily converted into full ownership, from RedBird IMI, the UAE-backed vehicle which attempted to take control in late 2023.

DMGT, Lord Rothermere’s holding company, said it wished “every success to Axel Springer and The Telegraph” as it criticised what it called the “protracted and out-of-date” media regulation that delayed its deal.

The all-cash transaction marks by far Axel Springer’s biggest move into the UK market and a significant step towards former music journalist Mr Döpfner’s strategy of expansion in the English-speaking world. The company previously unsuccessfully bid for The Telegraph in 2004 and for the Financial Times in 2015.

Axel Springer had revenues of €3.8bn and reported a profit of €738m last year, making it one of the world’s largest news publishers.

Mr Döpfner, 63, said: “Axel Springer was founded in 1946 under a British press licence. He built his company inspired by the tradition of Fleet Street. The Telegraph was his North Star.

“More than 20 years ago, we tried to acquire The Telegraph and did not succeed. Now our dream comes true. To be the owner of this institution of quality British journalism is a privilege and a duty.

“We want to grow The Telegraph, while preserving its distinctive character and legacy, to help it become the most-read and intellectually inspiring centre-right media outlet in the English-speaking world. The Telegraph stands for freedom, personal responsibility, democratic values and a belief in open societies and market economies.”

Axel Springer’s international portfolio also includes Politico, Business Insider and the newsletter publisher Morning Brew. None has a significant share of the UK news market, meaning the takeover of The Telegraph is unlikely to raise competition or media plurality concerns.

The German publisher has an unusual corporate constitution which guarantees its support for the state of Israel and for decades its backing for European integration. However in recent weeks it removed the longstanding reference to a “united Europe” from the document, which it has not applied to its non-German assets.

A spokesman for RedBird IMI, which was blocked from taking control by a ban on foreign state ownership of newspapers, said: “Following a swift and efficient negotiation, we are pleased to have reached an agreement with Axel Springer to purchase RedBird IMI’s interest in the Daily Telegraph newspaper.

“With the strength of their commercial offer and a straightforward regulatory path to ownership we believe that Axel Springer is well placed to take The Telegraph forward into its next chapter.”

It remains possible that the Government will investigate Axel Springer’s takeover for its impact on the public interest, a process that would be likely to take months. The Telegraph has been in limbo since June 2023, when the Barclay family lost control to Lloyds Banking Group over their unpaid debts.

Lord Rothermere agreed to pay £500m for security over The Telegraph in November last year. The Government gave permission for the rights to be transferred last month.

However, DMGT, Lord Rothermere’s holding company, did not make a payment and its exclusivity lapsed, opening the door to Axel Springer.

DMGT did not fully commit to the acquisition after Lisa Nandy, the Culture Secretary, launched an investigation last month over concerns the deal could damage media plurality.

That had been expected given the planned combination of The Telegraph and The Daily Mail under the same owner. However, the growing potential for a Labour lurch to the Left and a brewing press regulation push raised the risk that the deal could be tightly restricted or blocked, and leave Lord Rothermere facing a loss.

A spokesman for DMGT said: “We have worked hard to complete the acquisition of The Telegraph and were confident that the organisation would have thrived under our long-term stewardship.

“We were optimistic about our plans to invest in its exceptional journalism and secure the future of a respected British media brand.”

They added: “We wish every success to Axel Springer and The Telegraph.

“We believe that the protracted and out-of-date regulatory framework guarantees that UK-based national newspaper groups are at a huge competitive disadvantage in any merger process.”

data.title: Britain’s hapless water watchdog is asleep on the job again
data.description:
Just when you thought things couldn’t get any worse for the parched residents of Tunbridge Wells and surrounding towns, here comes Ofwat to the rescue.

With some households in the region facing their second bout of water shortages in the space of a few weeks at the hands of South East Water, some must have been praying for a miracle. Yet the comically late arrival of Britain’s regulator is the equivalent of sending in Dad’s Army.

Responsibility for the fiasco that has unfolded ultimately resides with South East Water’s equally inept, overpaid chief executive Dave Hinton and the board of nodding dog directors who have somehow seen fit to allow him to remain in post.

But for an enforced and humiliating appearance in front of a parliamentary committee of senior MPs, Hinton has been completely missing in action throughout this debacle.

Nor is this catalogue of failures a one-off. Under its current boss, South East Water has a thoroughly woeful track record of failing in its basic duty to keep the taps running for 2.3 million customers in Kent, Sussex, Surrey, Hampshire and Berkshire.

This is the second officially-declared major incident in South East Water territory in as many months, with customers in Tunbridge Wells, Kent still facing disruption after weeks. Thousands of households were also hit by shortages in 2022 and 2023.

As my colleague Patrick Galbraith and I point out in a separate piece for The Telegraph, South East Water is well on its way to claiming the crown of Britain’s worst utility from industry basket case Thames Water, which really does take some doing given its current sorry state.

Mr Hinton should have been shown the door long ago, but he’s being kept in post by the company’s unforgivably meek independent directors, led by equally absentee chairman Chris Train, who knows a thing or two about companies that fall short.

Train is also the chairman of Sellafield, which was fined for serious cybersecurity failings in 2024. The following year, the Commons Public Accounts Committee (Pac) criticised the nuclear power plant for “failure, cost-overruns and continuing safety concerns.”

Yet, the idea that Ofwat’s intervention will make much of a difference really is the stuff of fantasy.

Britain is awash with dire regulators who are meant to protect consumers from cowboy firms. The Financial Conduct Authority was branded “incompetent at best, dishonest at worst”, by MPs and peers in 2024.

Meanwhile, Pac found in 2022 that failures at Ofgem had come “at a considerable cost to billpayers” after the collapse of a staggering 29 energy suppliers in less than 18 months.

The only regulator that has shown some bite is the Competition and Markets Authority. And its reward for standing up for consumers? A thorough defanging at the hands of ministers who claimed it had become an impediment to economic growth.

Otherwise, in a crowded field, Ofwat must be a contender for the most ineffective industry policeman of all time. It is a watchdog that has repeatedly demonstrated little, if any, willingness to properly hold the industry to account, as South East knows only too well from considerable experience.

The vast bulk of Britain’s waterways have become a public health emergency, and water shortages are now commonplace – the result of extreme underinvestment in the country’s decrepit pipes and sewers by an industry that has routinely prioritised generous executive pay and investor dividends.

On every conceivable measure and metric, companies have been failing consumers on a grand scale for years.

Ultimately, the buck stops with greedy, irresponsible suppliers and shareholders. Yet, years of regulatory apathy have allowed something as basic and fundamental as the provision of drinking water to be turned into a giant and lucrative experiment in financial engineering at the expense of customers.

On the occasions when Ofwat does act, it’s like watching a snail wake up from a coma.

South East Water is already the subject of a regulatory investigation – one dating back to 2023 that still hasn’t been resolved – so what chance of a quick verdict?

As Mike Martin, the MP for Tunbridge Wells, points out: “It’s obvious that South East Water has breached the terms of the license.”

In the face of Ofwat’s impotence, is it any wonder the company has been getting away with it for ages?

Four major outages have been racked up on Hinton’s watch, and South East Water has ranked in the bottom three companies for disruptions every year since he took over.

2023 was particularly shocking, with the company recording the most interruptions and outages of any supplier in England and Wales.

Meanwhile, South East has failed to meet more than 40pc of its targets every year for the last three years. Last year, just 13 of its 38 annual targets were hit. Unsurprisingly, Ofwat penalties of a few million pounds here and there have had little effect.

Financially, it is a mess, too. Total losses since 2022 stand at £130m, the result of profits being consistently wiped out by the cost of servicing £1.3bn of borrowings. 

At times, debt interest and dividends have eclipsed the money that has been spent mending leaky pipes and other vital infrastructure.

Hinton, meanwhile, has earned nearly £2.3m since taking charge in 2020 and is in line for a 30pc pay rise, plus a £400,000 long-term “service bonus” regardless of performance. Talk about reward for failure.

It is an untenable situation that has long been crying out for a supervisor with real mettle.

Lynn Parker, senior director for enforcement at Ofwat, is right to say that “the last six weeks have been miserable for businesses and households across Kent and Sussex” – but why did it take so long for Ofwat to do anything?

Even now, it’s hard to avoid the conclusion that Ofwat only acted because Emma Reynolds, the Environment Secretary, had called publicly for it to step in.

While the regulator has dithered, senior MPs, together with Martin, have done a brilliant job of holding Hinton and colleagues to account.

Ofwat, on the other hand, is hard to take seriously. The threat of penalties, including the annulment of South East’s licence, is somewhat hollow to say the least, given the Government’s plans to abolish Ofwat, the Drinking Water Inspectorate and the Environment Agency, and replace it with a single water regulator responsible for the entire water system.

It is a welcomed move. Long-suffering customers deserve better and the disbanding of the current abject regime is a rare opportunity to create a watchdog with genuine clout. Labour cannot afford to get it wrong.

data.title: Sir David Kirch, reclusive collector of rare banknotes who gave £100 million to the elderly of Jersey
data.description:
​Sir David Kirch, who has died 89, was a London property speculator who assembled what was thought to be Britain’s largest collection of provincial banknotes and the world’s most extensive personal collection of airship memorabilia; but he sold it all in the process of becoming one of the nation’s most generous philanthropists.

In the 1960s the reclusive Kirch had amassed a property empire that at one point included swathes of Kensington and Earl’s Court, as well as the Shaftesbury Theatre in the West End – which he threatened to demolish. Tall and elegant, he was viewed as an enigma. “Often at weekends, he takes his mother down to his cottage at Shoreham-on-Sea, otherwise he sits alone in his 15-room mansion in Hyde Park Gate and watches colour TV after eating his solitary dinner served by his Spanish butler,” reported the Evening Standard. In 1973 he settled as a tax exile in Jersey.

On reaching “three score and ten” in 2006, he decided to mark the milestone by distributing £100 Co-op vouchers at Christmas to every islander aged 70 and over – a commitment he honoured even on discovering that there would be 9,000 recipients. “I am fond of old people because I’m old myself,” he explained. He was delighted to learn that one pensioner had been able to buy a microwave oven, and called it “Mr Kirch”, so that “every time it pinged they thought of me”.

He repeated the scheme in subsequent years – later upping the voucher to £110 – and established a charitable trust to provide homes for pensioners on Jersey. In 2012, on being diagnosed with cancer, he announced that he would leave £100 million, the bulk of his fortune, to the elderly of Jersey.

Observing that his two happiest encounters with money were, first, in the making of it, and second, in the giving away, he proceeded to liquidate his assets to fund his trust. He sold his Rolls-Royce in favour of a Mini, and auctioned his collection of nearly 4,000 English provincial banknotes issued by defunct 19th-century institutions such as Scarborough Old Bank, Glastonbury & Shepton Mallet Bank and many others, their £5 and £10 denominations often fetching four-figure prices. He also sold a more unusual collection of “skit notes” – historic joke banknotes with political or satirical themes, issued by entities such as “the Bank of Love”.

His 15-ton collection of airship relics, sold in 2012, included medals awarded to the crew of the R101 which crashed in France in 1930 and a singed beer bottle salvaged from the wreckage of the Hindenburg in 1937.

David Roderick Kirch was born in Wimbledon on July 23 1936, the son of Leonard Kirch and his wife Margaret. His father had done well as a wholesale meat trader, but chose to keep travelling by train at six o’clock to Smithfield because, if the other traders saw his chauffeured Rolls-Royce, “he’d never get the price he wanted”.

At Tonbridge School, David demonstrated a flair for selling stamps. His commanding officer on National Service agreed that he would make a better businessman than a soldier, and since “I wasn’t clever enough to go to university,” as Kirch recalled, he found a job in the Lloyd’s insurance market.

On the untimely death of his father, aged 59, Kirch inherited £5,000 and launched himself in the meat trade. When his firm was taken over by a Dutch company, who wanted his Kingston cold store rather than his business, he realised that property was a better bet than meat, and bought a stake in a Hampshire estate agency.

He joined forces with his brother Peter in 1962, and they borrowed heavily to buy up chunks of west London, specialising in properties with sitting tenants that could be converted to more lucrative short-term lets. One flat in Courtfield Road was rented to the Rolling Stones; the police stripped off the oak panelling in the drawing room during a drugs bust.

Their first big gamble, as Kirch told an interviewer, was to buy 23 houses in Earl’s Court for more than £250,000 – 90 per cent of it borrowed – though they were producing rental income of only £1,000 a year. “Once you had dealt with sums of a quarter of a million,” he observed, “things do become much easier.”

In the early 1970s the Kirch brothers bought into a quoted company, Peureula (Sumatra) Rubber Estates, which they renamed Peureula Investments and through which they amassed unpayable debts of some £35 million – chiefly to Keyser Ullman, one of the fringe banks heaviest hit by the property crash of 1973. “They can only ever hope to get their money back,” Kirch said in 1976, “which they won’t.”

Having lost nearly everything, David Kirch later rebuilt his portfolio to a value at one point estimated at £250 million.

His enthusiasms as a collector ranged from glass eyebaths to memorabilia from the Crystal Palace (which he dreamed of rebuilding) and a bench that once belonged to Lord Lucan. One particular indulgence was cars; another was gambling. At the Casino de la Fôret at Le Touquet, Ian Fleming challenged him to a race back to their hotel in their sports cars.

Being a covert homosexual, Kirch recalled, was another form of gambling: “Sex was that much more exciting when it was illegal.” He regretted never having children, and observed that his life had spanned an era when his friend, Lord Montagu, could be imprisoned for homosexuality, to the legalisation of gay marriage and adoption by same-sex couples.

In 2013 David Kirch was appointed KBE.

His long-term partner Serge Cuhat died in 2009 in an Alpine ballooning accident.

Sir David Kirch, born July 23 1936, died February 28 2026​

data.title: Hungary ‘kidnapped Ukrainian bankers carrying £60m and nine kilos of gold’
data.description:
Hungary has been accused of kidnapping seven Ukrainian bank employees transporting £60m in cash and nine kilograms of gold.

Ukraine says that Viktor Orbán’s government has carried out “state terrorism” as tensions between Kyiv and Budapest reach a boiling point.

The State Savings Bank of Ukraine, Oschadbank, said its staff were transporting banknotes in Euro and US dollar denominations as well as gold bars from Austria to Ukraine when they were detained.

Hungary’s tax authority says it is ‌pursuing ‌criminal ⁠proceedings on suspicion of money laundering.

One of the ​Ukrainian nationals is believed to be a ‌former general ​of the ⁠Ukrainian intelligence services.

“This is state terrorism and racketeering,” said Andrii Sybiha, the Ukrainian foreign minister. “We are talking about Hungary taking hostages and stealing money.”

Now, Ukraine has urged its citizens to avoid travel to Hungary owing to “the inability to guarantee their safety amid the arbitrary actions of the Hungarian authorities”, the foreign ministry said.

Oschadbank said that the location of its employees was unknown, but that GPS signal data indicated their vehicles were in central Budapest “near one of the law enforcement agencies of Hungary”.

“The National Tax and Customs Administration [NAV] is conducting criminal proceedings on suspicion of money laundering: on March 5, it took into custody seven Ukrainian citizens, including a former Ukrainian secret service general, as well as two armoured cash transport vehicles,” the tax authority said in a statement, according to national news agency MTI.

“This year alone, more than $900m [£675m], €420m [£365m] and 146 kilograms of gold bars were transported to Ukraine through the territory of Hungary,” the statement added.

On Thursday, the Hungarian prime minister had threatened to resort to “force” in an escalating standoff.

Kyiv and Budapest have been embroiled in a feud since oil flows through the Druzhba pipeline that transports Russian crude to Central Europe, were suspended on Jan 27 by what Kyiv said was a Russian strike on pumping facilities in western Ukraine.

Hungary and Slovakia, widely seen as Russia’s closest partners in the EU, have accused Volodymyr Zelensky, the Ukrainian president, of imposing an “oil blockade” by deliberately refusing to repair the pipeline, which has been the main route for delivering Russian oil to their countries since the 1960s.

Both sides have resorted to threats, with Budapest saying it will block a planned €90bn (£78bn) EU loan for underfunded Ukraine until it resumes shipments via the pipeline.

Mr Zelensky countered on Thursday, saying: “We hope that one person in the EU will not block the €90bn… otherwise, we will simply give the address of that person to our Armed Forces; our guys can call him and speak to him in their own language.”

On Friday Mr Orbán said Hungary would also block transit shipments to Ukraine until the issue was resolved.

“We have stopped diesel exports to Ukraine,” he told state radio. “We will maintain power exports and we will stop transit shipments going through Hungary that are important for Ukraine… until we get Ukraine’s approval for the oil shipments.”

Hungary will hold parliamentary elections on April 12, with Mr Orbán trailing behind Peter Magyar, the leader of the pro-EU Tisza Party, in the polls.

The Hungarian incumbent has made the war in Ukraine a central pillar of his campaign, portraying the fighting as a drain on his country’s finances and saying his political opponents are colluding with Kyiv and Brussels.

Mr Magyar, who has signalled that he could move to reduce Hungary’s dependence on Russian oil, condemned Mr Zelensky for “threatening” Mr Orbán with his remarks about the Ukrainian army.

“No foreign state leader can threaten anyone, not a single Hungarian,” he said, calling on the Ukrainian president to “withdraw” his comments.

data.title: Taylor Swift helps Croydon shopping centre shake off the bad times
data.description:
The Taylor Swift effect is usually measured in the billions her tours pump into national economies.

But in Croydon, it appears that so-called Swiftonomics has worked on a much smaller scale too.

Store owners at the Whitgift Centre, a down-at-heel shopping mall built in the late 1960s, say they are hopeful for a boom thanks to a star turn in Swift’s music video Opalite.

The global pop singer, whose Eras tour generated an estimated £1bn for the UK economy, descended on the south London mall to film part of a retro video for her latest single.

The six-minute film, released in February and with more than 15 million views on YouTube, features a scene in which Swift and Irish actor Domhnall Gleeson descend the impressive double escalator while holding pretzels on a date.

They walk on the distinctive red and white checked floor where they meet talk show host Graham Norton, who plays a ‘magical cleaning product’ salesman.

Whitgift store owners told The Telegraph that the centre was “not in a good state” and that most shop fronts sit empty, with more than 70 vacant lots.

However, its moment in the spotlight may change this, as fans have already begun flocking to the venue.

Kate Sims, who works in a health store in the building, told The Wall Street Journal: “People have come in the shop whispering, ‘Did you hear Taylor Swift filmed here?’”

Irina, an employee at heritage British fashion company Hawes & Curtis, also confirmed that she could overhear “customers sometimes talking about it” now.

For Croydon residents, Swift’s feature has also proved an exciting – and surprising – development.

“I’m not sure why Taylor picked such a rundown place to film, but I’m gassed about it,” Poppy Hogg, 24, said.

The Swift fan told the US newspaper that she “lost her head” because she was so happy about it and added that she hurried to visit the venue in the days after the video was released.

One such fan included Thomas Duke, who runs an Instagram account with more than a million followers titled Stepping Through Film.

He visited the centre and took a snapshot of the escalator with a picture of Swift and Gleeson marking the spot.

In a post liked by almost 40,000 people, he wrote: “Taylor Swift in Croydon, the Whitgift Centre is basically famous now.

“I visited the shopping centre where the ‘Opalite’ music video was filmed – it was so cool to see!”

The film enthusiast said: “The centre in Croydon was transformed into an Opalite paradise as Clarks (the shoe shop) became the spot where Graham Norton was selling spray bottles, the escalators became the spot where hands were held, and the whole centre became a 1990s haven.”

“It was strange visiting as most of the shops are all closed up, but the magic still lives on with some TS imagination,” he added.

Lauren Furey, publicity manager for Croydon’s independent David Lean Cinema, told the BBC the fact that the centre was partly closed made it “more cost-effective when you don’t have to navigate around open businesses”.

She quipped: “I think we can thank Taylor Swift for the reason that [Whitgift] escalator is working again.”

Rowenna Davis, the Labour candidate for mayor of Croydon, also jumped on the bandwagon.

In a video of herself on the same escalator, she thanked Swift for “bringing a bit of sparkle” back to the centre, adding that if she were elected mayor she would “want our town centre to have that sparkle all the time”.

Stephen Boros, who runs the family luggage business in the centre, said the mall usually experienced “really poor footfall” and had been “looking for regeneration for many years”.

“It’s been a long time coming,” he said of the previously floated plans for an extensive redevelopment.

data.title: Bank of England investigates collapse of £2bn shadow bank
data.description:
The Bank of England is investigating the collapse of a £2bn British “shadow bank” as fears about the sector rip through stock markets.

Officials at the Prudential Regulation Authority (PRA) have requested information from lenders including Barclays that backed Market Financial Solutions (MFS), a private credit provider that was placed into administration amid allegations of fraud last week.

PRA, a division of the central bank that regulates lenders, is reportedly concerned about insufficient risk assessment and due diligence by banks of MFS and its related companies, according to the Financial Times, which first reported the news.

The PRA is also investigating whether banks have indirect exposure through lending to private capital groups that may have backed MFS independently.

A Bank of England spokesman said: “We are constantly monitoring the financial system and wider markets and stay in close contact with firms. It is the responsibility of firms to manage the risks to which they are exposed.”

The enquiries come amid a broader upheaval in the private credit sector. Shares in BlackRock tumbled by 6pc in the US on Friday after the asset manager limited withdrawals from one of its flagship private credit funds for the first time.

Blackrock’s HPS Corporate Lending Fund, which is marketed to individuals, said it would not offer redemptions above the traditional 5pc limit each quarter, despite receiving requests to redeem 9.3pc of shares in the most recent quarter.

Private credit funds, often called “shadow banks”, extend loans but do not take deposits and so are not subject to the same kind of strict regulations as banks. The sector has exploded in size since the financial crisis as banks have faced stricter regulation.

However, there are concerns about loose lending standards and the opacity of the sector. Several Wall Street leaders have expressed worries about private credit, with Lloyd Blankfein, the former boss of Goldman Sachs, saying he could “smell” a crash on the horizon and Jamie Dimon, the chief executive of JP Morgan, warning of “cockroaches” in the sector.

MFS described itself as a specialist provider of buy-to-let mortgage lending and bridging finance. It was part of a fast-growing crop of so-called bridging lenders in the UK. These firms provide short-term, property-backed loans to borrowers who may not qualify for traditional bank financing and often charge higher interest rates.

A number of large banks gave capital to MFS to lend, including Santander, Jefferies and Barclays, which has between £500m and £600m tied up in the firm.

Private capital firms including Apollo’s Atlas SP Partners and Castlelake also extended financing to the failed mortgage lender. Some of these firms may have used leverage from banks to make these loans in the first place.

Last week, a court placed MFS into administration amid accusations of fraud. It has been estimated that there is a shortfall on its balance sheet of as much as £930m.

Investors reportedly first noticed financial irregularities in November. Barclays, which also provided banking services to MFS, froze the bridge loan provider’s accounts in January.

Last week, a court placed MFS into administration amid accusations of fraud. It has been estimated that there is a shortfall on its balance sheet of as much as £930m.

Investors reportedly first noticed financial irregularities in November. Barclays, which also provided banking services to MFS, froze the bridge loan provider’s accounts in January.

data.title: Soaring household energy debts ‘to hit £7bn by the end of 2026’
data.description:
Britain’s household energy debts will rise from £5.5bn to hit £7bn by the end of the year, a new report has warned.

The increase suggests that at least two million households will struggle to keep up with their gas and electric bills by December 2026, according to a study commissioned by trade body Energy UK.

Persistently high energy bills are largely to blame for the increase in debts, analysts said, although there had also been an uptick in people choosing not to pay.

The increase is problematic not only for those indebted to energy suppliers, but also for other households who have to pay an average surcharge of £50 a year to cover the missing money.

“The household energy debt crisis is spiralling out of control,” the study says. “In the last three years alone, total energy debt and arrears have more than doubled.

“Official figures [from regulator Ofgem] suggest the total now stands at £4.5bn [but] Energy UK’s analysis shows that the true figure is already around £5.5bn.

“Without urgent intervention, the total amount of debt and arrears owed is likely to hit £7bn by the end of the year. This would require a further £10-£15 per year to be added to energy bills.”

Ofgem revealed on Wednesday that annual energy bills will fall by £117 from April as the price cap – the average amount suppliers are allowed to charge their domestic dual-fuel customers – is set at £1,641.

However, bills are still much higher than before the energy crisis, when the price cap was at £1,277.

Energy UK’s report comes amid growing industry concern over rising energy debts and suppliers’ inability to tackle the problem.

Some analysts argue that the surge in debt is partially because of regulations brought in after the British Gas prepayment meter scandal in 2023.

An investigation revealed that debt collection agents working for British Gas were forcibly entering the homes of vulnerable customers to install “pay-as-you-go” prepayment meters, even when those customers had clear medical or financial needs.

Once installed, such meters automatically disconnect energy supplies unless they are topped up. However, the backlash led to such measures being banned.

Britons ‘cannot afford their bills’

Energy UK’s report said: “The reason for such a dramatic increase in energy debt and arrears is that a series of regulatory actions have made it much easier to get into debt, and harder to get out of debt.

“It is striking that no other utility has seen similar increases in debt and arrears.”

Energy UK also highlighted how there were almost one million households for which energy suppliers have no details, making it “extremely difficult to recover unpaid bills in this scenario”.

An Ofgem spokesman acknowledged the problem: “The current levels of energy debt are unsustainable, and this is a challenge that requires action from everyone – the regulator, government, and industry alike.”

Alex Belsham-Harris, head of energy policy at Citizens Advice, added: “We estimate that nearly seven million people are living in households in debt to their supplier.

“Every day our advisers help people who are making impossible decisions between keeping their homes warm to a safe or comfortable level or putting food on the table.”

Simon Francis, coordinator of the End Fuel Poverty Coalition, said: “Energy debt has risen for one simple reason: energy bills have remained far higher than household incomes can sustain.

“This is not a story of widespread ‘on’t pay’ behaviour, it is overwhelmingly about people who simply cannot afford their bills.”

Bill Bullen, chief executive at Utilita Energy, which specialises in supplying energy to lower-income households, said that rising debts would inevitably lead to higher bills for everyone else.

A government spokesman said: “We are working urgently with Ofgem to drive debt out of the energy system and are also delivering reforms that put people first.”
