data.title: Even auto giants know it: the electric car boom is out of charge
data.description:
“I think the customer has spoken. That’s the punchline,” said Jim Farley, the chief executive of Ford.

The American boss was speaking last week as his company unveiled a $5bn (£3.7bn) annual loss, barely two months after it had booked a shock $19.5bn write-down.

The cause? An aggressive bet on electric vehicles (EVs) that backfired spectacularly.

In 2025, sales of the Mustang Mach-E crossover and the F-150 Lightning pickup truck – once hailed by Farley as the “truck of the future” – went into reverse.

Worse, the electric Model-E division has booked losses of more than $13bn since 2023.

Now it has consigned the F-150 to the dustbin and has scrapped much of its future EV plans, with the company set to put a greater emphasis on hybrids.

Ford isn’t the only automotive giant counting the costs of a failed bet on electric.

In the past year, the world’s biggest carmakers have written off more than $60bn from their balance sheets as they retreat from an EV boom that never was.

The figure includes a €22bn (£19bn) charge reported by Vauxhall owner Stellantis, along with a $7.6bn hit to General Motors, €5.1bn at Volkswagen Group, $4.5bn at Honda and $1.2bn at Volvo, among others.

“Most of the Western carmakers are now facing big issues,” says Felipe Muñoz, of Car Industry Analysis.

Broadly speaking, EV sales are growing. But a rapid shift to electric that both carmakers and politicians had hoped for has failed to materialise. “Many drivers are still not comfortable making the shift,” says Muñoz.

At the same time, net zero regulations are beginning to bite in the UK and Europe – with companies facing fines if they cannot meet increasingly stretching targets – just as low-price competitors from China have arrived to undercut them.

Many of those Chinese manufacturers are themselves trying to outrun a crisis, as sales at home grind to a halt.

All across the world, a grim reality for carmakers is setting in: drivers simply don’t want EVs in the volume they hoped.

“The plans they set out were too ambitious – and what we’re seeing now is the reality,” says Munoz.

Covid-era subsidies

Much of today’s mess can be traced back to the heady days of the pandemic, when strange things were happening in the car market.

As the spreading coronavirus shut down factories around the world and governments panicked, subsidies were lavished on electric cars to try to support the flagging automotive industry.

This also came as central banks across Europe were slashing interest rates to boost their flagging economies, which made borrowing cheaper.

At one stage, it was so cheap to buy an electric car in Germany that models such as the Renault Zoe or even the Mercedes-Benz EQC crossover could be leased for less than the price of a mobile phone contract.

At the same time, governments were strengthening net zero policies. In Britain, the then prime minister Boris Johnson announced a ban on the sale of new petrol cars by 2030; in America, Joe Biden proposed a $174bn stimulus package for EVs.

Tesla, the electric carmaker founded by Elon Musk, was also being eyed enviously by its more traditional rivals – as sales of its Model 3 and Model Y cars grew rapidly.

EV sales were being supported by a sharp rise in petrol prices that followed the outbreak of the Ukraine war, which made EVs look cheaper relative to internal combustion engine (ICE) cars.

Against this backdrop, things only seemed to be heading in one direction.

In response, many car companies announced ambitious plans to electrify their line-ups.

Jaguar Land Rover announced that Jaguar would become an “all-electric” brand by 2025. Ford pledged to completely electrify by 2030, while VW doubled its end-of-decade target for EV sales from 35pc to 70pc.

But in the years since those announcements, EV sales have slowed as many of the temporary factors that boosted the market have receded.

Petrol prices have fallen from their peaks, interest rates have climbed and governments have reduced grants available to drivers.

In the US, meanwhile, Donald Trump has removed the generous Biden-era subsidies, dismissing the green energy transition is a “scam”.

It has forced manufacturers to walk back or delay most of their pledges – and to announce costly write-downs.

Ben Nelmes, of the New Automotive consultancy, says: “One of the big things that drives car companies is what all their competitors are doing.

“So when Tesla was really taking off in those years, there was this big rush to try and recreate its success.

“But a lot of those targets and [government] regulations have since been either watered down or rolled back.

“The car industry was also really hoping that sales volumes globally would go back to where they were pre-pandemic – and in most cases, that just hasn’t really happened.”

EVs are not ‘good value for money’

Perhaps the biggest reason sales of EVs haven’t been as strong as hoped is price.

In Europe, the EV cost nearly €63,000 in 2024 compared with around €51,000 for an internal combustion engine car, according to analysis by Jato Dynamics. This is despite predictions that falling battery prices would lead to similar price drops for cars.

As a result, European sales of EVs have so far struggled to break past the 20pc mark.

By comparison, EVs in China have reached parity in cost with ICE cars – at about €22,000 on average – and now account for around 50pc of all new car sales. This has been achieved in no small part thanks to generous subsidies from Beijing.

Andy Palmer, a veteran car industry executive known as the “Godfather” of EVs for his role in developing Nissan’s popular Leaf model, blames Western manufacturers for failing to invest in affordable models.

In the US and Europe, too many brands have focused on pricier – and more profitable – SUV models rather than cheaper city cars, he says.

“I think the fundamental issue is that the cars are too expensive and they are being compensated by subsidies, and that ultimately doesn’t work,” he says.

“You’ve got to get parity of cost with ICE cars. And EVs have got to offer more than simply they’re clean.

“They’ve got to offer basically good value for money, they’ve got to offer something different in terms of the user experience, rather than simply being a copy of ICE cars.

“That’s where the Chinese have got it right. The Chinese have pitched their EV prices at parity, if not cheaper, and they are adopting at rates that give huge economies of scale.”

With EV sales failing to take off in the way that was hoped, even the watered-down electric car mandates in Europe are starting to look challenging for manufacturers.

In the UK, for example, the zero-emission vehicle (ZEV) mandate required 28pc of new car sales to be electric last year compared with the 23.9pc actually achieved by the industry.

The target rises again to 33pc this year and gradually ratchets up to 80pc by 2030, when sales of new pure petrol and diesel car sales will be banned outright. By 2035, hybrids are likely to go the same way.

Car companies, led by the Society for Motor Manufacturers and Traders (SMMT), are urging a rethink, arguing that they are having to withdraw petrol cars from sale and discount their EVs heavily just to avoid punishing fines already.

Xavier Martinet, the president of Hyundai’s European business, recently said the Government needed a “reality check”, or risk some carmakers pulling out of the UK altogether.

“If you don’t sell EVs, you might not even do the ICE sales,” he told The Telegraph this month. “So what will customers buy, at the end of the day? That’s a major question.”

Range anxiety

In the UK, there’s another barrier to adoption: infrastructure.

For many drivers, range anxiety remains a worry despite the growing mileage of newer electric cars. This is why the Government has set out targets to boost the number of chargers nationally to more than 300,000 by 2030.

But the charger rollout has been criticised repeatedly for being patchy and too focused on London and the South East, with a House of Lords report in 2024 warning that ministers needed to “turbocharge” the rollout.

Instead, charging companies complain that they have still faced a menagerie of problems that were avoidable and are slowing down the rollout.

Securing planning permission on many sites remains too time-consuming, according to industry body ChargeUK, while funding through local authority-run schemes has been hit by delays.

Most significantly, charging providers have been hit by by high network costs. These are fees charged to businesses to fund the maintenance and expansion of the electricity grid – and they have been rising rapidly in recent years.

The network charges depend on the capacity of a site, or how many chargers it has, meaning that companies are effectively punished for doing what the Government wants and building bigger charging stations.

In one case, a national provider told The Telegraph that its network charges for a single site had rocketed from about £80 a year in 2020 to nearly £40,000 per year today.

This is pushing up the cost of public charging, which is already far more expensive than home charging – increasing the “driveway divide” between households who have their own charger and those that don’t.

According to Zapmap, the cost of charging at home is as little as 8.5 pence per kilowatt hour compared with 54 pence at public chargers.

Some charging companies are responding by cutting investment or building batteries on their sites to reduce reliance on the grid. The Telegraph revealed that the annual number of charger installations fell for the first time in 2025.

Signs of financial pressure are also emerging.

A string of deals was announced last week to combine various EV charging providers, including a merger of Mer with Octopus Energy-backed Be.EV.

That follows takeovers of Trojan Energy and SureCharge by larger providers Connected Kerb and Shell-Unitricity respectively. Industry executives think more such deals are on the way.

The strain on the sector is why charging companies have been urging ministers not to relax the ZEV mandate – even as car manufacturers say the targets are punishing high.

Jarrod Birch, of industry body ChargeUK, says: “Charging businesses have committed £6bn in private investment underpinned by the certainty and confidence offered by the ZEV mandate.

“Weakening or delaying the mandate – reviewed and adjusted less than 12 months ago – would undermine that confidence and the UK’s general ability to attract investment and deliver growth.

“Investors need to know that binding targets will remain binding, not shift in the wind.”

Chinese carmakers race to dominance

All these headwinds are bad enough on their own. But what could have been a significant headache for Western carmakers is fast becoming a full-blown crisis as new Chinese competitors are rapidly expanding in the market.

Brands such as BYD, SAIC, Chery, Geely, XPeng, NIO and Leapmotor have had their research, development and production backed by the state – allowing them to take greater risks and put their foot on the accelerator.

Beijing has also supported a rapid rollout of charging infrastructure, and until recently was lavishing subsidies on consumers at home to buy electric cars.

From an almost standing start, the Chinese carmakers have raced to dominance.

In 2020, Chinese drivers bought 900,000 battery-electric cars and 200,000 plug-in hybrids. In 2024, sales were 6.4 million and 4.9 million, respectively.

EVs now account for more than half of all car sales, and almost every EV sold is a Chinese brand.

Western carmakers haven’t been able to keep up in this race, and it is starting to show. Geely last year became the second Chinese carmaker after BYD to overtake Volkswagen in the Chinese market.

The German carmaker’s joint ventures with state-owned FAW and SAIC had a combined market share of 11pc last year, about half its former peak.

As Chinese buyers become more patriotic – Porsche sales have nosedived there – VW will work with its partner to make China-friendly models.

The likes of General Motors and Toyota are also losing ground in the world’s biggest market. They are either failing to launch EVs quickly enough, or not pricing them cheaply enough.

Boom years are over

It is not just Western brands hurting in China, however. The domestic boom has started to turn sour for local manufacturers too.

Subsidies ensure that the factory lines keep churning, but the queue of buyers is getting shorter. The market’s low-hanging fruit has been picked, just as weak labour and property markets have sapped consumer confidence.

With a glut of unsold cars and a dearth of buyers, the industry has resorted to steep discounting to keep shifting stock.

The China Automobile Dealers Association estimates that this price war wiped more than 471bn yuan (£50bn) from the carmakers’ revenue in the past three years.

The strategy is barely working: BYD’s sales in January were half those a year ago, and previous high-flyer Xpeng reported a third decline.

In February, investors began shedding their holdings in the listed companies, fearing the boom years might be over.

Beijing sat up and took notice. For more than a year, the Chinese authorities used public statements and meetings with executives to criticise the carmakers for their “race to the bottom”, known in China as “involution”.

But their words went unheeded, and last week the state administration for market regulation banned the industry from selling vehicles below production cost.

Cosimo Ries, of analysis firm Trivium China, isn’t convinced that even this will stop the price war. And as losses mount, he says, the smaller players will fight back, and they’ll be backed by local and regional governments who don’t want to see their champion fail.

He Xiaopeng, the boss of XPeng, agrees: “The competition in the auto market in 2026 will be even more brutal and bloody,” he said recently.

The Chinese carmakers’ domestic struggles are not good news for their Western rivals.

With unsold stocks piling up, BYD and its brethren are turning to a familiar option for glut-stricken Chinese firms: pump and dump offshore.

BYD said last week it was planning to boost exports by 25pc this year. In January alone, BYD’s German sales surged more than tenfold, with Dolphin hatchbacks outselling Teslas by two to one.

Britain is fertile ground, as it hasn’t thrown up a tariff wall. BYD quadrupled its sales last year and entered the top 20 brands last year, outselling Mini, Mazda and Tesla.

Greece and Italy are also proving to be attractive markets. One in every seven EVs sold in Europe last year was a Chinese import, when cars made in China by Western companies are included.

“The speed at which the European consumer has adopted Chinese cars is much faster than our adoption of Japanese or Korean cars. That is a bit worrying from the perspective of a Western automotive company,” says Niklas Brundin, a partner at consulting firm Arthur D Little.

One industry veteran says that since the UK and Europe are mature car markets, companies tend to grow only by taking market share from somebody else.

“A few years ago there were about 40 major car brands in the UK, and by the end of the decade there’ll be something like 75. There isn’t enough growth in the market to support that,” he says.

“Those extra 35 brands are going to have to take the share from somebody, and that’s going to result in a lot higher competitive intensity. And none of the Chinese companies are coming here to be a player with a 1pc market share.”

In other words, a fight to the death has begun.

Europe fights back

Faced with an onslaught from agile, cutting-edge Chinese companies backed by state subsidies, the initial response from Europe has been to shelter behind a Brussels tariff wall. But its defences look weak.

Chinese companies have responded by switching to selling more hybrid vehicles and setting up factories within Europe.

Matthias Schmidt, an automotive expert, reckons a fightback is coming. In the next year, he predicts the incumbent Western brands will start bringing cheaper models to market, using the cheaper lithium iron phosphate battery technology that powers the Chinese brands.

“We’re expecting to see prices for consumers fall and price parity be met,” he says.

Because the EVs will then be more commercially viable, “it will be in the manufacturers’ interests to push those vehicles, not just to meet regulatory guidelines but because eventually they will become more profitable”.

But for this strategy to fly, the EV market has to reawaken. And here, industry reckons the Government must work harder – or at least smarter.

The EU and the British Government are trying to force the industry to go all-electric by the mid-2030s. But it is becoming clear that not enough motorists are on board with EVs to keep this target on track.

Governments have had differing responses to this dawning reality.

The Trump administration has executed a volte-face, forcing American carmakers to wear heavy losses on their EV investments.

Brussels is poised to water down its targets and standards. But a reform package outlined late last year was criticised by industry as complex, incomplete and insufficient – and not enough to fend off the Chinese.

Britain is undertaking a lengthy review of its targets that require carmakers to shift an increasing proportion of their sales to EVs. Often, carmakers can only meet these targets by selling unwanted EVs at a loss.

“The regulatory trajectories are really steep. It’s so much ahead of that underlying demand that it’s putting huge financial pressure on the industry,” says Mike Hawes, the chief executive of the Society of Motor Manufacturers and Traders.

“We’re not denying the aspiration, the ambition – but it’s making sure it’s deliverable in a timescale that’s affordable for consumers and affordable for businesses.”

Rather than make it more affordable, Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor, has announced a new tax of 3p a mile for EVs from 2028, or 1.5p for plug-in hybrid drivers.

On the other side of the ledger, the Government introduced a grant for EV buyers, but only about a quarter of models will qualify.

“For the industry and especially for the consumer, it’s mixed messages,” Hawes says. “At a time when you need to be pulling every lever to try and bolster that consumer demand, instead it’s two steps forward, one step back.”

‘Model T moment’

The EV bubble that inflated in the first half of this decade has well and truly burst. But the technology is not going away.

Even Ford, despite its huge losses, remains committed to electric cars. Jim Farley, the company’s boss, has said its back-to-basics “Universal Electric Vehicle” platform – built by engineers from the ground up – is the most important product he has ever worked on.

It will be the basis of a new effort to crack the mass market, with Ford reportedly aiming for cars that can sell in the consumer sweet spot of $30,000 to $35,000.

Andrew Bergbaum, an automotive expert at AlixPartners, says this kind of ground-up overhaul is ultimately needed to bring down the cost of Western-made EVs.

Farley has claimed that cracking EVs could deliver a modern “Model T moment” – a reference to the best-selling car that made the company’s name in the early 1900s.

“This is us going back to our roots,” he told Auto Car in January. “As Wolfgang Puck said, ‘A lot of people could run a four or five-star restaurant; not many people can do a good buffet in Las Vegas.’

“We learned so much being an early mover in EVs and a full-line manufacturer. We learned a lot about where we need to put our capital.”

To critics, it looks like an extremely expensive lesson.

data.title: Greggs blames rising minimum wage for £1.35 sausage rolls
data.description:
Greggs has blamed soaring minimum wage and employment taxes for pushing up the price of a sausage roll.

The bakery chain said Labour’s increase in employers’ National Insurance contributions last year was the “main driver” of a 5.5pc increase in its costs in 2025, while a new net zero packaging tax also contributed.

Greggs added that higher employment costs, including increases in the minimum wage, would continue to drive cost inflation in 2026.

The business is slashing costs to try to offset the increases but said it was having to cover some of the pressure “through careful pricing activity”.

The food-to-go retailer raised the price of a sausage roll by 5p to £1.35 and raised coffee prices by 10p in January, after what it called a “very tough” run-up to Christmas.

Higher costs ate into profits last year, with Greggs announcing a 4pc fall in operating profits for 2025 on Tuesday, despite a sales rise of nearly 7pc to £2.15bn last year.

Ahead of Chancellor Rachel Reeves’s Spring Statement, Greggs boss Roisin Currie urged the Government to do more to support business and create jobs.

She said: “My position is always trying to make sure that any decisions the Government makes both look after businesses and help us continue to grow, because that’s what grows the economy.

”The other factor is employment, and just trying to make sure that we find a way to support more young people into the workplace, because that is key to the success of a future growing economy.”

She added that it was a “challenging environment for high-street businesses”.

The company’s share price plummeted 13pc to its lowest level in five years before recovering.

More stores but flat profits

Greggs’s soaring wage bill was compounded by higher production costs and investment in upgrading online systems and building new distribution centres in Derby and Kettering.

Statutory profits before tax plummeted 18pc to £167.4m, which the retailer said was because of a one-off gain from selling a supply chain site the year before.

Greggs added that its 2025 results included a £4.5m hit for a historic VAT understatement the company reported to HMRC.

Analysts warned that the “disappointing” results were further undermined by low consumer confidence and the widespread use of weight-loss jabs.

Demand for Greggs products plummeted during last year’s unusually long and hot summer. The retailer said the “prolonged high temperatures experienced in June and July” had a “material impact on footfall and consumer behaviour.”​

Despite this, Greggs opened 121 net new shops in 2025 and now operates 2,739 stores in Britain.

The company said it would be maintaining its target to open “significantly more than 3,000” UK sites over time, with around 120 net openings planned for 2026 and new “bitesize” formats in small, high-footfall areas.

Ms Currie warned investors that profits for 2026 were likely to be flat compared to last year.

However, she said: “We have a strong pipeline of new shop openings in 2026, primarily in new catchments that drive strong returns, and our investment in supply chain capacity is on track.”

Julie Palmer, of analyst BTG, warned that the market “is likely to remain very difficult for retailers in addition to hospitality companies” because of low consumer confidence.

She said: “Continued inflation increasing the cost of ingredients, energy bills staying sky-high and minimum wage hikes taking effect could see many players forced to push up prices, cut jobs and even close branches.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: France cashes in on Britain’s net zero blitz through £10bn deal
data.description:
To understand why Paris-based utility company Engie surprised the market with a late-night deal to buy one of Britain’s biggest power networks, consider two words: net zero.

Catherine MacGregor, the chief executive of Engie, said in a statement late on Wednesday confirming the £10.5bn takeover of UK Power Networks (UKPN) that the deal would help the French company to become “the best energy transition utility”.

Transition is the industry term for shifting to electric – and it is not hard to see why UKPN appeals.

The company is a crucial part of Britain’s power system that runs the entire electricity distribution network for London and the South East of England.

“We are very excited to be part of this adventure,” MacGregor said on Thursday. She said the goal was “making sure that we can accompany the UK in its ambitions for growth and its clean power 2030 target”.

The responsibility may be huge but so too are the potential profits. UKPN is the monopoly owner of cables carrying power to 19 million people.

Geographically, it serves people ranging from Cromer on Norfolk’s north coast to Brighton on the south coast and everything in between.

Its job is to take electricity from National Grid’s high-voltage lines, reduce the voltage and then feed it into 8.5 million homes and businesses. The average domestic customer had £113 added to their bill for those services last year – an amount set to rise sharply over the next five years of planned upgrades.

UKPN also supplied about 71 terawatt hours of power last year – 28pc of the UK total – via its 45,000km of overhead lines and 147,000km of underground cables.

But it is the numbers on the balance sheet that likely appealed most to Engie.

Electricity distribution networks are geographic monopolies so their charges and profits are overseen by government regulator Ofgem, which is meant to keep them under control.

However, the regulator has come under fire for allowing companies such as UKPN to make what critics claim is excessive profit.

A recent report from consultancy Ibis World suggested that the six companies operating the UK’s 15 regional power distribution networks were set to make profit margins of up to 81pc this year. The industry says it needs large margins because it has to make substantial capital investments.

Still, the sector is certainly doing well.

UKPN’s last published accounts for 2024-25 showed its turnover surged to £2.5bn, up from £1.8bn the year before. It produced earnings before tax and depreciation of £1.8bn while post-tax profits rose from £540m to £853m.

“We have a legally binding commitment [to the UK] to help achieve net zero by 2050 that means heat has got to be electrified and transport needs to be electrified,” says Basil Scarsella, the chief executive of UKPN.

“The forecasts suggest if that happens then peak demand would at least double and if it doubles, we need to reinforce – significantly reinforce – the network to make sure that we don’t get in the way of the transition. We are going to do that.”

As a result, the company is pledging £1.5bn a year of new investment for the next few years, with funds expected to increase after that. The cost of those upgrades will filter through into bills.

Yet, this is not the only reason why the deal has attracted attention.

The sale of UKPN will also see a crucial piece of UK infrastructure fall largely under the control of France with a board dominated by the country’s economics ministry, which holds 40pc of Engie’s voting rights.

It tightens France’s grip on British electricity systems, following years where it has been building up its UK assets.

The same ministry oversees EDF, France’s state-owned energy generator, which runs all of the UK’s nuclear power stations and is currently building two more at Hinkley in Somerset and Sizewell in Suffolk.

Dale Vince, the founder of Ecotricity, says this continues a trend which has been happening for some time.

“We gave up energy sovereignty a long time ago when the Conservatives sold off our networks and supply companies,” he says.

“So the French may have a bigger stake in our networks but National Grid is listed on the New York stock market and our water companies are mostly owned from tax havens. This is the legacy of 1990s Conservative privatisations.

“Failing national infrastructure, starved of investment but pumping out billions in dividends to foreign shareholders and simply not serving our national needs.”

So should Britain be more worried about handing over more of its energy sovereignty to France?

MacGregor says not: “We are in the business of running regulated assets, which are subjected to very focused national regulation. And that’s what we do all the time.”

She says the French government is “very far from having that type of influence”.

She added: “With UKPN, we will be respecting the governance of UKPN as a UK regulated entity but also, of course, doing what Ofgem [the UK regulator] and our different stakeholders expect us to do.”

‘UKPN is no longer owned’ by China

Others claim there is, in fact, reason to welcome Engie’s takeover. Until this week, UKPN was owned by Hong Kong billionaire Sir Li Ka-shing’s CK Infrastructure Holdings – a company with close links to the Chinese government.

That position may have seemed acceptable back in 2010 when CK bought UKPN from EDF – then its owner – but the growing tension in UK-China relationships has since caused increasing concern amongst UK ministers.

Richard Tice, the Reform Party’s energy spokesman, says that the deal is good news for Britain on balance because it removes any Chinese control from the system.

“I’m delighted that UKPN is no longer owned by a Chinese corporation in hock to the Chinese government,” he says.

“I suspect the Chinese can see the writing on the wall. The pressure is growing for their control of key UK assets to come to an end and the French are our friends.”

While many would agree with Tice’s sentiment, Ofgem’s latest annual report into the UK’s power distribution networks may offer a different perspective.

In the regulator’s rankings of the best-performing power networks, UKPN has consistently ranked at or near the top.

Its results last year were so good that Ofgem allowed it a performance bonus of £44m – higher than any other company – including £8.5m for customer satisfaction alone.

Simon Gallagher, a grid expert, says this has not always been the case.

Gallagher, who now acts as managing director of UK Network Services, a company specialising in helping businesses get grid connections, was formerly a senior manager with UKPN under both its previous owners.

“Before EDF’s takeover in 2010, it was one of the worst performing distribution companies for customer services and for power cuts. Under China’s CK Infrastructure, it was transformed into one of the best,” he says.

Still, he says this is “not because of Chinese ownership”.

“It is because the Chinese largely left it alone and because it has a good leadership team. The big question is whether Engie will do the same,” he says.

“History suggests that when specialist companies like UKPN are absorbed into a larger company, they tend to be compromised and performance goes off a cliff.”

It may be a risk but it is one that bosses at Engie have been quick to note.

On Wednesday night, they said they stood ready to continue UKPN’s strong performance.

“We have every intention to make sure UK Power Networks remains a top notch operator here in the UK,” MacGregor said.

If it succeeds, the rewards will be significant.

data.title: Jim Ratcliffe’s chemicals empire suffers debt downgrade
data.description:
Sir Jim Ratcliffe’s debt-laden petrochemicals empire has been put under deepening strain after a downgrade of its credit rating.

The billionaire’s Ineos conglomerate will have to pay higher borrowing costs on its junk-rated debt after it was given a negative outlook by S&P Global, one of the world’s three major credit rating agencies.

S&P handed out B+ and B ratings for two arms of Sir Jim’s sprawling empire, meaning they are viewed as “more vulnerable to adverse business, financial and economic conditions”. S&P had previously given both the divisions BB- ratings.

The ratings agency forecast a drop in Ineos’s earnings, warning of even lower credit ratings to come if the company is unable to get the ratio of its £18bn debt pile below six and a half times the level of its underlying profits.

S&P said Ineos was facing turmoil from “challenging trading conditions” around the world for the petrochemical sector, which is vital for oil and gas production.

“The petrochemical industry is currently grappling with significant overcapacity, primarily driven by aggressive capacity expansions in China outpacing weak global demand,” the rating agency told investors.

The downgrade adds to the mounting problems for the Manchester United co-owner, who has faced criticism this month for his comments on immigration.

Sir Jim, a resident of Monaco and worth an estimated £17bn, said he was sorry for offending “some people” after he said Britain was being “colonised by immigrants”. He had faced pressure to apologise from the Prime Minister and the world of football.

In his chemicals business, the billionaire has blamed mounting losses at his Grangemouth chemical works on government carbon taxes and Britain’s sky-high energy costs.

Accounts published in December for Ineos’s Scottish plant showed it made a pre-tax loss of £431m in 2024, following a £600m loss the year before. It took losses in the last two years to more than £1bn.

Around £5bn of Ineos’s debt burden was trading at, or close to, distressed levels on Thursday.

S&P reduced its forecasts for underlying profits at two subsidiaries of Ineos, adding that it expected any improvements to be driven by savings and lower costs from restructuring the business.

Around £2.5bn of loans issued by Ineos’s US finance vehicle were trading around 82 cents on the dollar on Thursday, while a further £2bn attached to its Quattro division was slightly above 70 cents – the level at which the market believes there is a considerable risk of a default.

S&P said: “Despite projected improvements in 2026, we believe that leverage will remain high, which we now view as commensurate with the current rating.”

The update from S&P marks the latest in a series of downgrades by debt ratings agencies that risk compounding Ineos’s financial strain by making it more expensive to borrow.

Moody’s has lowered its rating on Ineos twice since September. The most recent downgrade in December followed publication of the company’s financial results.

Moody’s said there had been “continued and greater than expected deterioration” in Ineos’s operating performance. Revenues declined 20pc and pre-tax earnings plunged 55pc.

Ineos was contacted for comment.

data.title: Rolls boss defends request for taxpayer support after handing investors £9bn
data.description:
Rolls-Royce has defended its request for hundreds of millions of pounds of government support as it prepares to hand shareholders up to £9bn.

Tufan Erginbilgic, the company’s chief executive, said on Thursday that state subsidies were needed to help develop the engineering giant’s new engine for narrow-body planes and would be “enormous for the economy”.

The return of Rolls-Royce to narrow-body planes – the kind of short-haul aircraft used by the likes of budget airline Ryanair – was “the single biggest opportunity for a generation” in the UK, he said.

The move has been backed by Labour in the industrial strategy published last year, although it is not yet clear whether ministers have agreed to give Rolls-Royce taxpayer support.

This uncertainty has coincided with German and US officials attempting to persuade Rolls-Royce to outsource some of the development work to suppliers in their countries.

Mr Erginbilgic – dubbed “Turbo Tufan” in the City after reviving Rolls-Royce’s fortunes – refused to speculate on whether a deal with the UK would be reached and insisted it was “not a competition”.

He also defended the request for investment, arguing it was “not uncommon” in the sector and Rolls-Royce would invest far more itself.

He said: “The reason [narrow-body] is the biggest opportunity out there is because it is a $1.6tn (£1.18tn) market – you can create a big supply chain.

“So it’s not only Rolls-Royce; it’s lots of players. You create up to 40,000 jobs, and 80pc of what we do is actually exported.”

The chief executive added: “So you increase exports, you improve economic growth significantly, and our estimation is actually this programme alone will create a gross value of more than £100bn.

“This is actually very good for the UK in terms of job creation and economic growth, and the company – the country – actually needs it.”

His remarks came as Rolls posted a 40pc rise in profits and announced it would return between £7bn and £9bn to shareholders from 2026 to 2028.

Shares initially jumped more than 8pc in early trading, although this later eased to around 4.9pc.

That followed an announcement that the company had exceeded previous turnaround targets set by Mr Erginbilgic, who took over in 2023 and has since led a significant recovery at the British engineering giant.

Rolls-Royce said profits last year leapt from £2.3bn to £3.4bn, after sales rose from £17.8bn to £20.1bn.

The company, which in aviation makes money from the number of “flying hours” its engines operate, is benefiting from the recovery of air travel as well as other major trends such as Western rearmament and the boom in demand for data centres.

Its business spans commercial jet engines, diesel generators and propulsion systems, defence systems (such as fighter jet engines), nuclear submarine reactors and nascent nuclear small modular reactors.

However, Mr Erginbilgic’s turnaround – which came after he warned the company was standing on a “burning platform” in the wake of the pandemic – has involved laying off some 2,500 workers and renegotiating loss-making contracts with airlines.

He said: “We are consistently achieving outcomes that were not possible before our transformation.

“With our new capabilities and mindset, we have navigated challenges from supply chain to tariffs and delivered a strong performance in 2025, all while we built the foundations for significant growth for years to come.”

On Mr Erginbilgic’s bid for support, he added: “This is a programme that the industrial strategy of the UK already identified as the most important programme. It is natural that the Government will look to support that.

“Obviously, we are working with the Government for the right support. But one thing I’m going to say, there are all sorts of numbers out there.”

He added: “Whatever they support, we will appreciate that, but it is going to be a fraction of what we are spending on this programme.

“We have already spent more than a billion pounds, and think about the product development phase, think about the industrialisation phase – you know this industry; we are talking about billions and billions.”

The Government has been approached for comment.

data.title: Miliband deal opens door to Chinese investment in UK wind farms
data.description:
Ed Miliband has opened the door to greater investment by Chinese companies in Britain’s energy infrastructure in a deal that critics say threatens national security.

The Energy Secretary on Friday quietly published the memorandum of understanding he struck with Beijing on his visit to China nearly a year ago, following accusations that he was trying to keep the agreement secret.

It commits the UK to closer cooperation with China on offshore wind farms, electricity grids, battery storage, carbon capture and hydrogen, while leaving the door open to potential cooperation on other areas, including civil nuclear power.

In addition to research and policy exchanges, one section makes clear that both countries will “aim to facilitate opportunities for British and Chinese companies to collaborate to create mutual trade and investment opportunities”, including investments in manufacturing and services.

A spokesman for the Department for Energy Security and Net Zero said the document was not “binding” and that all such deals would be subject to rigorous national security checks.

Labour ‘making us dependent on China’

But the Conservatives claimed Labour’s agreement risked making Britain dependent on Chinese green energy technology that could later be “weaponised” by Beijing.

Claire Coutinho, the shadow energy secretary, said: “The release of Ed Miliband’s secret China deal is a victory for transparency and only comes after months of pressure from the Conservatives.

“Miliband has agreed to ‘cooperate’ on areas where China has complete dominance in global supply chains.”

Ms Coutinho added: “He has stripped out previous commitments to support British industry, and, shockingly, didn’t include a single mention of human rights abuses in Xinjiang or Chinese solar panels being made with slave labour.

“Labour’s net zero targets are making us more dependent on China for our energy supply, just as the world is getting more dangerous.

“We cannot allow our security to be undermined because of ideology.”

‘Wacky conspiracy theories’

Mr Miliband’s deal emerged after months of pushback from the Energy Department, which previously insisted that there was no precedent for publishing deals of this kind.

Officials have pointed to the 2015 deal struck by the Conservatives under David Cameron, who had encouraged a “golden era” of relations between the UK and China.

However, that “dialogue” was later suspended under Theresa May amid growing concerns about the security implications of Chinese involvement in critical infrastructure, with ministers later cancelling China’s involvement in Britain’s nuclear programme as well.

Mr Miliband revived energy cooperation talks with China on his visit last year.

But when pressed to publish the agreement, he accused Ms Coutinho of peddling “wacky conspiracy theories” and refused to say why he would not release the full text 17 times when challenged in a select committee hearing.

Mr Miliband’s new commitment to facilitate investment by Chinese companies in Britain is expected to revive scrutiny of proposals by Mingyang, a Chinese wind turbine manufacturer, to build a £1.5bn factory in Scotland.

That plan is controversial because the Ministry of Defence has reportedly warned that the Chinese could use the turbines as spy sensors or use them as an excuse to send engineers to the UK regularly.

Mingyang has denied that it poses a threat, and there is no suggestion of wrongdoing by the company.

The Government is still considering whether to approve the company’s plans.

Deal ‘in UK’s interests’

On Friday, a spokesman for the Department of Energy Security and Net Zero said Mr Miliband had published the deal to address “inaccurate claims and misinformation”.

The spokesman added: “The 2025 agreement provides a platform to share research and ideas between both countries, and, unlike the 2015 agreement, explicitly excludes co-operation on solar.

“As we have always said, it is in the UK’s interest to engage with the world’s largest emitter and one of our biggest trading partners to tackle the climate crisis and protect the British people.”

Last year, a report by the Royal United Services Institute (Rusi) argued that relying on China to hit Britain’s net zero targets would pose a risk to security.

It warned that dangers risked being “hardwired” into major infrastructure, potentially repeating the mistake Europe made by becoming dependent on Russian gas before the Ukraine war.

data.title: Gas price surge threatens to send household bills soaring
data.description:
Gas prices surged 50pc on Monday after Iran stepped up attacks across the Middle East in a move that risks unleashing a fresh wave of price rises and higher bills.

Analysts warned that household energy bills could jump to £2,500 if the conflict causes prolonged disruption to global oil and gas supplies, while drivers face the prospect of paying an extra £10 for a full tank of fuel.

It came as a senior Bank of England official admitted that policymakers could not protect the country from energy price shocks triggered by strikes on Iran.

Alan Taylor, who helps to set interest rates, said the Bank’s measures to influence inflation were too slow-acting to prevent surging oil and gas prices feeding through into higher bills for households and businesses.

European wholesale gas prices jumped by 52pc on Monday – the steepest jump since March 2022 – after state-run energy giant QatarEnergy ceased production following strikes on a processing base.

Oil prices also continued to surge after Iran struck Ras Tanura in Saudi Arabia, the world’s biggest oil export terminal, in response to ongoing attacks by the US and Israel that killed Ali Khamenei, Iran’s supreme leader.

Prices rose 8pc to approach $79 a barrel, the highest level since January last year.

Rachel Reeves will attempt to deliver an upbeat assessment of the economy on Tuesday in the Spring Statement even as her tax and spending watchdog warns that unemployment is on course to rise to its highest level in a decade.

Campaign groups are piling pressure on the Chancellor to announce support for households and businesses in the face of surging oil and gas prices, calling for Ms Reeves to scrap plans to raise fuel duty.

The Chancellor is hoping that falling inflation will pave the way for more Bank of England interest rate cuts.

However, the threat of inflation prompted City traders to slash bets on rate cuts. Investors are now pricing in just one this year, down from two last week.

Global stock markets fell, with the Britain’s benchmark FTSE 100 down more than 1pc and Germany’s Dax shedding 2.6pc.
Fears that higher energy prices could reignite inflation also triggered a rise in the cost of government borrowing, in a move that will hinder Ms Reeves’s ability to balance the books in the autumn.

Mr Taylor, a member of the Bank’s Monetary Policy Committee (MPC), said it was “too soon to tell” how the conflict could impact the UK economy.

However, he said that over the past 50 years, energy shocks had contributed to large spikes in inflation.

Analysts warned that prolonged disruptions to gas exports from Qatar and the United Arab Emirates risked a “repeat of 2022”, when household bills rocketed.

In a research note, investment bank Stifel said the European gas price at €100 per megawatt hour would be enough to drive the UK’s energy price cap to £2,500 a year. The cap currently stands at £1,758 per year, and is due to fall to £1,641 in April.

European natural gas prices rose to €43 per megawatt hour on Monday, its highest level in a year, but analysts warned continued disruption could push the price even higher.

Analysis by the RAC suggested drivers could expect to pay an average of £1.36 per litre of petrol if oil prices remained close to $80 a barrel, up from an average of £1.33 today. At $100 a barrel, a level analysts think may be reached in the coming weeks, it would cost drivers an extra £9.30 for a full tank of fuel, based on a capacity of 55 litres.

Logistics UK, which represents hauliers across road, rail, sea and air, called on the Chancellor to scrap plans to begin raising fuel duty this year.

Ben Fletcher, the lobby group’s chief executive, said: “The global price of oil is already climbing: this increased cost will soon be seen at the UK’s pumps.”

Sir Mel Stride, the shadow chancellor, said: “The latest developments in the Middle East make it even more disappointing that Labour chose to raise fuel duty at the Budget instead of prioritising motorists and commuters. For every year of the last Conservative government, we found the resources to freeze fuel duty. Labour did not make the same choices and increased spending on benefits instead.”

The Iran conflict risks a dramatic reduction of crude and liquefied natural gas supplies through the vital Strait of Hormuz trade route, which risks a sharp increase in prices.

The Qatari shutdown alone poses a risk to inflation in Britain, which relies on the country for about 2pc of its imports.

Analysts at Capital Economics estimated a sustained rise oil prices could add up to 0.3 percentage points to the headline rate of inflation, which stood at 3pc in January.

Ruth Gregory, at Capital, said: “The rise in natural gas prices could add something similar. So CPI inflation could be 0.5 percentage 0.6 percentage points higher than otherwise.

“Given inflation is currently above target, inflation expectations are higher than before the pandemic, and the public’s inflation expectations tend to respond to changes in energy prices, the Bank of England is likely to be more sensitive to this than other central banks. This could delay or limit the extent of rate cuts this year.”

data.title: Trump tells Starmer: ‘Open up the North Sea. Immediately’
data.description:
Donald Trump has urged Sir Keir Starmer to restart North Sea drilling “immediately” to combat soaring energy prices triggered by the US-led war with Iran.

The US president urged the Prime Minister to ramp up oil and gas production as the price of wholesale gas in Europe surged by another 20pc on Tuesday, on top of a 40pc surge on Monday.

Asked what advice he would give the Prime Minister, Mr Trump told The Sun: “Two very simple things.

“Open up the North Sea. Immediately. Your energy prices are through the roof.

“And stop people from coming in from foreign lands who hate you.

“Open up the North Sea and stop people from pouring into his country from faraway lands.”

Brent crude oil, the international benchmark, rose as much as 3.5pc in the early hours on Tuesday, tipping back above $80 a barrel. Some analysts have warned that prices could hit $100 if the Iran war drags on.

Rising oil and gas prices threaten to fuel inflation, presenting a difficult backdrop for Rachel Reeves as she prepares to deliver her Spring Statement on Tuesday.

President Trump has repeatedly urged the Prime Minister to ramp up oil and gas production, having been elected on a promise to “drill, baby, drill” in the US.

By contrast, Labour has increased the windfall tax on oil and gas company profits to 78pc and extended it to 2030. The Government has also restricted new exploration licences and scrapped investment allowances.

Mr Trump described the North Sea as a “treasure chest for the United Kingdom” last July, while in September his energy secretary said British ministers were “strangling” the country’s oil and gas industry.

The UK’s recent crackdown on its own sources of oil and gas has been accompanied by a steady rise in reliance on other countries for fossil fuels – mainly Norway and the USA.

Britain also relies on Qatar for 2pc of its gas imports. QatarEnergy closed a refinery on Monday after an Iranian strike, fuelling the surge in wholesale prices.

The boss of the UK’s largest petrol station business criticised the Government’s shift away from North Sea production as energy prices soar.

Alasdair Locke, chairman of Motor Fuel Group, said motorists would face higher prices at the pump “in the coming days and weeks” as the impact of the conflict in the Middle East filters through.

“What it does rather highlight is the absurdity of a lot of our current energy policy, particularly with relation to energy security,” Mr Locke told BBC Radio 4’s Today programme.

“The fact that Qatar cannot produce and now export gas – and we import an awful lot of gas from Qatar – why on earth are we shutting down our own production in the North Sea on the alter of net zero?

“We all know that we need oil and gas in this country in the mix for decades to come.”

The North Sea Transition Authority estimates there are 2.9bn barrels of oil equivalent of proven and probably oil and gas reserves in UK waters. A further 6.2bn boe are likely recoverable but not yet commercially developed, while 4.6bn boe are estimated to be in prospects not yet drilled.

data.title: Farmers face spring fuel crisis as prices jump 40pc
data.description:
Farmers are facing a 40pc surge in fuel costs during the crucial spring planting season, threatening to unleash a fresh wave of food price rises.

The cost of red diesel, which powers tractors and other farm machinery, has jumped from roughly 70 pence per litre on Monday to £1 over the past week as the crisis in the Middle East threatens to force the Gulf to halt energy exports within days.

Experts said the increase was roughly double that seen at the start of Russia’s invasion of Ukraine. The jump will add thousands of pounds to farmers’ fuel bills each week and threaten the spring planting season.

Patrick Crehan, the fuel procurement manager at the AF Group, one of Britain’s biggest agricultural wholesalers, said: “When Russia invaded Ukraine, the market went up significantly. It got very, very busy. But this is on a different scale.”

Mr Crehan warned that while there were no signs of the “panic buying” seen in 2022, he said deliveries of red diesel that usually take a few days were now facing delays of “at least a couple of weeks”.

It raises the prospect of some farmers being unable to plant their crops or paying eye-watering sums for fuel.

“Everyone needs to be planting,” he said. “It’s double the increase that we saw last time, and obviously, the implications could be quite severe if it continues.”

Red diesel is a byproduct of oil. It is dyed red to stand out from regular diesel used in cars and vans. The price jump follows a surge in crude oil prices, triggered by the conflict in the Middle East. Brent has risen from $72 (£53.98) a barrel last Friday to just over $90 today.

Mr Crehan said some farmers would start renegotiating contracts with supermarkets if fuel prices continued to surge, paving the way for higher costs on shelves.

“We’ve got some farmers that use 5,000 litres or 10,000 litres per week who are suddenly seeing a 30 pence per litre increase in prices. And it’s quite worrying for them, especially when they may not have budgeted for it.”

The National Farmers’ Union (NFU) warned that price rises could further undermine farm profitability at a time when many are already struggling. The sector is also feeling beleaguered after Rachel Reeves imposed death taxes.

Jimmy Goodley, an arable farmer in Norfolk, said prices had become so volatile that wholesalers could not offer a price until the day of delivery, creating huge financial uncertainty.

He said: “The fuel that you order is priced on the day it’s delivered. So you have no idea what that price will be. As anyone who’s ordered heating oil in the last 72 hours will tell you, these price hikes are very, very significant.”

The Gulf is home to some of the world’s largest fertiliser factory sites, with a prolonged shutdown threatening to disrupt production and raise costs.

Tom Bradshaw, the president of the NFU, said: “The escalating conflict in the Middle East is of concern, as any persistent impacts to global energy markets will have a knock-on effect on the future availability and price of fuel and fertiliser.

“We are seeing immediate price volatility, but at this stage, it is too early to say how the UK may be impacted in the medium term.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: British factories warned of chaos under Brussels’ ‘made in Europe’ plan
data.description:
British factories risk being cut out of EU supply chains under Brussels’ “made in Europe” plan, the British Chambers of Commerce (BCC) has warned.

The European Union is pushing for new quotas on the use of domestic components in products such as cars, which would end the bloc’s traditional reliance on China and the US.

However, the new legislation risks damaging British business caught up in the “chaos”, the BCC warned.

William Bain, the head of trade policy at the BCC, said the most extreme version of the bloc’s “made in Europe” policy could trigger “a chilling effect on cross-border trade and cause chaos for EU-based producers reliant on UK parts and components”.

The lobby group called on the Government to urgently negotiate an exemption for British companies.

Peter Kyle, the Business Secretary, urged officials in Brussels to classify British companies as European under the new rules.

Mr Bain emphasised that the stakes were very high.

He said: “The UK Government is very much alive to this danger and dialogue is ongoing but this issue is not yet on the radar of enough firms and its seriousness cannot be underestimated.”

Measures being considered by Brussels include content rules that will require up to 70pc of the components in the goods firms make to be of EU origin.

The bloc is also weighing up new rules on sustainability, capping foreign direct investment and red tape governing procurement.

The UK’s position as a third country leaves British companies at risk of being squeezed out of supply chains, with car manufacturing particularly exposed. Brussels has already begun to set out legislation to increase subsidies for EU-made vehicles.

Patrick Keating, the head of government affairs at Honda Motor Europe, said the car manufacturer was “concerned about the strong emphasis on ‘made in the EU’ requirements in the Industrial Accelerator Act”.

He added that the bloc’s proposed legislation risked “limiting global supply chain resilience and reducing consumer choice during the transition to sustainable mobility”.

Brussels’s “made in Europe” agenda also threatens to derail Sir Keir Starmer’s attempts to secure an EU reset.

In May 2025, the Prime Minister announced his plans to bring the UK closer to the EU. Britain has since signed new agreements on animal and plant health, electricity and carbon pricing.

Mr Kyle visited Brussels last week to unveil a new agreement with the European Commission to work more closely on competition law.

He said the UK was not asking for “free access” to the EU’s markets by requesting to be considered part of the bloc’s “made in Europe” scheme.

“This is the moment at which we try to focus on areas of new collaborations, not trying to restrict the ones that we already have,” he said.

A government spokesman said: “The UK is committed to playing its part as a close and trusted European partner and we are of course engaging with the EU on ‘made in Europe’.

“Now is the time for us to work together – as like-minded partners – to boost growth and open up trade.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: WH Smith’s rebrand has to work – our high streets need it
data.description:
The rebrand of WH Smith’s high street stores to TG Jones has been met with a mixture of ridicule and confusion, and perhaps rightly so.

One of the most recognisable names in British retail has been replaced by something that is essentially meaningless – hardly a surprise given that it was dreamt up by Modella Capital, the investment firm that snapped up the shops for a song last June.

In the interest of fairness, it is worth pointing out that the rebrand was forced upon Modella – a fact that most shoppers appear to be unaware of.

Under the terms of the deal, it wasn’t allowed to keep the WH Smith name because it is still being used above hundreds of shops at airports, train stations and hospitals that the company held on to.

Yet that doesn’t change the fact that the TG Jones moniker has fallen flat with the great British public. With shoppers increasingly staying away from the stores, it is hardly a shock that Modella is already drawing up drastic cost-saving measures – a move that is expected to lead to a sizable number of its shops being closed for good.

Judging by the feedback from Telegraph readers to the news, few people will mourn the chain’s fate. I expressed similar feelings when WH Smith announced it was getting out of the high street last year.

A visit to a WH Smith store is about the most soulless shopping experience that exists today, I said. Yet with hindsight, such sentiment may be a classic case of “be careful what you wish for”.

It no doubt sounds far-fetched, given the desperate state of some TG Jones stores, but the future of the high street could depend on their survival.

For decades, WH Smith was regarded as a high street “anchor tenant”: a household retailer that helps tether other, smaller businesses to town centres, because they drive a disproportionate amount of footfall to the area.

When these big names pull out from somewhere, a domino effect often occurs, prompting others to scramble for the exit.

The likes of Woolworths, Debenhams and BHS once fell into this category, but they all vanished from our shopping centres years ago. Others like Marks & Spencer are replacing many older city centre stores with a smaller number of jazzed-up, larger outlets, often in out-of-town retail parks. John Lewis, meanwhile, has pulled out of some places altogether with the closure of around a third of its department stores during the pandemic.

This endless retrenchment goes a long way to explaining why so many towns now feel close to deserted save for the usual sea of Turkish barbers, nail bars and horrendous vape shops.

Of the traditional “anchor” tenants, only Boots and the Post Office remain. Incredibly, despite multiple rounds of closures, the former still has 1,800 stores, meaning it is a fixture on practically every major UK high street.

But it also has pharmacy counters, opticians and increasingly acts as a de facto local GP by providing doctor consultations, vaccinations and blood pressure checks. They are the sort of services that should ensure people continue to walk through its doors in meaningful numbers, and by extension, onto other nearby businesses.

The Post Office is arguably an even more essential feature of local communities, especially given that the big banks continue to shamefully abandon their customers through never-ending rounds of branch closures.

Bound by legislation that requires it to maintain 11,500 outlets, the Post Office also provides essential services – such as banking, mail and bill payments – that attract a steady stream of visitors, who then often go on to spend money elsewhere.

The Post Office says that for every two in five visits to a post office, money is spent elsewhere in neighbouring shops and premises.

If – and I appreciate it’s a big if – TG Jones can regain anchor chain status, it could prove to be a desperately-needed lifeline for many hollowed out town centres.

Modella says it can be done, or at least that was its position upon taking over close to 500 WH Smith stores last June. Chairman Steve Curtis told me his mission was to emulate Boots and become “a vanguard retailer” that is part of the “lifeblood” of communities.

Good luck with that, many will say. Amid a torrent of scepticism, Modella was quick to defend the new TG Jones name. “As a very well-known surname in the UK, Jones feels like a worthy successor to Smith and carries the same sense of family,” it said – somewhat hopefully perhaps – upon taking over.

It has failed to win customers over. Privately, Modella executives concede that the firm underestimated the impact of being unable to retain the WH Smith name, along with years of underinvestment by its former parent.

Some accuse Modella of naivety, even recklessness. Its harshest critics wonder whether its true intention is to run down the business as part of some secret yet highly cynical asset-stripping exercise.

Yet that would be quite a gamble for a firm that recently presided over the insolvency of two other high street chains: The Original Factory Shop and Claire’s Accessories.

Bouncing back from a third collapse, particularly one involving a business that has been a fixture of the high street since George III was on the throne, would be immensely challenging.

Modella insists it approaches all investments honourably, yet the Chancellor’s tax raids have dealt a serious blow to its chances of success at TG Jones.

A lifeline could come from a proposed tie-up that puts Post Office counters and banking services in every TG Jones shop that survives the forthcoming store review.

That sounds like a more compelling proposition than yet another phone shop, kebab house, or dog grooming service. Modella is hopeful it can persuade Post Office executives to agree to the plans, and I understand a major bank is on standby too.

The future of many stores, however, is likely to rest on landlords agreeing to cheaper rents. There will be resistance but the alternative is much worse for them and all of us: another wave of empty shops, many destined to remain derelict for years.

Some experts believe the state of the high street provides an opportunity to move away from retail and reinvent our town centres.

Perhaps, but I don’t think people are even close to being at the optimistic stage yet. Many are rightly angry about the demise of their local communities and concerned about a proliferation of cash-only businesses, some of which are suspected of being fronts for organised crime.

Labour would do well to take the issue more seriously. Research has shown that support for Reform is higher in places with the most acute high street decline. It is no coincidence that Nigel Farage has taken a sudden interest in the legality or otherwise of Turkish barber shops.
